-- 88 of 97 --

Chapter 3 • Ethical and Legal Considerations in Crisis Counseling 	89
Dr. Mary Bartlett
It is no small matter to break confidentiality, so it
is important not to do so without first consulting
another counseling professional regardless of how
long you have been practicing. Two important things
to remember are to avoid practicing in isolation,
especially when working with a client who is having
suicidal thoughts, and to document the conversation
you have with another professional as input received
in consultation about the case. This helps to ensure
you are following best-practice guidelines and have
considered all angles before breaking an important
trust with your client even if ethically obligated to
do so.
VOICES FROM THE FIELD 3.6
Confidentiality and the Suicidal Client
Think about potential breaches of confidentiality. Read
the ACA legal case Alleged Breach of Confidentiality
found at www.hpso.com/risk-education/individuals/
legal-case-study/Alleged-Breach-of- Confidentiality.
After reviewing this case, discuss the following
questions: What would you have done differently?
How would supervision have helped? How would peer
consultation have assisted this counselor? When would
a conversation with an attorney have been appropriate?
THINK ABOUT IT 3.4
Erin Shifflett
When providing counseling services in a disaster mental
health capacity, you may find yourself facing a variety
of ethical challenges. One issue to consider is the fea-
sibility of ensuring confidentiality in potentially chaotic
situations. Bear in mind that many first responders with
whom you will be working may request access to infor-
mation to maintain the welfare of those being helped
collaboratively. Approach these requests from a treat-
ment team perspective to ensure clients feel informed
and empowered at a potent

title: readings CRISIS reading 1 (Part 32/34)
content:
ntially disempowering and
confusing time in their lives.
VOICES FROM THE FIELD 3.7
Confidentiality During Chaotic Circumstances in the Field
Termination in Crisis Intervention
Because crisis intervention is generally brief and focused on immediate needs, crisis
counselors must use appropriate termination skills with clients. Appropriate termina-
tion should not only bring closure for the crisis counseling relationship but also bolster
the goals and plans laid out in the crisis counseling process. A distinction should be
made between termination and abandonment. Appropriate termination may decrease
the allegations of perceived abandonment by a client.
Termination occurs when a counselor or a client decides to end the counseling
relationship. According to section A.11.c of the ACA Code of Ethics (2014), professional
counselors terminate cases when the treatment goals have been reached and the client
no longer needs counseling, when the client is not being helped by further counseling
or might be harmed by further counseling, when the client or someone associated with
the client poses harm to the counselor, or when the client does not pay counseling fees.
M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 89	M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 89 	06/04/23 7:38 PM	06/04/23 7:38 PM

-- 89 of 97 --

90 	Part I • Elements of Crisis Intervention
In those circumstances where clients need further counseling services after termination,
the counselor provides the client with referral information for other appropriate mental
health providers. Unfortunately, lack of attention to termination documentation can
lead to lawsuits based on abandonment (Corey et al., 2019). A final termination sum-
mary should be added to the client file. Bond and Mitchels (2014) recommend that this
summary include the reason for termination, a summary of progress, the final diagnostic
impression, a follow-up plan, and other pertinent information. Now, complete Think
About It 3.5.
Think about termination documentation. How would
the termination summary for a client in a disaster
response setting differ from the termination sum-
mary for a student graduating from a university? How
would the termination summary for a client in a disaster
response setting differ from the termination note for a
client discharged from a clinical mental health center?
When a client has a suicide crisis and does not return
to the clinical mental health center following hospi-
tal discharge, how would the final client contact be
documented?
THINK ABOUT IT 3.5
When a client is neglected, deserted, or negligently terminated, the counselor has
committed abandonment of the client. The ACA Code of Ethics (2014) prohibits abandon-
ment; section A.12 states, “Counselors do not abandon or neglect clients in counseling.
Counselors assist in making appropriate arrangements for the continuation of treatment,
when necessary, during interruptions such as vacations, illness, and following termi-
nation.” Special considerations for termination at university counseling clinics would
include times when a student drops out of school, leaves school for medical purposes, is
placed on academic probation or conduct suspension, or graduates from the institution.
A counselor must think about myriad factors when considering or faced with ter-
minating a therapeutic relationship. Counselors should discuss the process of termina-
tion with the client during the first appointment and have clinical documentation of this
discussion. While a discussion about termination at the outset of the therapeutic relation-
ship does not prevent unforeseen circumstances for termination, it better prepares the
client and goes far in the preservation of the counseling relationship. The following list
highlights a few of the many circumstances that increase a counselor’s risk of malpractice
due to abandonment:
• The counselor is no longer receiving compensation from the client for professional
services.
• The counselor recognizes that a referral might be in order.
• Therapeutic goals are no longer being achieved.
• The counselor is abruptly unavailable.
• The counselor fails to follow up after a client’s hospital discharge.
• The counselor does not respond to a client’s request for crisis treatment.
• The counselor allows managed care to dictate termination.
• The counselor lacks clinical coverage during planned absences (i.e., vacation time).
• Records do not verify outreach efforts to a client who breaks or misses appointments.
M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 90	M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 90 	06/04/23 7:38 PM	06/04/23 7:38 PM

-- 90 of 97 --

Chapter 3 • Ethical and Legal Considerations in Crisis Counseling 	91
• A high-risk client drops out of treatment.
• A client is “fired” or refused treatment.
• A client is not notified in writing that a case is being closed.
• The client’s record indicates a failure to review/consult/refer.
• Another treatment team member or staff member’s notes do not verify that a treat-
ment plan is being followed.
For further information on client abandonment, see ACA (2014), Corey et al. (2019), and
Remley and Herlihy (2020).
Corey et al. (2019) recommend that clients be given written information about the
closure plan, including contact information for other counselors or resources. If a cli-
ent misses appointments and stops coming to counseling, the counselor should send a
follow-up letter expressing interest in the client’s welfare and asking the client to contact
the counselor by a specified date if the client wants to resume counseling. If a termination
letter is to be sent to the home of the client, written consent to send any information to
that address should have been obtained during the informed-consent process. Similarly,
written consent to leave any information on a client’s phone should have been obtained
during intake. The written consent should state the specific address or phone number for
which the client has granted permission. The counselor should document all attempts
to contact a client. Case Study 3.8 focuses on the factors a counselor must take into con-
sideration when terminating a therapeutic relationship.
CASE STUDY 3.8
Termination Considerations
Alex lost a home and two family members in a series of tornadoes that recently swept
across the state. Alex began to experience symptoms of acute trauma and approached
you for care as a disaster relief counselor. You began seeing Alex regularly since arriv-
ing at the disaster site. Alex has engaged well therapeutically and appears to be making
progress. You were notified by the Red Cross that your term of service will be up in a
week and need to terminate with each of your assigned cases at the location. When you
mention discontinuing services, Alex becomes distressed.
Discussion Questions
1. What obligations do you have to Alex?
2. How will you ensure Alex’s continued recovery?
3. To what resources can you refer Alex?
4. Which ACA ethical standard(s) is(are) applicable in this case?
As mentioned previously, termination in a mass crisis or disaster circumstance
should be understood from a special perspective. Formal assessment, admission, and
ongoing counseling normally are not possible under such circumstances. According
to the American Red Cross (2022), ongoing care and provision of counseling services
M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 91	M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 91 	06/04/23 7:38 PM	06/04/23 7:38 PM

-- 91 of 97 --

92 	Part I • Elements of Crisis Intervention
should be referred to local counseling services, case managers, and area providers
to avoid therapeutic abandonment. An example of how such a conversation would
occur follows:
C"#$%&'"(: 	I’m glad we had the chance to work together these past few
weeks since your home was destroyed. I understand this has
been a very surreal and overwhelming time for you.
C')&$*: 	I don’t know what I would have done without you.
C"#$%&'"(: 	Well, actually you did a lot of the work to move forward
under difficult circumstances and have a lot to feel proud of.
You have devised a plan of recovery for yourself, are in the
process of securing more permanent housing, and have been
able to return to work with transportation assistance. All of
this means our work together has reached the right conclusion.
At this time, I’d like to suggest community resources you may
access for longer-term recovery support.
The severity of responses to mass crisis may have long-lasting effects for some.
Psychological distress may remain high long after crisis counseling support services
have been provided to those affected. This begs the question: What ethical responsibility
do counselors have to ensure a healthy transition from crisis counseling to appropriate
community-based treatment services once the additional support has been removed?
There is some uncertainty about this limit of responsibility; therefore, many counseling
training programs include crisis intervention coursework. For those who are already
in practice, it is recommended they seek some training in crisis response counseling to
explore whether they want to work with crisis populations or how they will work with
a person who has survived a mass disaster and is referred to them for support (Norris
& Rosen, 2009).
Spiritual and Multicultural Considerations
To dismiss discussions surrounding a client’s religious or spiritual views related to a
crisis is to devalue the client’s worldview and not treat the entire person. Often, clients
may entertain ideas that tragedy has come about because of God’s wrath or disfavor.
Counselors should be prepared to safely explore these concerns. This requires that coun-
selors examine their own religious or spiritual biases and understand that many survi-
vors have symptoms of secondary trauma of a spiritual nature (Zalaquett et al., 2009).
Disaster workers might hear clients question whether their sin had brought the disaster
upon them. Furthermore, d

title: readings CRISIS reading 1 (Part 33/34)
content:
isaster counselors may have to contend with remarks from
volunteers working the crisis who may express such concepts of judgment to clients
(Hard, 2020).
If the disaster occurs in a school system where the common separation of church
and state is stressed, counselors must take that into consideration. Keep in mind that
according to the U.S. Supreme Court, the First Amendment as applied to public schools
was intended for public schools to not sanction or support a specific religion or faith over
others. Counselors must find a balance between honoring the guidelines of the state and
recognizing (and honoring) the needs of clients, regardless of where the tragedy took
place (Remley & Herlihy, 2019).
M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 92	M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 92 	06/04/23 7:38 PM	06/04/23 7:38 PM

-- 92 of 97 --

Chapter 3 • Ethical and Legal Considerations in Crisis Counseling 	93
In the case of a counselor responding to a person’s life crisis, perhaps related to
suicide, an examination of personal religious beliefs is essential to ensure maintaining a
value-neutral assessment. Failure to take a nonjudgmental stance may result in further
risk to the client; by assuming that the client has the same moral or religious beliefs,
counselors may foster shame and anger in the client who then may withhold pertinent
information needed to assess the level of lethality (Granello & Granello, 2007). Even
secular counselors should exercise sufficient latitude to explore these questions with
clients; doing less results in a failure to meet the needs of the client.
Research indicates that marginalized populations may find that predisaster norms
set the tone for the postdisaster reality. Therefore, contrary to popular belief, disaster is
not a social leveler, and underrepresented groups may find that their circumstances have
declined (Hard, 2020). Persons in the sexual, affectional, intersex, and gender-expansive
communities (lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, questioning [LGBTQ+]) may become
further marginalized following a disaster. As Hard (2020) observed, while national ser-
vice organizations (e.g., American Red Cross) may have LGBTQ+–affirming policies,
individual volunteers may be from less accepting backgrounds, thus requiring coun-
selors to act as diversity advocates on behalf of LGBTQ+ victims. Therefore, counselors
should be versed in multicultural sensitivity and make efforts to indicate their sensitivity
to multicultural issues.
The counselor’s respect for cultural diversity should not vary according to setting,
whether in a school, private practice, or university setting, which is often a multicultural
microcosm. For example, Asian culture has a focus on time orientation that is either
past or present (e.g., valuing ancestral wisdom and present experience; Hays & Erford,
2023). In contrast, Native American culture tends to be present oriented (i.e., focusing
on the present experience; thinking about the present) when exposed to traumatic cir-
cumstances. These cultural variances require that counselors be aware of how time ori-
entation and similar factors influence the crisis response of clients in order to accurately
assess current mental status and needs (Remley & Herlihy, 2020). At the same time, it is
important to note that the above observations are trends that do not necessarily apply
to all persons within these cultures. Therefore, we should not place all persons from a
perceived culture into the same template but assess individuals in accordance with their
unique cultural perception. Understanding differences is important but making deci-
sions based on assumptions may not be helpful.
In addition, some cultures are more socially focused rather than individually
focused. Responses from people of these cultures will be directed toward what is most
beneficial to the community’s best interest. People from different cultures will vary in
terms of eye contact, body language, affect, willingness to discuss personal matters, and
in their response to authority figures. Ethical practice requires the development of mul-
ticultural competence among counselors; therefore, counselors are responsible for being
well versed in multicultural competencies when serving others (ACA, 2014; Remley &
Herlihy, 2020).
Crisis Intervention in a Technological World
Technology influences crisis intervention. In an age where many adolescents and adults
seem always to have smartphones in hand, crises are reported immediately to authorities
and help is often obtained quickly and in some instances in real time (e.g., via Skype or
Zoom). Crisis intervention information and resources can be accessed quickly on one’s
M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 93	M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 93 	06/04/23 7:38 PM	06/04/23 7:38 PM

-- 93 of 97 --

94 	Part I • Elements of Crisis Intervention
smartphone. Social media such as Facebook and Twitter provide opportunities to share
experiences of a crisis in real time. It is also obvious that social media holds the possi-
bility for quickly spreading misinformation. So, counselors must be aware of accurate
information in real time to help clients navigate anxieties caused by false information.
Additionally, a client in crisis can have easier access to a counselor or other mental health
providers via text messages, e-mail, and other modes of electronic messaging. Impor-
tantly, evidence supports the effectiveness of tele-mental health for addressing depres-
sion, anxiety (Rees & Maclaine, 2015), and posttraumatic stress disorder (Turgoose et
al., 2018). Videoconferencing, online forums (Kaur et al., 2016), smartphone applications
(Kerst et al., 2020), text messages, and e-mails (Torniainen-Holm et al., 2016) are useful
communication methods for mental health services.
Furber et al. (2014) found that both text messaging and phone-based psychotherapy
were beneficial resources for clients in crisis. According to Almond and Budden (2012),
text messaging is seen as a positive tool during hostage negotiation, suicide prevention,
and general crisis intervention. Sude (2013) found that text messaging used in mental
health practice increases the responsiveness of counselors to clients, allows clients to be
more candid in their communication, is helpful to clients who prefer this method of com-
munication, and may create a more connected feeling between client and counselor. The
limitations of texting in practice include ethical concerns such as boundary issues, main-
taining confidentiality, and potential for a diminished therapeutic relationship because
the client and counselor are not face to face.
Technology also has influenced online crisis counseling services. Gary (2010)
reported that online crisis counseling services are a valuable resource for clients in crisis
who are geographically isolated, self-sequestered, or have limited access to face-to-face
counseling (e.g., transportation problems, work release time). In a meta-analysis of the
literature regarding online counseling, Richards and Vigano (2013) found positive client
perceptions of online counseling with benefits in the areas of client anonymity, conve-
nience, and client disinhibition. Conversely, although the digital domain may enhance
speed and access to services, danger of the swift spread of disaster-related rumors and
disinformation and exposure to those who prey on the vulnerable remain problematic.
Counselors must stay attentive to new technological tools that can help them support
clients in times of crisis and promote counselor and client safety.
Miller (2020) expands on the caveats of digital consultations versus face-to-face ses-
sions. The virtual sessions may be more beneficial in certain circumstances when there
is a danger that those who already have reduced access to health care (e.g., materially
disadvantaged, less able to navigate the changes to health care delivery, and less digitally
fluent). However, the digital divide also may increase polarization in mental health care.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, tele-mental health services were practical, time-
and cost-effective, and appropriate for the support of clients, family members, and
counselors (Smith et al., 2020), when increased mental health symptoms were observed
among diverse populations. In particular, tele-mental health allowed isolated and remote
clients access to services that were otherwise unavailable (Xiaoyun & Fenglan, 2020).
Furthermore, expanding telehealth to include mental health reduced interpersonal con-
tact and the spread of COVID-19 by reducing risk exposure of noninfected individuals
in waiting room areas.
M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 94	M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 94 	06/04/23 7:38 PM	06/04/23 7:38 PM

-- 94 of 97 --

Chapter 3 • Ethical and Legal Considerations in Crisis Counseling 	95
However, the use of technology during the pandemic was not all positive. Pro-
viders assumed that clients had Internet and computer access, could relate to the new
method of care delivery given the technology-mediated relationship, and that the con-
venience and cost-effectiveness of providers working remotely would be beneficial,
without determining the holistic cost to the patient. While the social isolation brought
on by the pandemic may have reduced the viral spread, it also led to reduced access
to family, friends, and other support systems and created feelings of loneliness, anxi-
ety, and depression. Unmanaged mental health symptoms may have lengthened the
health effects on patients, adding to the potential cost burden of managing the disease.
Therefore, protocols for those with COVID-19 should address both the physiological and
mental health needs of clients.
Now read Voices from the Field 3.8 for further insights into the use of telehealth
counseling during the pandemic. Finally, Table 3.1 provides additional resources for
further study and training.
Emily Tucker, LPC, NCC
How did the pand

title: readings CRISIS reading 1 (Part 34/34)
content:
emic change the way you prac-
tice and what were your initial concerns? Initially
the greatest concern was how to navigate scheduled
appointments while also honoring the safety con-
cerns and mandated business closures. Fortunately,
our administrative staff was responsive and assisted
in communicating ongoing changes to clients and to
each therapist. During the first month or two of the
pandemic, appointments were handled over tele-
phone or telehealth platforms such as HIPPA video,
TheraPlatform, Zoom, or SimplePractice. These are just
a few examples of the hundreds of telehealth platforms
available today.
What was the most challenging administra-
tive issue you encountered when moving from
face-to-face versus telehealth? Initially, finding
and evaluating telehealth platforms quickly while also
coordinating schedule changes, new client intake, doc-
umentation, and other daily responsibilities felt over-
whelming. The complete shutdown occurred within a
few days, so there was a very limited time frame in
which these important decisions had to happen. My
counseling practice had to adapt and had to do so prac-
tically overnight. Peer resources, networking groups,
and national and state agencies provided some clarity
and guidance regarding these changes and the neces-
sary order of importance. Personal health and safety
from possible exposure to a novel virus was paramount,
followed by maintaining continuity of care and con-
nection with clients as a nearly equal second. This pri-
oritization allowed some grace and time for providers
to explore and transition to the best telehealth plat-
form or method of providing telehealth services. Some
providers, due to necessity in the client population or
due to service type (e.g., working with children and
families or those in crisis), were able to maintain an in-
person practice with very specific prevention protocols
in place. As time progressed, guidance increased for
those meeting in person. Consent forms for in-person
COVID-19 risk waivers were provided as well as similar
informed consent documents for telehealth. In sum-
mary, action came first with a priority on continuity of
care and maintaining client contact followed by various
options to notate, document, and address liabilities and
concerns of clients’ insurance companies and providers.
What are the greatest liabilities and ethical
concerns you faced when crisis counseling? That is
an in-depth question because as a former crisis phone
counselor I feel there are many layers to this answer.
VOICES FROM THE FIELD 3.8
Telehealth Counseling and the COVID-19 Pandemic
(continued)
M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 95	M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 95 	06/04/23 7:38 PM	06/04/23 7:38 PM

-- 95 of 97 --

96 	Part I • Elements of Crisis Intervention
In that setting (i.e., crisis telephone hotline) I focused
on responding to clients in crisis where they were in
that moment. Connecting, communicating support,
reflecting, exploring, and assessing for suicidal ide-
ation were critical. This included discussing how and
when the caller intended to act. This suicide assessment
process required a delicate balance of gentle directive
questions, reflective listening, and unconditional posi-
tive regard. Ideally, this balance honored the feelings
of the client while helping me internally navigate my
own reactions and fears for an actively suicidal client.
Concerns for liability were coupled with our roles
as listener and holder of a safe place for the client to
process their thoughts and feelings. This discussion
typically included complicated and often polarizing
feelings related to an inner experience of despair,
helplessness, and isolation coupled with an urgency for
relief from the intense emotional pain they are experi-
encing. As the listener and support person for someone
in this much pain, a therapist or call center volunteer
must maintain some inner sense of calm, curiosity, and
connection with the client. Thus, self-care and self-
awareness are critical to best serve our clients.
Liabilities also include documentation of location
(including address), confirmation of privacy of the cli-
ent, and review of consent and exceptions to confi-
dentiality. As therapists providing telehealth services,
we are required to first clarify to our clients’ reasons
we as counselors would have to break confidential-
ity while also seeking to navigate creating a sense of
trust and safety. Building rapport with each client is
essential. This is no different from face-to-face settings.
The additional piece when working with someone via
a telehealth platform is that we must confirm the
location, address, and contact information of our cli-
ent at the onset of every contact in case information
is disclosed that requires immediate intervention (i.e.,
an actively suicidal client discloses immediate intent,
means available, and intention to harm self or others
during a call). Without ascertaining the address and
location as well as an emergency contact, we are far
less able to ensure an outcome that protects the client
and honors the therapist’s ethical and legal obligations.
Essentially, it is difficult to send help to someone when
we do not have their location.
Additional concerns surrounding liability included
clients traveling or returning home, especially at the
onset of the pandemic. In early 2020, each state
required a licensure process unique to that state. A
few states had mutually reciprocal agreements so that
counselors could practice in either state, but this was
not the case in my home state. Fortunately, the emer-
gency guidelines put in place at the federal level and
in many (but not all) states were generous about relax-
ing interstate practices and distance-counseling restric-
tions. Organizations like Person Centered Tech and IFS
Telehealth Collective helped organize the overwhelming
amount of legal, administrative, and ethics data as a
resource for therapists struggling to continue serving
their out-of-state clients while also complying with all
laws and ethical considerations. All in all, the transition
to telehealth had moments of discomfort and anxiety, as
well as opportunities to develop and grow as a therapist
and a human being. I have embraced the transition to
telehealth and am grateful for this opportunity to con-
tinue connecting throughout a very challenging time.
M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 96	M03_Jackson_4_78918.indd 96 	06/04/23 7:38 PM	06/04/23 7:38 PM

-- 96 of 97 --

Chapter 3 • Ethical and Legal Considerations in Crisis Counseling 	97
SUMMARY
Legal and ethical matters can be confounding for
counselors in the best of circumstances. In times
of crisis, it is even more imperative that coun-
selors be well acquainted with legal and ethical
obligations. Crisis counselors may often find
their normal coping mechanisms overwhelmed
during disaster or crisis interventions. When the
counselor’s normal coping is impaired, lapses in
professional judgment may occur. The preven-
tion of ethical and legal entanglements is far
preferable to remediation of problems following
a lapse in behavior or judgment. This chapter
discusses preventive considerations for crisis
counselors, including wellness recommenda-
tions, continuing education, supervision and
peer consultation, and ethical decision-making
models.
HIPAA was enacted to address many per-
ceived failings in the management of information
in the health care industry. FERPA was enacted
to address concerns about privacy violations and
the inclusion of immaterial comments and per-
sonal opinions in student educational records.
HIPAA and FERPA have not only added layers
of protection to client records but also increased
the complexity of counselor responsibility in
protecting both the client’s and their own inter-
ests. HIPAA and FERPA have provisions for
crisis situations, but confidentiality is still cen-
tral to ethical practice, even during times of cri-
sis. Crisis counselors must be familiar with the
particulars of HIPAA and FERPA, including
when they apply and overlap, as well as other
legal requirements in their state. Furthermore,

title: readings Crisis Class 3 reading (Part 1/29)
content:
163
PREVIEW
Suicide and homicide are viewed as preventable global public health problems. Suicides
and homicides have a mental health impact on family members, friends, coworkers,
neighbors, and members of extended social networks, and even the professional
community struggles with the implications of these preventable deaths. Nonsuicidal
self-injury (NSSI) has always been a concern, but only recently has it received a similar
clinical focus and formal assessment to suicide and homicide. Even after inclusion in
the DSM-5-TR, assessment of NSSI occurs less frequently or is more targeted in certain
populations, like adolescents or persons diagnosed with borderline personality disorder.
However, an NSSI assessment should also be part of all suicide risk assessments and is
now required as a standard of care. Whether intervening before suicide, NSSI, or homicide
(prevention), while occurring (intervention), or after it has occurred (postvention),
professional counselors play a vital role in society’s response to helping those in need of
violence-free coping strategies, intervention based on sound practices, or, if necessary,
the best and most productive short-term and long-term response to survivors of suicide
or homicide. This chapter is explicitly for crisis counselors to learn how to be effective in
responding to members of their communities touched by suicide, NSSI, and homicide.
LEARNING OBJECTIVES
Upon completion of this chapter, readers will be able to:
6.1 Describe the prevalence and rates of suicide, homicide, and NSSI.
6.2 Describe risk factors, protective factors, and warning signs of suicide.
6.3 Distinguish between acute and chronic suicidal risk.
6.4 Describe how counselors assess a client’s suicidal risk as no, low, moderate, or high.
6.5 Describe effective questions and assessment strategies for suicide intervention.
6.6 Describe appropriate treatment planning and care management with a suicidal
client, including a safety plan, outpatient and inpatient treatment, and postvention.
6.7 Describe assessments and treatment for NSSI, including the use of a Q-tree.
6.8 Describe threat assessment and treatment options for intervention with people
who are homicidal or are threats to others.
6 Risk Assessment and Intervention:
Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury,
and Homicide
Judith Harrington, Lisa R. Jackson-Cherry, and Bradley T. Erford*
C H A P T E R
*We thank Charlotte Daughhetee for her contributions to previous editions of this chapter.
M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 163	M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 163 	18/04/23 4:55 PM	18/04/23 4:55 PM

-- 1 of 87 --

164 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
PREVALENCE AND RATES OF SUICIDE, HOMICIDE, AND
NONSUICIDAL SELF-INJURY
Learning Objective 6.1 Describe the prevalence and rates of suicide, homicide,
and NSSI.
Clinicians and epidemiologists alike watch closely the rates and prevalence of suicide
and homicide to monitor the presence of conditions that may increase risk factors for
certain populations. (Nonsuicidal self-injury historically was not included in these
prevalence reports and more research needs to be conducted.) Counselors should know
what the terms prevalence and rate mean to interpret how members of certain at-risk
groups might require more attentive care:
• Prevalence is a nominal figure that states how many lives were lost to suicide (or
any other type of malady). For example, the prevalence of suicide in County RST
was 75 persons, contrasted with County XYZ, which lost 33 persons.
• Rate or crude rate is a value explaining data that has been interpreted by leveling
the playing field, so to speak; in other words, if all members of a population (or
an age group, gender, or ethnic group) were divided into groups of 100,000, the
rate would indicate how many people died per 100,000 people (sometimes called
per capita). For example, County RST, a very populated county, lost 75 persons to
suicide or homicide, but its rate was 10.9 per 100,000, or 10.9. County XYZ, a small,
sparsely populated county, lost 16 persons to suicide or homicide, but its rate was
19.7 per 100,000, or 19.7. Due to the common denominator of 100,000, the higher
rate for County XYZ is considered more alarming, even though the prevalence of
suicide was smaller in the rural county.
Epidemiologists also study the causes of death and the rankings of these causes, and
they track the rates of suicide and homicide, as rates provide a more uniform view for
analysis to which prevention efforts may be targeted. For example, the suicide rate for
Americans age 65 years and older is approximately 18 in 100,000, but the prevalence
is lower than in other groups because the population number (or size of this group)
of older persons is smaller. A rate of 18 in 100,000 is considered alarming because it is
higher than the national overall rate. For suicide, the national rate is 14.5 per 100,000.
Professionals want to learn why persons in each group are more prone to die by suicide
or homicide. Youth have a lower rate of death than older Americans; however, suicide!is
ranked as the second leading cause of death ranking among youth, and homicide is the
sixth and third leading cause of death among 0 to 14-year-olds and 15 to 24-year-olds,
respectively (Drapeau & McIntosh, 2020; World Life Expectancy, 2020). Cause of death
ranking is often illustrated in chart form listing the 10 highest prevalence of deaths per
cause (e.g., heart disease, malignancies, respiratory disease, liver disease, unintentional
injury).
Drapeau and McIntosh (2020) reported an estimated 1,124,125 annual suicide
attempts in the United States or 25 suicide attempts for every suicide. Cerel et al. (2014)
estimated that for every suicide, 147 individuals (survivors) are connected to the person
who died by suicide (6.6 million annually). When examining the vast effects of those
affected by suicides, mental health professionals must be prepared to serve the high
numbers of survivors.
M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 164	M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 164 	18/04/23 4:55 PM	18/04/23 4:55 PM

-- 2 of 87 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	165
Suicide is a public health problem that all crisis counselors, regardless of setting,
must learn to assess and treat because of its prevalence in society. In the United States
in 2020, suicide claimed 45,979 lives, with about one person dying from suicide every
11.1 minutes (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention [CDC], 2022a). Consider these
data:
• In 2020 in the United States, 12.2 million adults experienced thoughts of suicide
(CDC, 2022a).
• In 2020 in the United States, 3.2 million people planned to attempt suicide (CDC,
2022a).
• In 2020 in the United States, 1.2 million persons attempted and survived a suicide
(CDC, 2022a).
• Though suicide happens in all age groups and is the 10th leading cause of death
across all age brackets, it is the ninth leading cause of death in the United States
among persons age 10–64, and the second leading cause of death for youth age
10–14 and adults age 25–34 (CDC, 2022a).
• Men die from suicide at a ratio of 3.6 for every 1 loss of life to suicide among women
(American Association of Suicidology [AAS], 2021).
• Women attempt suicide more often than men, at a ratio of 3:1; men die more often
than women, mainly because of more lethal means (AAS, 2021).
• The impact of suicide loss has staggering personal and economic impact on
individuals and communities. The CDC (2022a) estimated an annual cost of
$70!billion in the United States from lifetime medical and work-related expenses
due to suicide attempts and suicide deaths.
• AAS (2021) estimated that 135 individuals are exposed to or affected by each suicide
death, which is 6.9 million persons annually in the United States.
• The rates of suicide per 100,000 persons across the United States ranged from
6.2 per 100,000 (Washington, DC) to 29.4 per 100,000 (Wyoming) (Drapeau &
McIntosh, 2020).
• Suicide rates have been increasing. From 2014 to 2019, the rates per 100,000 persons
climbed respectively from 13.4, 13.8, 13.9, 14.5, to 14.8, with a slight decrease in
2019, to 14.5.
Furthermore, more than 1.2 million nonfatal suicide attempts occurred in the United
States in 2020, sometimes resulting in permanent injury or disabling conditions and
sometimes imperiling a concerned person who tried to prevent the suicide from hap-
pening (CDC, 2022a).
For a worldwide analysis of suicide deaths and homicide deaths, consult the World
Health Organization (WHO, 2021c) and the World Population Review (2022). The num-
ber of suicides worldwide in 2019 was 703,000 (WHO, 2021c) and the number of homi-
cides globally in 2017 was 464,000 (World Population Review, 2022).
Nonsuicidal self-injury (NSSI) behaviors are less often reported in the literature and
often not the focus of a risk assessment unless it is the presenting problem. This does
not mean the prevalence of NSSI is not occurring at alarming rates. NSSI is reported to
occur among 13–17% of American adolescents and young adults (Swannell et al., 2014).
Furthermore, NSSI relates to other mental health issues including depression, anxiety,
posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD), and suicidal risk. The recent inclusion of NSSI
M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 165	M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 165 	18/04/23 4:55 PM	18/04/23 4:55 PM

-- 3 of 87 --

166 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
behaviors in the fifth edition, text revision of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of
Mental Disorders (DSM-5-TR; American Psychiatric Association, 2022) reveals the need
for a thorough risk assessment that inc

title: readings Crisis Class 3 reading (Part 2/29)
content:
ludes NSSI behaviors.
Effective risk prevention requires input from multiple systems and increased
involvement from governments; the professions of mental health, public health, and
medical health; law enforcement; schools, universities, and other institutions of learning;
the media; and community leaders. Effective risk assessment also begins with the ability
of the crisis counselor to initiate and ask direct questions about risk assessment. If the
crisis worker does not bring up any of these issues or feels uncomfortable bringing these
issues up, the client may not feel comfortable bringing up risk issues. The first step to
providing a competent risk assessment is to be aware of comfortability in asking about
risk assessment. Activity 6.1 provides structured practice in asking clients about suicide.
You can also use the same activity to practice assessing NSSI and homicidal risk.
Form a group in which participants stand in two lines
facing each other. At the direction of a leader, the
participants in line 1 ask their partners in line 2, “Are
you having thoughts of suicide?” The members of line
1 should then move to the right (or circle to the other
end of the line if they are at the far-right end of the line)
and ask the next partner the same question. Continue
doing this until all line 1 members have asked everyone
in line 2 the same question. Then repeat the activity, but
this time, every member of line 2 now asks members
in line 1, “Are you having thoughts of suicide?” After
the exercise, discuss together as a group the following
questions:
• Which role did you prefer: being the person
asked or being the person doing the asking?
Explain.
• Why do you think it is important to practice
asking the question, “Are you having thoughts
of suicide?”
• How do you feel about using the word suicide
or about looking someone in the eye and asking
directly about suicide?
• What are some other equally direct and
nonjudgmental ways that you could ask about
someone’s intention to self-injure?
• What has been your experience with suicide, both
personally and professionally? Consider what you
do and do not know about working with a client
in a suicidal crisis or emergency. What more do
you need to know before you work with a client
who is suicidal? What personal attitudes, values,
opinions, or views do you have about suicide?
ACTIVITY 6.1
Suicide Terminology
The use of terminology is important when discussing suicide since the language chosen
could reflect personal attitudes and affect those impacted by suicide. For example, the
phrase “committed suicide,” although once common, is less acceptable as it implies a
negative perspective of the person. Compare the phrase to “committed adultery” or
“committed murder,” as they often carry a negative view of the person who participated
in or “committed” the act. If we define a person solely by the label of a mental illness,
we diminish the whole person.
In comparison to other types of medical deaths, we often refer to the cause of
death rather than placing responsibility on the person, such as “cancer-related death”
or “died of cancer.” Following these guidelines, language such as “died by suicide,”
“suicide death,” or “took their own life” are more acceptable phrases as they offer a
more humanistic approach to those grieving the death of a suicide and those who may
M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 166	M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 166 	18/04/23 4:55 PM	18/04/23 4:55 PM

-- 4 of 87 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	167
have attempted or taken their own life. Terminology now considered unacceptable has
been used for decades by professionals, so be patient and use this as a time to provide
educational guidance to those in the field who may still use what now is viewed as
unacceptable terminology. Professional literature will still include what is now viewed
as unacceptable language. Try using the examples in Table 6.1 of “acceptable language”
in place of what is now viewed as “unacceptable language” around suicide.
Many individuals are impacted by the loss of a loved one from suicide, so it is
essential to understand correct terminology when referring to those impacted by suicide
and with other professionals. Primary victims are the individuals who took their own life
or died by suicide. Attempt survivors are those persons who have attempted to take their
own life but did not die. Survivors are loved ones affected by the loss of a person who
died by suicide.
RISK FACTORS, PROTECTIVE FACTORS, AND WARNING SIGNS
Learning Objective 6.2 Describe risk factors, protective factors, and warning signs
of suicide.
Most often, counselors attempting to provide a thorough risk assessment for suicide
will assess and report only on the risk factors of a client experiencing thoughts of
suicide or who has attempted suicide. Although this is an essential part of the suicide
assessment, protective factors are just as clinically essential to explore because they
offer insights into what is actually protecting or preventing the individual from taking
their life. So specifically ask, “What has kept you from following through on taking
your life?”
Counselors should discern these protective factors from the overall assessment and
build them into the care management aspect of treatment. If a counselor only focuses
on the risk factors and does not integrate the protective factors, the level of risk and
lethality may not be accurate. Risk and protective factors are individualized and depend
on unique client circumstances. Therefore, no single risk or protective factor can be used
across clients to indicate certain lethality and risk. Rather, the entire assessment profile
helps a counselor determine the most effective client care management action.
Risk Factors
Risk factors increase the likelihood of a person experiencing suicidal thoughts or
behaviors. Risk factors are often seen as the absence of something (e.g., supports,
medication compliance, medical wellness, loss of loved ones) but may include underlying
Acceptable Language 	Language to Avoid
Died by/of suicide 	Committed/Completed suicide
Suicide death 	Successful attempt
Suicide attempt (attempt suicide) 	Unsuccessful attempt
Person experiencing suicidal thoughts or behaviors 	Suicide attempter
TABLE 6.1 Acceptable Language to Describe Suicide and Language to Avoid
M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 167	M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 167 	18/04/23 4:55 PM	18/04/23 4:55 PM

-- 5 of 87 --

168 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
meaning-related factors (e.g., feelings of guilt/shame, abandonment, being a burden)
that often increase risk lethality.
Many special groups have a heightened risk for suicide. These include Native
Americans; members of the military or veterans; first responders; older persons; persons
with life-threatening depression and/or bipolar disorder; victims of bullying; and youth
who are lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, questioning, and other (LGBTQ+). For these
groups, suicide death rates are higher than average for the population. It is important to
note that a certain group does not have traits that make them more suicidal; rather, other
external determinants elevate the risk that members of some groups may become self-
violent. These factors may include marginalization, trauma events or complex trauma,
poverty, and exposure to self-violence or violence, among other factors.
Although agencies and clinicians may have different ways to inquire about risk
factors, for the following discussion, risk factors are placed into three main categories:
health factors, environmental factors, and historical factors. These categories may have
some factors in common.
HEALTH FACTORS 	Health factors include medical or mental health concerns. Often,
physical illnesses such as a terminal disease may also affect a person emotionally and
existentially. Thus, such illnesses may become complex risk factors. For example, an
individual with cancer may not be able to work, may become dependent on others, and
this may affect their family (e.g., financially or via role changes). In addition, the
individual may perceive they have become a burden to family members for their care,
which may be a significant change from their previous role of taking care of others.
Therefore, it is important that counselors understand not only the surface risk factor
(medical diagnosis) but also other factors that may complicate the risk (e.g., financial,
job status, perception of being a burden on others, independence).
Some mental health concerns may also impact the risk for an individual. Often
when the client is not able to think through rational situations or is impulsive, a higher
risk of acting on suicidal thoughts exists. Mood disorders, schizophrenia, anxiety
disorders, substance use, and certain personality disorders may also present a higher
risk for suicidal behaviors.
ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS 	Environmental factors can include cultural, behavioral,
and personality issues. Cultural issues include, but are not limited to, those relating to
race/ethnicity, gender identity, socioeconomic status, political affiliation, religious/
spiritual affiliation, sexual orientation/identity, age, and sex. Other environmental
factors may include, but are not limited to:
• Hopelessness
• Impulsive or aggressive tendencies
• Family history of suicide
• Recent job or financial loss
• Recent loss of relationship
• Perceived rejection associated with personal meaning
• Access to lethal means
• Lack of social support and sense of isolation
• Stigma associated with asking for help
• Lack of health care, especially mental health and substance use disorder treatment

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	169
• Cultural and religious beliefs, such as the belief that suicide is a noble resolution
of a personal dilemma
• Exposure to others who have died by suicide (in real life or via the media)
• Ability to be a part of one’s personal rescue plan from past suicide thoughts/
behaviors
HISTORICAL FACTORS 	Historical factors are unique to the client’s life and include, but
are not limited to, history of trauma or abuse, abandonment of social supports, family-
of-origin risk factors, previous suicide attempts, family history of suicide, or humiliating
past events that are still causing pain for the client.
Protective Factors
Protective factors make a client less likely to engage in suicidal behavior. Protective
factors can also promote resilience and ensure connectedness with others during diffi-
cult times, thereby making suicidal behaviors less likely. Often, the factors listed as risk
factors for some clients may also be protective factors. For example, a client’s religious
beliefs may be the reason the client is not following through on the plan to die by suicide.
Consider this statement made by a client, “I have not followed through because I do
not believe I would be joined with my other family members in heaven.” A counselor
would not say, “Yes, according to your beliefs, you may not go to heaven.” Rather, a
counselor may respond, “It sounds like being with your family after death is important
to you. Tell me more about your religious/spiritual beliefs and the importance of being
reunited with loved ones.”
Another protective factor may be a single mother who says, “I have
not acted on my suicidal thoughts because I would hate for the father of our
children, who has been abusive to me, to be given custody and abuse the
kids. I cannot do that to my children.” Her desire to make sure her children
are cared for is a protective factor. A counselor would not reply with, “Yes,
that would be quite selfish to have your children experience the abuse you
did because you decided to kill yourself.” Rather, a response to support
the protective factor may be, “It sounds like you do not want your children
to experience the same abuse from their father that you did, and you are
concerned that if you took your own life, they would not be cared for the
way you want to take care of them. Tell me more about your children and
what you want for them.”
Other protective factors include, but are not limited to:
• Hopefulness
• Restricted access to highly lethal means of suicide
• Strong connections to family, friends, social, and community supports
• Skills in problem solving, conflict resolution, handling problems in a nonviolent
way, and lack of impulsivity in past situations
• Ability to see alternatives, limited cognitive distortions, and less impulsivity
• Cultural or religious beliefs that discourage suicide and support self-preservation
• Medication compliance
• History of ability to solve problems and create solutions
• History of demonstrating skills for handling emotional crises

170 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
• Expression of concrete and detailed future plans
• Family commitments, such as raising children or caring for siblings
• Willingness to sign a no-harm contract
• Being resilient in past situations
• Having a sense of purpose or meaning
Warning Signs
Warning signs may be present prior to death by suicide but not recalled until after the
fact. Survivors may experience guilt in not intervening in the suicide, which may cause
more complicated grief issues. Other clients may not present with warning signs at all.
Often, individuals who do not present with warning signs are those who truly want to
die and do not want any intervention. In addition, some warning signs may be normal
behavior for the person who died and therefore may not be seen as a warning sign by
family, friends, and counselors.
Some warning signs may fall into risk factors. Aside from assessment of risk,
counselors should be aware of various warning signs that a client may present
and provide psychoeducation and information to family members and friends (as
appropriate), such as including possible things to look for when they know their loved
one has indicated they are experiencing thoughts of suicide. The following are common
warning signs of suicide that may lead to acute suicidal thoughts or behaviors requiring
immediate intervention (Rudd et al., 2006).
• Talking about wanting to die
• Researching methods of dying, preparations, or rehearsal of taking one’s own life
• Talking about feeling hopeless or having no purpose; self-hate statements
• Talking about feeling trapped or being in unbearable pain
• Talking about being a burden to others; recent hospital discharge
• Increasing use of alcohol or drugs
• Acting anxious, agitated, impulsive, or reckless, especially if this is not the norm
• Sleeping too little or too much
• Withdrawing or feeling isolated
• Showing rage or talking about seeking revenge
• Displaying extreme mood swings
• Cognitive, behavioral, or mood changes
ACUTE VERSUS CHRONIC SUICIDE RISK
Learning Objective 6.3 Distinguish between acute and chronic suicidal risk.
Acute suicidal risk is defined as an individual who reports or presents with a desire
to die in a foreseeable time frame—usually 24 hours or less—with statements about a
specific plan, intent, means, and method (Kleespies, 2000). Some counselors may allow
for a longer time period, but there is still a specific time frame. For example, a client
who has been experiencing chronic and complex grief may state, “I just cannot get over
my mother’s death. On Mother’s Day this year, I plan to end it all so I can be with my
mother.” It would still be appropriate to consider this as an acute risk situation and to
take appropriate measures to start the preventative suicidal assessment and intervention.
M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 170	M06_Jackson_4_78918.indd 170 	18/04/23 4:55 PM	18/04/23 4:55 PM

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	171
Acute crisis refers to situations that require immediate professional intervention.
For example, Jobes (2016) concluded there is a strong relationship between acute risk
and acute crisis events, such as personal failures that may impact core meaning or
situations that are perceived as humiliating to the client. Any recent events need to be
assessed to determine whether the client perceives the events as humiliating. These
acute crises alone may increase the suicidal risk and lethality to a high level, resulting
in immediate and imminent risk. Examples include demotions at work, a relationship
breakup, legal situations, discovering a betrayal, foreclosure or encountering adverse
financial situations, educational failure, and medical issues that may be perceived to
cause a financial burden on family members. Often, a humiliating event can cause an
individual to not think clearly and, combined with impulsive personalities, can trigger
suicidal behaviors.
Chronic suicidal risk is often connected with long-term patterns of relationship
difficulties, interpersonal conflicts, poor decision making in various aspects of life,
and repetitive destructive patterns causing life situation difficulties. These chronic
risk situations may be associated with chronic suicidal thoughts and possibly chronic
behaviors, but individuals may have less specific plans and less lethal intent or means.
Thoughts of suicide may last over long periods of time and may not appear as imminent
or immediate. Chronic risk must be assessed for the possible emergence of active or
acute risk.
Individuals with chronic risk can be described as having “markers” of a potential
suicide risk that is not currently imminent. In other words, individuals may present with
some identifiers of persons who could become acutely suicidal but are not currently in
imminent danger. Just as with other medical diagnoses, the client in your office with a
family history of cancer (a risk factor) may not ever be stricken with cancer. A person
who is chronically in suicide risk may thus have markers or risk factors for potential
suicide, but there is a chance the person will never attempt suicide. Chronic risk should
be observed closely for the possible emergence of active or acute risk. A few of these
chronic markers might include the following:
• Being from a high-risk suicidal group
• Diagnoses including but not limited to mood disorders (depression or bipolar
disorder), anxiety, eating disorder, thought disorder, substance use disorder or
some personality disorders
• History of trauma, abuse, violence, self-injury, previous hospitalizations, low self-
esteem, high self-hate, tolerance and acceptance toward suicide, oppression related
to sexual orientation, perfectionism, divorced parents, family history of suicide
attempts or fatalities, and other factors
In summary, chronic risk suggests the presence of longer-term potential

title: readings Crisis Class 3 reading (Part 4/29)
content:
al of
suicidality, but with less specificity of intent or plans (e.g., means or methods) to carry out
an imminent attempt. The counselor has more time to help the client mitigate, manage,
or resolve psychological pain (and the need to end the pain), can be less directive,
and may not resort to immediate protection such as hospitalization as a treatment
strategy. The counselor will instead reassess suicidality regularly, manage safety, and
attempt to stabilize long-term risk with effective counseling. Table 6.2 provides further
differentiation between the acute and chronic risk categories.

172 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
LEVEL OF SUICIDAL RISK
Learning Objective 6.4 Describe how counselors assess a client’s suicidal risk as no,
low, moderate, or high.
The gathering of information regarding risk factors, protective factors, warning signs,
and acute versus chronic risk will likely render a decision connected with lethality. Most
counselors assess a client’s suicidal risk on a continuum of low, moderate, or high:
• Low suicidal risk includes the presence of suicidal ideation/behaviors or infrequent
thoughts without a plan and means to carry out the plan.
• Moderate suicidal risk includes serious thoughts and desire to die with ideas of a
plan and means, often no access, some existence of social support, but also some
risk factors.
• High suicidal risk includes details of a plan and means to carry out the plan,
presence of risk factors, and absence of protective factors including support.
Although many scales indicate low, moderate, and high, there should also be a note if
there is no (or none) suicidal risk detected. Indicating “no or none” is a best practice
and allows you and other referring counselors to have a better comparison of suicidal
thoughts and behaviors over time.

174 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
experiencing suicidal thoughts. Law enforcement “hostage negotiators” also use
variations of this guide for crisis response teams.
In addition, the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (NSPL; 2007) recommends
following a rubric of assessing suicide risk based on the individual’s desire, capability
to attempt, and intent to die or attempt suicide.
Suicidal desire is indicated by:
• Desire to harm self or others
• Psychological pain such as hopelessness, helplessness, or feeling trapped
• Perceiving oneself to be a burden on others
Suicidal capability is evidenced by:
• History of suicide attempts
• Exposure to someone else’s death by suicide
• History of or even current violent behaviors toward oneself or others
• Having the means to hurt oneself or others
• Current intoxication or excessive substance use
• Recent dramatic mood changes or loss of orientation to reality
• Extreme agitation, rage, increased anxiety, decreased sleep, and recent acts or
threats of aggression
Suicidal intent is reflected by
• Attempt in progress
• Plan with a known method to hurt oneself or others
• Preparatory behaviors (e.g., giving away possessions, finalizing one’s business
affairs, saying goodbye [perhaps cryptically], counting pills, reading up on how to
use the method, loading and reloading the gun, rehearsing the method) and stated
intent to die
Now see the various scenarios in Case Study 6.1, which asks readers to assess potentially
suicidal cases.
CASE STUDY 6.1
Potentially Suicidal Cases
After reading the following brief potentially suicidal cases, indicate the risk factors
(health, environmental, historical), protective factors, warning signs, and other relevant
information and indicate whether you believe the suicidal risk is acute or chronic or no,
low, moderate, or high risk.
Case A
Kelson is a 22-year-old who has recently spent 27 months in a conflict zone. Kelson
is headed home for an extended furlough and fidgets and is agitated on the 18-hour
plane ride home. Kelson arrives home to discover their former partner is now involved
with a high school friend. In addition, Kelson’s dad has remarried a woman whom
Kelson has never met. The marriage occurred when Kelson had been deployed for just 4
months. Kelson’s new stepmother has moved into the house where Kelson lived before

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	177
The field has produced a variety of acronyms to assist counselors in recalling risk
and protective factors and warning signs during suicide assessments. These should not
serve to replace a formal model and Q-tree (which we discuss next) but may be helpful in
gathering information on risk and protective factors. The following is not an exhaustive
list but provides examples:
• IS PATH WARM (Juhnke et al., 2003): Ideation, Substances, Purpose, Anxiety,
Trapped, Hopelessness, Withdrawal, Anger, Reckless, and Mood
• SIMPLE STEPS (McGlothlin, 2008): S—Are you Suicidal?, I—Ideation, M—
Means, P— Perturbation, L—Loss, E—Earlier attempts, S—Substance abuse,
T—Troubleshooting the client’s problem-solving ability, E—Emotion (hopeless,
hapless, worthless, loneliness, depression), P—Parents or family history/exposure,
S—Stress and life events
• MAN THIS ISN’T FAIR (Sankaranarayanan, 2007): M—Mental issues (depression,
anxiety, agitation, guilt, shame, delusions), A—Attempts at self-destruction, N—
No positive support from family/peers, T—Triggering stressors, H—Hopelessness,
I—Ideas and intent, S—Substance abuse, I—Illness/chronic pain, S—Suicide in
family history, N’T—(suicide) Note, F—Final arrangements/saying goodbye/
giving away possessions, A—Access to means, I— Isolation, R—Recent psychiatric
hospitalization
The above is only a guide to the factors that may affect risk and lethality.
There is no set number of indicators someone may have to determine lethality,
and a single indicator may not constitute higher risk. In other situations, an
individual may have many indicators but may not be high in lethality. In
still others, a few indicators together may be essential enough to qualify for
high risk. For example, if a client says they have a plan and means, have no
hope, and will follow through on their plan without seeking intervention,
then the individual may certainly have a high suicidal risk. There are many
assessments in the field that can be used to quantify the variables connected
with suicide.
EFFECTIVE QUESTIONS AND ASSESSMENT STRATEGIES
FOR SUICIDE INTERVENTION
Learning Objective 6.5 Describe effective questions and assessment strategies for
suicide intervention.
A suicide risk assessment should always begin at the first session in counseling, and it
should be the main assessment goal in any crisis. The counseling field has a plethora
of assessments and models for assessing suicide risk. A standard of care in our
profession is to not only provide a risk assessment of every person, regardless of the
client’s presenting problem, but also have a framework (model) upon which to base
the assessment. Although the profession does not dictate a specific suicide assessment
to use, it is a standard of care to use effective risk assessment to practice ethically and
reduce negligence.

178 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
An effective suicide risk assessment relies heavily on clinical judgment and
interpretation of available facts, indicators, evidence of past behaviors, rating of current
mood and symptoms, and consideration of all these data against the backdrop of the
field’s knowledge of risk factors, protective factors, epidemiology, and causative factors
that are known to increase the risk of suicidal death. Regardless of the model used, there
are a set of questions that should be integrated to weigh the risk and protective factors
and make a sound clinical impression of lethality to provide a treatment plan.
Question-Tree Approach for Suicide Assessment: Essential Questions
Regardless of the assessment used, certain questions should be part of the suicide
assessment. Depending on the answers, follow-up questions should be asked for a
comprehensive assessment. If a counselor only stays with a surface question and does not
follow up with additional questions, the assessment is not complete and the treatment
plan may be ineffective or inaccurate, leading the counselor to an incorrect lethality
level. Using a question-tree (Q-tree) approach allows the counselor to weigh the risk
and protective factors and formulate the most appropriate plan for a client experiencing
suicidal thoughts or behaviors (see Figure 6.1).
Notice that the risk and protective factors are included in the Q-tree. In addition,
some indicators may be difficult to assess by a question. Desire to die may not, by itself,
indicate an individual’s risk level. Rather, the counselor must assess how capable the
client is to follow through on the desire. Capability is defined by a sense of fearlessness
and impulsivity to follow through on a specific plan and the means available to follow
through on the plan to die or attempt suicide. Neither desire nor capability
predict active behaviors toward seeking death as strongly as intent (Kalafat
et al., 2005). Strong intent to die may lead to reduced motivation or reasons
for living and can result in a more lethal method selection.
The recommendations issued by the NSPL (2007) include exploring
buffers, which are like protective factors to lessen suicidal risk. These may
include (1) immediate supports, (2) social supports, (3) hopefulness and
future plans, (4) engaging in the therapeutic process, (5) desire to live, (6)
core values and beliefs that conflict with suicide, and (7) a sense of meaning/
purpose.
TRIAGE APPROACH 	While using any suicide assessment or Q-tree,
counselors must also be observant and use the TRIAGE approach.
Counselors should observe a client’s affective factors (e.g., nonverbals such as
being withdrawn, uncooperative, distant, disinterested in taking part in a
plan), behavioral factors (e.g., how likely a client is to act on their thoughts or
follow through on a plan to stay alive), and cognitive factors (e.g., Does a
client seem rigid in their thought process or open to exploring th

title: readings Crisis Class 3 reading (Part 6/29)
content:
he situation
and options for life? Are thoughts distorted?). In addition, developmental
factors (e.g., the perception of death as related to a client’s understanding of
death by age, cognitive level, or developmental abilities and limitations) and
environmental factors (e.g., various cultural values important to the individual)
should be gathered and documented.!See Chapter 1 for more details.

180 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
Suicide Lethality Assessments
Many assessments are available in the field and used as frameworks by crisis counselors
in lethality assessment. It is a standard of care to integrate at least one model into your
practice to provide documentation for each client. Regardless of the model selected, the
Q-tree discussed above should be integrated into any of these models. Some models will
indicate a section within the model for gathering risk and protective factors, although the
Q-tree or essential questions are not written into the model. The following assessments
provide a sampling from the field.
BECK SCALE FOR SUICIDE IDEATION 	The Beck Scale for Suicide Ideation (BSI) is a self-
report instrument requiring 10 minutes to administer that evaluates a client’s thoughts,
plans, and intentions to die by suicide. The 21 items are administered by the counselor
using a rating scale that includes items such as “I have made plans to die by suicide”
and “I have access to lethal means.” The instrument requires specific, detailed recall of
suicidal experiences, such as “I was XX% sure I would die by suicide” and “I have only
felt suicidal (number of) times.”
CHRONOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT OF SUICIDE EVENTS 	The Chronological Assessment of
Suicide Events (CASE) model was developed by a former emergency department (ED)
doctor who observed firsthand the high prevalence of suicide risk and suggested there
was a window of opportunity where professionals could intervene when a client was
discharged from psychiatric admission to the ED or psychiatric hospitalization. His
concern was with the high demands on ED medical professionals and being able to bet-
ter assess suicidal risk. The CASE method focused on the immediate factors of the cli-
ent’s suicidal risk—the conditions immediately prior to the psychiatric emergency (6–8
weeks buildup or a pileup of distress), the influences prior to 6–8 weeks of buildup
(e.g., long-ago exposure to suicide, family history of suicide, traumatic events)—and
concludes with questions about the “now” of suicidal feelings and pending days ahead.
The approach emphasizes five sets of question-asking:
1. Shame attenuation (i.e., normalizing feelings of psychological suffering and the
immediate issues the client has experienced)
2. Behavioral incident specificity (e.g., “Where is the weapon?”, “And then what did
you do?”, “And after that, what did you do?”, “Who knows about your plans?”)
3. Gentle assumption (e.g., “How have you thought of killing yourself?” rather than
“Have you ever thought about killing yourself?”)
4. Symptom amplification (e.g., “How much time do you spend thinking about
suicide in a 24-hour period?”)
5. Denial of the specific (e.g., asking clients if they have considered other methods—
this will not give clients the idea of having more options)
COLLABORATIVE ASSESSMENT FOR MANAGING SUICIDE 	Jobes (2016) developed an
assessment that combines the interview with the written instrument tool. Collaborative
Assessment for Managing Suicide (CAMS) is evidence and theory based and requires
close collaboration with the client to understand the details of the suicidal risk. CAMS
integrates safety and treatment planning and has been integrated into a 4-hour online
training. It can be found at http://www.empathosresources.com (Empathos, 2015).

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	181
COLUMBIA-SUICIDE SEVERITY RATING SCALE (C-SSRS) 	The Columbia-Suicide Severity
Rating Scale (C-SSRS; Posner et al., 2011) includes detailed categories of suicide ideation,
intensity of ideation, and suicidal behavior to assess lethality of suicide. The scale has
been adapted and developed for various populations (e.g., general population, schools,
and first responders) and has suggested actions coded as red, yellow, and green based
on the responses of the individual.
MINI-MENTAL STATUS EXAMINATION (MMSE) 	The Mini-Mental Status Examination
(MMSE) is a brief (10- to 15-minute), quantitative measure to assess cognitive
impairment for those age 18–85 years, is available in over 70 languages, and is
recommended as a screening tool for depressed individuals (Folstein et al., 2016). The
MMSE demonstrated score validity and reliability in psychiatric, neurologic, geriatric,
and other medical populations. It is used to evaluate five areas of mental status:
orientation, registration, attention and calculation, recall, and language. The maximum
score is 30, and a score of 23 or lower demonstrates a level of cognitive impairment
indicating a referral for a more intensive cognitive evaluation. Orientation, place, and
recall are not impacted by level of education and are important to assess prior to
administering the examination. If only assessing for orientation, place, and recall, a
total maximum score is 17. The MMSE is attributed to the following descriptors that are
frequently referred to in many client charts:
• Oriented *3 (person, place, time)
• Oriented *4 (person, place, time, situation/event)
POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SUICIDE IDEATION INVENTORY 	Positive and Negative Suicide
Ideation Inventory (PANSI) is a 20-item self-report measure of positive and negative
thoughts related to suicide. It contains statements such as “I am in control of my life”
and “I think about killing myself,” to which clients respond on a 1- to 5-point Likert-
type response scale. This instrument is intended to be used as a screening tool for adults
and high school students.
SUICIDE ASSESSMENT FIVE-STEP EVALUATION AND TRIAGE 	The Suicide Assessment
Five-Step Evaluation and Triage (SAFE-T) can be used by mental health professionals
during the initial contact with a person experiencing thoughts of suicide. The five-
step assessment includes identification of risk and protective factors; conducting an
inquiry about suicidality (implementing a Q-tree); determining level of risk and
selecting an appropriate intervention; and documenting the process, including a
follow-up plan.
TASK MODEL OF CRISIS ASSESSMENT AND INTERVENTION 	The task model of crisis
assessment and intervention (see Table 6.4!and Chapter 1; see also Jackson-Cherry,
2018) can be integrated for a suicide assessment and focuses on four important areas of
crisis assessment leading to a more effective and appropriate four-task intervention plan
for individuals in virtually every type of crisis, regardless of setting. Assessment of
safety/stabilization; biopsychosocial/spiritual elements; clarification of the problem(s);
and coping skills, resources, and supports are essential first steps toward more accu-
rately defining the actual crisis situation.

182 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
TREATMENT PLANNING AND CARE MANAGEMENT WITH A
SUICIDAL CLIENT
Learning Objective 6.6 Describe appropriate treatment planning and care
management wi

title: readings Crisis Class 3 reading (Part 7/29)
content:
th a suicidal client, including a safety plan, outpatient and inpatient
treatment, and postvention.
After assessing a client’s acute or chronic risk and determining the level of risk, many mental
health professionals might again ask, “Now what do I do?” Assessment is only part of the
intervention process. Rudd et al. (2004) and Jobes (2016), among others, provide qualified
step-by-step recommendations for appropriate treatment planning and responding. Just as
all treatment plans for clients seeking counseling for any condition are customized to meet
their needs, the same is true for clients at risk for suicide. The assessment process yields
valuable information to assist with the design of appropriate treatment interventions. This
section reviews several ideas for counselors to consider, beginning perhaps with the most
important intervention of all: the development of a customized safety plan.
Safety Plan
Developing a safety plan with the client is an industry standard and expectation, but
many mental health professionals were trained prior to the emergence of research
and data to write a “no-harm contract.” Based on research, best-practice thinking,
examination of the potential for false security regarding liability, and more evolved
legal, ethical, and clinical reasoning about how much a suicidal client might be able to
“agree” to such a “contract,” the no-harm contract has been replaced with professional
and empirical preference for the safety plan.
Even seasoned practitioners who are reluctant to do away with the no-harm
contract should be advised that not only is it ill-advised but it is also in contradiction
to many more modern insights about safety planning, and they could run the risk of
increasing liability if using no-harm contracts even in conjunction with safety plans.
Liability for knowing what to do in the case of a suicidal crisis is clearly the burden
of responsibility on the professional and not the client, who would be potentially
