Academic Honesty:
Northwest University expects honesty from students in all areas, including their academic lives.
Academic dishonesty is a serious violation of university standards and may result in the students’
receiving an “F” on the assignment, receiving an “F” for the course, or being dismissed from the
University. Instances of academic dishonesty are coordinated through the respective graduate
program office and typically reported to the Provost’s Office.
Academic dishonesty includes cheating on assignments or examinations, submitting the same (or
essentially the same) paper in more than one course without prior consent of the current
assigning professor(s), sabotaging another student’s work, and plagiarizing. Plagiarism is “using
someone else’s ideas or phrasing and representing those ideas or phrasing as our own, either on
purpose or through carelessness” (MLA). Any student disciplined by a faculty member for
alleged academic dishonesty has the right to appeal the disciplinary action. The student should
initiate his/her appeal according to procedures outlined in the Northwest University Student
Academic Appeals Policy, which is available in the provost Office.
Attendance Policy:
Class attendance is extremely important for student learning and achievement of specified course
outcomes, and, in turn, the program aims. Hence, attendance at all class sessions is expected. At
minimum, students absent for a class session will need to make up any missed assignments or
exercises, watch the class recording, and complete the expected readings. Students may not miss
more than one class. Additional policies and consequences are at the discretion of the individual
professor of the course. More than one missed class session will result in failure of the course
and require a retake to continue in the program. In this circumstance, the student is encouraged to
withdraw from the course and retake the class when it is offered again. In the event of unusual
circumstances (e.g., extended illness, emergency), a student may appeal the automatic course
failure. A proposal outlining a plan for satisfactory course completion will be required,
composed by the professor, and agreed to by the student. In addition, the plan will require
approval by the program director and Dean.
Disability Accommodations and Procedures:
For students with learning differences and/or physical disabilities, as defined by the ADA and Section 504 of the
Rehabilitation Act, Northwest University takes an individual and holistic approach to providing
accommodations. Accommodations are developed and approved on an individual basis by the Student Development
Office, not the CMHC program or individual faculty members. Students needing accommodations for a diagnosed
disability should follow the procedures provided on the Student Development Office’s website for
disability accommodations.

-- 5 of 14 --

6
Communication Policy:
Timely communication between the professor and students is important to the program's overall
functioning. Therefore, both students and faculty should respond to email or phone messages
within two business days. If the professor is out-of-office, an automatic reply message (on email)
or voicemail recording (phone) provides the date of his/her return, as well as an in-office contact
for urgent requests.
Grading Policy:
Grading is conducted on the Discovery Grade Book page of the course. Students are expected to
monitor their progress accordingly. Assignments and tests will generally be graded within two
weeks. Final papers and projects may take up to three weeks. The professor will let you know if
there are exceptional circumstances preventing a timely return of grades. If you have concerns
about grading timing, please check with the professor, program coordinator, or program chair.
Late Work Policy:
It is the student’s responsibility to meet all deadlines for class assignments. Late assignments
will be accepted up until one week after the due date with a 10% reduction. After that, late
assignments will not be accepted without prior approval. Grading is conducted on the
Discovery grade book page of the course. Students are expected to monitor their progress. It is
your responsibility to communicate with the professor promptly about late work and absences.
APA Style and Formatting
All assignments should be turned in using the current edition of the APA style manual. APA
formatting includes a title page, headers, abstract, introduction, discussion, summary, references.
Please review the APA style guide for information on how to correctly format your papers. In
addition, assignments should be written in APA style with a focus on clarity, conciseness, and
cohesion. Unless noted as a reflection, students should use academic language and review
assignments for grammatical errors.
Mechanics of the Course:
A) Class Sessions
Class sessions will be used for a variety of purposes including:
Lecture
Class and group discussion
Writing
Presentations
B) Exams: Midterm Exam
The course will have one exam in class 3. The content of the exam will cover materials from
chapter 1-4, and 6 of the Jackson-Cherry & Erford text. It will be a 60-question exam and
students will have 90 minutes to complete. It is a closed book exam.
C) Response, Research, or Written Assignments:

-- 6 of 14 --

7
Assignment 1- Case Study: Crisis Assessment and Intervention
This is a Key Assessment
Applying knowledge and information from your reading and the assessment and intervention
with suicidal clients videos (Psychotherapy.net, 2008) we will watch: The wounds we cannot see
featuring Nancy Ross in class and develop a case study, imagining Nancy Ross as your client.
Due in Class 5
• Summary of the client’s history (including family history, academic/occupational history,
medical information, developmental/social history, history of trauma)
• Any current crisis issues, including suicide risk factors, and your plan to address them
• Ethical and legal considerations including mandated reporting
• Multi-cultural and spiritual considerations
• Client strengths
• Evidence for diagnostic considerations
• Basic Treatment plan: identify at least 2 problems, 2 Client goal(s) with at least 2
objectives for each goal, and your intervention and plan for working with Nancy. These
should be written in SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Timely)
format. Use the treatment plan layout for this page.
• Identify resources and referrals you would offer this client
• Countertransference reactions may emerge for you as you consider working with this
client
• Questions you have, to address in supervision
Please answer the above items in paragraph form. No bullet points, except on the treatment plan,
please use this assignment to pull in learning from other classes in your studies and use APA
formatting. Use precise language to clearly demonstrate your learning and ability to identify
crisis intervention techniques and understanding with Nancy.
Instructions:
• Length is approximately 4-6 pages). This does not include the Student Title Page
or Reference Page.
• APA 7 Format, 1-inch margins, 12-point Times New Roman font, double spaced,
student title page and reference pages.
*Please note: This assignment is a key performance indicator for your proficiency in Counseling
Theories, Skills, and Helping Relationships at this point in the program. Your knowledge of
counseling skills, case conceptualization, suicide prevention, and treatment planning into
counseling practice, both in-person and technology assisted settings, for those in crisis will be
assessed.

-- 7 of 14 --

8
Assignment 2: Assessment and Intervention with Suicidal Clients Training: Due in Class 2
This assignment aims to deepen your understanding of assessment and intervention strategies
with suicidal clients through guided training videos and a corresponding manual.
INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Watch Training Videos: You are required to watch the following three training videos
provided by Psychotherapy.net on the topic of Assessment and Intervention with
Suicidal Clients (Parts 1,2, and 3). Ensure you pay close attention to the techniques,
strategies, and case examples presented in the videos.
2. Reflection: This reflection assignment is designed to help you critically engage with and
synthesize the knowledge gained from the training videos and manual on Assessment
and Intervention with Suicidal Clients. Through this reflection, you will connect the
course material with your personal insights, professional development, and future
practice.
Possible Reflection Prompts:
• Key points: What important points did you learn in watching John Sommers-Flanagan work with
various suicidal clients? What stands out most to you about how he worked with his clients? Did you
get a solid sense of the concepts and techniques, and the flow of the interventions in the demonstrated
cases? Why or why not?
• What I found most helpful: As a counselor/therapist, what was most beneficial to you about the ideas
and/or techniques presented? What specific tips or perspectives did you find helpful and might you use
in your own clinical work? What about the way he worked challenged you to think about something in
a new way?
• What does not make sense: Which of his ideas or interventions did not make sense to you? Did
anything push your buttons or bring about a sense of discomfort or resistance in you, or just not fit with
your own style of working?
• Individualizing your Approach: What might you do differently from Sommers-Flanagan when
assessing or treating a suicidal client? Be specific about what different models, interventions or
techniques you might apply.
• Acknowledging Cultural Differences: How did the counselor differ his approach with each client?
How did he take into account cultural, ethnic, gender, or spiritual differences of the clients that he
worked with?
• Other questions/reactions: What questions or reactions did you have as you viewed the video? Other
comments, thoughts, or feelings?
3. Guidelines: Be brief and concise. Do NOT provide a full synopsis of the video. This is
meant to be a brief reaction paper that you write soon after watching the video—we want
your ideas and reactions. Your reflection should be 2-3 pages in length, double-spaced,
using APA 7th edition formatting. This includes: Title page (required but not counted in
the page count), proper headings to organize your thoughts, and References page (if you
cite additional sources). Format: Use 12-point Times New Roman font, include a title
page, page numbers, and properly formatted in-text citations (if applicable), and structure
your reflection with an introduction, body, and conclusion.

-- 8 of 14 --

9
Assignment 3: Psychological First Aid Module (25 points) Due Class 4
Students will complete a psychological first aid module online using the link provided:
https://learn.nctsn.org/course/index.php?categoryid=11. Full points are awarded based on
students providing evidence that the module has been completed (e.g., a snapshot of the
certificate). If a student does not pass the quizzes in the module, they will not receive credit for
this assignment.
Note: Because Assignments 2 and 3 take a significant amount of time, these modules will
replace one live class session, to make up for this added instructional time.
Assignment 4: Institutional/Disaster Response Presentation- (Class 4)
Working in groups, students will select a real-life example of a crisis in a school or a natural
disaster (e.g., Columbine school shooting or Hurricane Katrina) and give a 20-minute
presentation to your colleagues. Presentation topics will be approved by the professor. Groups
and events will be selected on the first day of class, and there will be no duplication. There will
be six groups total. *See Discovery for resources. Presentations will include:
1. Provide a synopsis of the crisis or disaster scenario. Every group needs a unique
type of crisis event. Event should have occurred at least 3 years ago and no more
than 15 years ago.
2. Conduct an event/incident assessment using one of the models from chapter 12 or
13 of the Jackson-Cherry and Erford textbook.
3. Analyze how the crisis was managed using media/historical information and
learning from the readings, considering what was done properly and areas for
change.
4. Using learning from the readings and media coverage (if available) to highlight
and discuss your understanding of the counselor role and responsibilities, as
members of an interdisciplinary community outreach and emergency management
response team. Your discussion should be specific to this event using real life
details and you must include details of other professionals essential to the
counselor role/as part of the team.
5. Long-term impact for survivors (may pull research from other events)
6. Provide strategies to support victims after the crisis event from a counseling
perspective.
7. Each participant will provide a 2-3 page personal reflection on the challenges
they might experience working with this crisis and strategies, strengths, and
characteristics for managing them. This reflection will be completed in class.
Assignment 5: Self-Care Practice and Reflection
This assignment is designed to help you intentionally experience daily self-care and reflect on
how consistent self-care affects your mood, stress level, awareness, and overall functioning. As
future helping professionals, developing sustainable self-care habits is an essential professional
skill. For two consecutive weeks (14 days), you will try to engage in one self-care activity each

-- 9 of 14 --

10
day. Self-care activities may include (but are not limited to):
• Meditation or mindfulness practice
• Walking or gentle exercise
• Yoga or stretching
• Creative activities (art, music, writing, crafts)
• Time in nature
• Journaling
• Prayer or spiritual practice
• Intentional rest or screen-free time
• You will identify an activity in class 5. Try to engage in the same activity every day, but
if you are unable to maintain, you can switch to another activity that you may be more
successful at completing.
Reflection
You will write a handwritten reflection. Reflection should be 2-3 pages. Reflections should
include
• What self-care activity you did
• Approximately how long you engaged in the activity
• How you felt before the activity
• How you felt after the activity
• Any thoughts, emotions, or observations that stood out
• How could you apply this to your professional work as a counselor?
This assignment is graded on completion and thoughtful engagement, not on the type of self-
care chosen or the outcome of the experience. There are no “right” or “wrong” reactions.
• Bring your handwritten reflection journal to Class 7.
• You may be asked to share general observations or insights from your experience in
small or large group discussion.
• You will not be required to read your journal aloud or disclose personal details beyond
your comfort level.
Assignment 6: Faith Integration Reflection
You will write a 2–3 page reflection paper (in-class) exploring how faith and spirituality can
inform and shape your approach to helping others cope with grief. This is a reflective and
integrative assignment rather than a research paper. This will be completed in class 6.
D) Participation
A portion of the student's final grade will be based on their participation in class and group

11
discussions, in class exercises, individual and group presentations, and attendance. Full points
will be rewarded to those students who attend all class sessions and actively participate in class
activities.

Exams and presentations will be graded the same day. Papers will be graded within 2 weeks. If
you have concerns about grading timing, please check with the professor, program coordinator,
or program Chair.
The students course grade will be based upon her/his mastery of course terms, concept, and
objections as demonstrated by classroom attendance, participation, presentations, and papers.
Please be on time with all assignments, if late, there will be a deduction of grade points. Grading
is conducted on the Discovery Grade Book page of the course. Students are expected to monitor
their progress.

title: Slides: Chapter 7 crisis counseling
content:
If this PowerPoint presentation contains mathematical equations, you may need to check that your computer has the following installed:
1) MathType Plugin
2) Math Player (free versions available)
3) NVDA Reader (free versions available)
Chapter 7
Understanding and Treating Substance Use Disorders with Clients in Crisis
Substance Use and Society
Data from the 2019 National Survey on Drug Use and Health (S A M H S A)
Over 50% of Americans age 12 years and older (about 132 million people) are current consumers of alcohol.
Over 21% of Americans age 12 years and older (nearly 58 million people) are current users of tobacco products.
Over 20% of Americans age 12 years and older (nearly 57 million people) reported using an illicit drug in 2019.
A majority (53%) of those reporting use of nonmedical prescription type drugs indicated the illicit substances were acquired from family and friends.
Of the 20.4 million who reported needing treatment for an illicit drug or alcohol problem, only 2.3 million (11%) received treatment.
Classification of Drugs (1 of 2)
Current drug classifications include:
Cannabinoids (e.g., marijuana, hashish, dronabinol)
Depressants (e.g., alcohol, tranquilizers, barbiturates, sleeping pills, inhalants)
Stimulants (e.g., cocaine, amphetamines, methamphetamine, caffeine)
Hallucinogens (e.g., LSD, phencyclidine, ketamine, mescaline, psilocybin)
Opiates (e.g., heroin, morphine, OxyContin, codeine, methadone, nicotine)
Psychotherapeutics (e.g., Prozac, Zoloft, Ativan, Xanax, BuSpar, Cymbalta, Abilify, lithium, Geodon, Zyprexa, Risperdal)
Some drugs can have multiple effects and may fall under several classifications.
Classification of Drugs (2 of 2)
The Comprehensive Drug Abuse Prevention and Control Act of 1970 classified controlled drugs into five schedules based on a continuum of potential for abuse, accepted medical use, and potential for dependence.
Drugs listed under Schedule I
have the highest potential
for abuse and dependence and have no current medical application in the United States (e.g., heroin, L S D).
Drugs listed under Schedule V
have the lowest potential
for abuse and dependence and have an established medical application (e.g., cough syrups with codeine).
Research on Substance Use, Crisis, and Society
Several studies documented the relationship between natural and human-generated disasters and subsequent patterns of substance use.
Researchers found an increased use of substances in the 2 years following terrorist events in the United States, and the rates of use were likely higher than estimated (DiMaggio et al., 2009).
Substance use can not only result from crisis situations, it can also affect crisis situations
Individuals who use substances are nearly six times more likely to attempt suicide than those who report no substance use problems (Dragisic et al., 2015).
Factors That Increase Risk for Substance Use Disorders
Since initial use often occurs during adolescence, most risk factors reflect events and issues relevant to that developmental stage. 
Factors include poor academic performance, alcohol and other drug (A O D) availability, peer pressure, living in a dysfunctional family environment, and undiagnosed problems, such as learning disabilities
Differentiating between risk factors for initial use and S U D is an important consideration given that a majority of those using substances do not develop problematic usage behaviors.
Counselors need to be aware of other potential risk factors such as self-esteem and self-worth issues, isolation, intimacy issues and fear of rejection, poor coping mechanisms, and impulsivity and compulsivity patterns.
Key psychiatric disorders that co-occur with S U D s include major depressive disorders, anxiety disorders, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and certain personality disorders.
Screening for Substance Use Disorders
Screening clarifies whether there is sufficient evidence of problematic substance use and, if yes, whether this use warrants further investigation (assessment).
Questions are designed to determine patterns of use and often focus on frequency, quantity, and problem areas.
Examples of frequency and quantity questions that might be asked during the screening process include “During the past 30 days, how many days per week did you drink alcohol or use drugs?” or “On a day when you did drink alcohol or use drugs, how many drinks did you have (or what quantity of drugs did you use)?”
Commonly Used Assessment Instruments (1 of 2)
The following considerations apply when using an assessment instrument:
The client must sign an informed consent form (e.g., a form that addresses the purpose of the assessment, risks, benefits, how the information will be used, the client’s right to refuse consent).
The counselor should see the client as more than the results (the assessment is only one of many pieces of the clinical picture).
The counselor should ensure that the assessment instrument is applicable and appropriate for the population it will be used to measure.
The counselor should have training and competency in administering the assessment.
The counselor must be able to interpret the results (Stauffer et al., 2020).
Commonly Used Assessment Instruments (2 of 2)
Four commonly used assessment instruments are the:
Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test (AUDIT; Babor et al., 2001)
Michigan Alcoholism Screening Test (MAST; Selzer, 1971)
Substance Abuse Subtle Screening Inventory–4 (SASSI-4;Lazowski et al., 2016)
CAGE (Ewing, 1984)
Assessment for Substance Use Disorders
An assessment is conducted for the following purposes:
to ascertain the degree and severity of a problem
to determine the presence of co-occurring disorders
to make accurate diagnoses
to assess the scope of the problem (i.e., whether it is specific to the individual or systemic, such as within the family or support system)
to guide the treatment planning process
to provide a mechanism for communication across treatment personnel (Erford, 2021)
Assessment for Substance Use Disorders
The primary focus of substance use assessment is to gather detailed information on substance use patterns, including drug(s) used; progression of use (amount and frequency of use over time); route of administration; reason for periods of increased usage or abstinence; treatment history; and evidence of tolerance, dependence, and withdrawal.
When conducting a substance use assessment, counselors should also evaluate the client’s current level of mental functioning, which is often done using the Mini-Mental State Examination in crisis situations.
Diagnosis
A well-conducted, thorough assessment should yield sufficient data and documentation to make accurate decisions and establish the degree of impairment.
With most other mental health diagnoses, providing a full diagnosis during a crisis phase would be premature and inappropriate due to the symptoms and period of time needed to accurately offer a valid diagnosis.
Documenting prior diagnoses, onset, relapse, current client reports, and presentations are essential to provide for the next phase of the treatment so the next clinician can formulate a diagnosis.
Substance Use Disorder Diagnosis (1 of 2)
According to D S M-5-T R, at least two of the following criteria must be met for an individual to be diagnosed with a S U D:
significant problems exist at home, school, or work due to use
physical hazards occur due to repeated episodes of impaired driving or operating machinery while under the influence
substance use results in recurring legal problems (e.g., public intoxication)
tolerance increases
withdrawal occurs
increasing amounts of the substance are taken over time
usage levels are not being reduced
Substance Use Disorder Diagnosis (2 of 2)
a lot of time is spent acquiring the substance
social or work activities are sacrificed due to use or recovery from use
use continues despite interpersonal and physical effects
there is craving, a strong urge, or desire for the substance
Severity is determined by the number of presenting criteria (i.e., mild: two to three criteria; moderate: four to five criteria; severe: six or more criteria).
Assessing a Client’s Readiness to Change
Norcross et al. identified the following five stages of change:
Motivational Interviewing
engaging—establishing a connection between counselor and client that helps to build the working alliance
focusing—involves the counselor developing and maintaining a focused direction in the discussion about change
evoking—assisting the client to take ownership for their motivation for change
planning—establishing a commitment to change with a specific course of action where talk shifts from whether and why it happens to when and how to change
Treatment Admission and Placement (1 of 2)
Treatment Admission and Placement (2 of 2)
A S A M has developed six dimensions that practitioners can use to assess clients at each level and provide a rationale/justification for a particular level of care. The dimensions also serve as standardized assessment language for general health care and mental health practitioners.
Counselors want to select the treatment level that is least intensive but the safest level of care based on the client’s presenting issue and assessed dimensions.
Substance Use Treatment and Crisis
Treating SUDs and symptoms becomes more complex when the client is also experiencing a crisis.
Given that brief interventions and motivational enhancement approaches were two of the highest-ranked, most effective approaches to address S U D s and symptoms, integrating a crisis model that aligns with these approaches allows the counselor to effectively address both the crisis response and S U D.
Relapse and Crisis
During treatment, counselors need to prepare for relapse by helping clients explore triggers and learn new cognitive-behavioral strategies. Even in short-term crisis centers, these triggers can be identified, explored, and documented when clients are referred for treatment.
Counselors need to help clients differentiate when use is viewed as a “lapse” or a “relapse.”
A lapse is a single episode of using a substance following a period of abstinence. The individual often takes responsibility for this lapse and examines the specific experience or trigger(s) that resulted to build on recovery.
A relapse is a return to sustained use of the same substance or another mood-altering substance following a defined period of abstinence.
Harm Reduction
Harm reduction approaches are tools to help the client reduce the negative consequences associated with use, regardless of one’s usage pattern. It can be helpful to find a common ground and meet clients where they are.
Marlatt et al. (2011) identified five underlying principles of harm reduction:
Strive for abstinence while considering alternative options to help minimize harm.
View prevention as a way to reduce the negative consequences or harmful outcomes associated with use but not focusing specifically on usage. 
Focus approaches on where the client is at that point in their use or addiction and develop approaches accordingly.
View harm reduction as a community-oriented process where action is taken at the grassroots level to advocate for change specific to the needs within that community.
Focus on minimizing harmful consequences and not about judging or criticizing client behavior.
Support Groups
Clients with SUDs or significant symptoms often find that as the condition progresses, they become alienated and isolated from family, friends, and coworkers as they spend more time in relationships with their substances and those who enable the behavior.
A common treatment goal is to learn to establish social and coping skills, which are critical components in developing and maintaining abstinence. Support groups play an instrumental role in facilitating and practicing these skills.
Encouraging support group attendance should align with the client’s stage of change.
Clients may also report feeling discouraged or frustrated after attending a meeting and should be encouraged to try out different meetings to find out which best match their needs.
Marital and Family Treatment
Research findings have demonstrated a reciprocal relationship between problem drinking and family and marital discord.
In cases where abstinence has been achieved, those individuals who continue to deal with unresolved marital and family conflicts may be at a higher risk for relapse.
Counselor attributes that tend to lead to successful outcomes include addressing client and family members simultaneously; focusing on alcohol problems first; using empathetic listening and dealing effectively with intense anger during the initial stages of counseling; developing a structured, directive approach that includes establishing rules, boundaries, and consequences; and viewing the change process as long term.
Co-Occurring Disorders and Crisis
SAMHSA (2022) defines a co-occurring disorder as the concomitant occurrence of a substance use and mental health disorder.
Counselors working with clients with co-occurring disorders must be competent in understanding each disorder as well as how to integrate treatment to ensure that the disorders are addressed simultaneously.
Counselors need to understand how substance use can present symptoms that mimic mental illness and other medical conditions.
Multicultural Perspectives, Substance Abuse, and Crisis
Counselors working with clients with S U D s or significant symptoms and in crisis need to understand how the culture of substance use influences each client’s worldview.
Historically, treatment has focused on reduction or abstinence goals, often with little consideration for understanding the role that culture plays in etiology or maintenance of use.
Counselors should maintain a cultural focus when working with clients through the five stages of change. Moreover, triggers for relapse behavior should be examined from a cultural perspective.
Safety and Self-Care
Counselors need to be aware of the dangers of working with clients who pose a threat to public safety or who have a history of violence, especially those who may also be under the influence of a substance.
Counselors should gauge the risk in working with clients who pose safety concerns and can take steps to minimize the risk by screening high-risk clients, avoiding seeing clients when there is a lack of physical security available, recognizing warning signs of violence, avoiding working alone during in-home counseling, and establishing rules prohibiting weapons in session.
Working with clients who have S U D s and significant symptoms creates unique challenges for counselors. Counselors who constantly face client resistance, ambivalence, motivational concerns, and relapse issues may question their effectiveness in facilitating change.
Diagnostic Considerations during a Crisis
A crisis can exacerbate and confound the diagnostic process, especially if the client has co-occurring issues.
The first consideration is to differentiate among symptoms that are due to the acute crisis and symptoms that present as part of the pathology.
Differentiating crisis symptom presentations from pathological symptoms helps counselors explain to clients that their experiences are normal by associating those symptoms with the crisis, which can help minimize the client’s stress.
Caution is warranted when providing a mental health diagnosis that may require more information than can be obtained in an assessment during crisis intervention.
Essential Substance Use–Specific Crisis Intervention Skills
Use of attending behaviors and active listening skills is important in crisis situations.
Creating warmth, expressing genuineness, and creating an atmosphere of trust, safety, and acceptance not only target the motivational factors inherent with treating S U D s and symptoms but also help clients in crisis deescalate from the acute stressor and feel more at ease and comfortable with the counselor.
Using a directive approach, conducted in a compassionate and empathic manner, allows for clarification and understanding of the crisis threat so that appropriate courses of action can be taken.

title: Slides: Crisis Chapter 8
content:
If this PowerPoint presentation contains mathematical equations, you may need to check that your computer has the following installed:
1) MathType Plugin
2) Math Player (free versions available)
3) NVDA Reader (free versions available)
Slides in this presentation contain hyperlinks. JAWS users should be able to get a list of links by using INSERT+F7.
Chapter 8
Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence
Overview of Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence (1 of 2)
Intimate partner violence (I P V) involves physical, sexual, and emotional aggression, violence, or stalking by a current or former intimate partner.
I P V is an inclusive term that is used to describe violence among opposite- or same-sex couples currently or formerly in a relationship.
I P V is only one aspect of domestic violence (D V) and, used by itself, is not inclusive of other individuals who are in violent relationships.
I P V is not the legal term used in federal or state statutes or regulations that outline mandated reporting laws for health care providers or laws that govern law enforcement practices when intervening in D V situations.
Overview of Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence (2 of 2)
Domestic violence (D V) is defined as “violence between spouses, or spousal abuse but can also include cohabitants and non-married intimate partners.”
States may further narrow this definition, and counselors should be aware of the laws that govern their practice with D V victims and perpetrators in their state.
Law enforcement intervention and charges will vary depending on the state’s definition of D V and assault or whether the person in the D V relationship aligns with the definition of D V or assault.
Statistics regarding D V vary due to the range of definitions and data collection methods used by researchers.
Most instances of D V are never reported, so the available facts and figures are considerable underestimates of its occurrence.
The Bureau of Justice Statistics (2021) reported that D V accounted for about 20% of all violent victimizations.
Cycle of Violence Theory
The cycle of violence theory was originally developed by Walker, a psychologist who based her theory on interviews with hundreds of women who experienced D V.
Each phase varied in time and intensity during each cycle of violence for the same couple and between different couples.
Educating I P V/D V clients about the cycle of violence can help in naming experiences as abusive.
Cycle of Violence Theory
Tension-building
The explosion or acute battering incident
The honeymoon phase of kindness and contrite, loving behavior
Cycle of Violence Theory 
Phase 1: Tension-Building
This phase is characterized by mounting pressure and strain in the relationship.
The abused partner “walks on eggshells” around the abusive partner, becoming compliant, nurturing, or whatever it takes to keep the abusive partner’s anger from escalating.
This phase also involves minor battering incidents.
Phase 2: The Acute Battering Incident
This phase occurs when tensions build to the point of explosion in an acute battering incident that is characterized by unpredictability, uncontrollable rage, brutality, and seriously damaging consequences.
The acute battering incident is typically triggered by an external event or the abusive partner’s internal state and can last for hours or even days.
During an acute battering incident, victims have reported dissociative experiences, such as feeling as though they are outside their own bodies and watching themselves being thrown into a wall, choked, or raped.
Cycle of Violence Theory 
Phase 3: The Honeymoon Phase
This phase is characterized by loving and repentant behavior from the abusive partner.
The abuser may shower the I P V/D V victim with gifts, beg for forgiveness, and promise never to be violent again.
The honeymoon phase results in the I P V/D V victim feeling needed and loved, leading to a renewed commitment to the relationship.
After repeated cycles of violence, the honeymoon phase may shorten or eventually be eliminated
Helpful Models for Understanding IPV/DV(1 of 3)
The Duluth model was developed in 1984 by a group of I P V/D V activists and is based on the actual experiences of female I P V/D V survivors.
The central component of the Duluth model is the Power and Control Wheel, a visual representation of the most experienced abusive tactics.
The Power and Control Wheel can be used to help female I P V/D V victims normalize and understand their experiences. It can also assist abusive partners in identifying violent behaviors as part of a batterer intervention program.
Another component of the Duluth model is the Equality Wheel, which is a visual representation of the changes needed to move from an abusive relationship to a nonviolent relationship.
Helpful Models for Understanding I P V/D V (2 of 3)
Counselors seeing clients in crisis should be aware of various resources in the community for referral purposes for clients experiencing I P V/D V, which may help normalize a victim’s experiences while in and after the crisis.
Learned helplessness theory has been used to explain why I P V/D V victims remain in abusive relationships.
An I P V/D V client may reach out for help from family, friends, or the police, yet the battering continues. Thus, the victim receives the message that efforts to control the situation are ineffective. Repeated incidents of uncontrollable violence diminish the victim’s motivation to respond, leading to passivity and learned helplessness.
The contextual model of family stress provides a more comprehensive view of I P V/D V. This model considers the interaction of the stressor event, available resources, an individual’s perception of I P V/D V, the external family context (culture, history, economy, development, and heredity), and the internal family context (family boundaries and rules, understanding of a stressor event, and family values, beliefs, and assumptions).
Helpful Models for Understanding I P V/D V (3 of 3)
Counselors are advised to consider how their interventions with victims and perpetrators of I P V/D V may be influenced by their beliefs about I P V/D V and why victims stay in abusive relationships.
Since I P V/D V impacts so many individuals and families, counselors must be aware of their beliefs and judgments about perpetrators and victims, since this is a population most likely to be served in a crisis setting.
How a counselor views victims and perpetrators in crisis may affect intervention.
Common Crisis Issues (1 of 3)
As a counselor responding to an I P V/D V crisis, the overarching goal is the safety of the victims, regardless of whether they decide to stay in or leave the violent relationships.
Simultaneously, counselors should also work to empower clients to solve problems effectively based on the client’s unique situation and available resources.
Counselors should conduct a triage assessment to determine which aspects of the situation require immediate intervention.
Common crisis issues that emerge when working with I P V/D V clients include attending to physical injury, establishing immediate safety, and deciding whether to report I P V/D V to the police.
Common Crisis Issues (2 of 3)
Attending to physical injury
Physical injury resulting from I P V/D V can range from scrapes and bruises to permanent disfigurement, disability, and death.
Emergency medical treatment may be required before any further counseling intervention can occur; therefore, counselors should be prepared to refer clients and arrange transportation to appropriate medical facilities.
Since I P V/D V can take the form of sexual assault and rape, counselors should also be aware of medical facilities that specialize in rape.
Establishing immediate safety
During an I P V/D V crisis, time is of the essence, and crisis counselors must gain thorough and accurate information quickly by conducting a detailed assessment of the situation, including violence severity, available resources, and barriers to accessing resources and treatment.
In some situations, the client may not be able to return home safely, so the counselor must arrange for emergency shelter. In the event a client decides to return home, a harm reduction plan should be developed.
Common Crisis Issues (3 of 3)
Reporting I P V to the police
The process for reporting an I P V/D V incident to the police is complex and differs from state to state. States outline who is a mandated reporter, what injuries require reporting, and what exceptions are outlined in the law.
Mandated reporting laws for I P V/D V are different from the mandated reporting laws for suspected child, elder, or other vulnerable persons abuse. If a victim of D V discloses there is also abuse to a child, elder, or vulnerable adult, then the mandated reporting under these separate suspected abuse situations must be made in compliance with state mandates.
The majority of I P V/D V incidents are not reported to the police.
Unless suicidality, homicidality, specific injuries sustained, or suspicion of child, elder, or vulnerable persons abuse is disclosed, counselors are not required to report I P V/D V incidents and should support the choice the client makes regarding police notification.
The counselor should discuss the pros and cons of police intervention with the client and then respect the client’s decision.
Intimate Partner Violence in Specific Populations
Culture is important to consider when understanding and treating clients experiencing I P V/D V. It guides how clients define, view, experience, and respond to I P V/D V.
Counselors responding to I P V/D V should attend to cultural dimensions such as sex, race/ethnicity, socioeconomic status (S E S), level of acculturation, language, religiosity, sexual orientation, ability status, and age.
Race and Ethnicity
The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention reported that 54% of Black women and 44% of multiracial, non-Hispanic women experienced sexual assault, I P V, or stalking in their lifetime, which is a higher proportion than White, non-Hispanic women (35%).
Asian or Pacific Islander women were less likely to experience I P V/D V over their lifetime compared to White (non-Hispanic) women.
This report also estimates Alaskan Native men and Black, non-Hispanic men experienced rape, physical violence, or stalking by an intimate partner more frequently than White, non-Hispanic men.
Regardless of the differences in I P V/D V prevalence rates by race and ethnicity, there seem to be similar reasons for remaining in violent relationships.
There are cultural subtleties in how I P V/D V is recognized and addressed that affect reporting trends across racial-ethnic groups. The interrelated factors include patriarchal family structure, S E S, immigrant status, and religious and spiritual beliefs.
Socioeconomic Status
The role of lower socioeconomic status (S E S) may be connected to higher rates of I P V/D V incidents.
Poverty is linked to limited resources, substance use, social isolation, pregnancy, and unemployment. These variables collectively create unhealthy environments that both initiate and perpetuate I P V/D V.
Others argue I P V/D V transcend S E S and occur at similar rates across S E S groups. However, middle- or upper- S E S groups often have the resources to seek more formal counseling, services, or supports that are not often used by reporting agencies.
Immigrant Status
I P V/D V is more common among immigrants and refugees than longer-term U.S. citizens overall, as migration from one country to another often creates isolation that facilitates I P V/D V situations.
Language barriers often prevent women in abusive relationships from seeking assistance. Lack of knowledge of available resources, distrust of government agencies, and lack of knowledge of the U.S. legal system may also be barriers to help seeking for immigrant women who experience I P V/D V.
Undocumented immigrant victims of I P V/D V may be more hesitant to report I P V/D V due to concerns with deportation.
Religious and Spiritual Beliefs
Religiosity, spirituality, and partner violence are linked in both positive and negative ways.
Some individuals who perpetrate partner violence may use (and misuse) religious texts and beliefs to justify or rationalize I P V/D V.
Some I P V/D V victims may stay in abusive relationships due to religious or spiritual beliefs that condemn divorce and promote male domination, superior male morality, the value of suffering, and submission and obedience to husbands.
Some I P V/D V victims may be hesitant to seek help outside of the religious or spiritual community or may be reluctant to disclose I P V/D V to preserve the family image.
Religious or spiritual practices may also serve as protective factors against I P V/D V and as a coping mechanism in recovering from its consequences.
Female-to-Male Violence
Most of the literature, as well as provision of shelters and other resources, have historically focused on women as victims of D V.
Research does support the higher prevalence of women in D V relationships, but data also show that men are victims of D V.
Gender norms may prevent men who suffer emotional, physical, or sexual abuse from reporting because disclosure might “emasculate” them; men may also fail to define their experiences as abusive or may fear being laughed at or ridiculed if they reach out for help; male victims of I P V/D V may also be reluctant to report abuse from a female partner for fear that they will also be accused of being abusive.
Additionally, there are fewer I P V/D V resources available for men.
To effectively meet the needs of male victims, counselors should validate their experiences, provide accurate educational information about I P V/D V, and provide gender-appropriate I P V/D V resources.
L G B T Q + Violence
The lifetime prevalence of I P V/D V for L G B T Q + (lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, questioning, and others) individuals is greater than that in heterosexual couples.
Homophobia and transphobia, or prejudice and discrimination against L G B T Q + individuals and their culture, contribute to the minimization of I P V/D V in the L G B T Q + community and preclude many from seeking help from traditional I P V/D V resources (e.g., shelters, community agencies, the legal system).
There is a lack of resources for L G B T Q + individuals who experience I P V/D V.
Disability Status
Controlling for race/ethnicity, age, and S E S, women with disabilities are physically or sexually assaulted at a rate double that of those without a disability.
A victim or perpetrator with a disability is at risk for I P V/D V, or the disability may be a result of I P V/D V toward the individual.
Vulnerability and powerlessness are prevalent in this population, as they often depend on their intimate partners to assist them with activities of daily living, such as providing medication, bathing, dressing, and running errands.
A vulnerable person is a person who is under the care of another person for some or even all their daily living activities. Because of being “under the care of” another, laws are in place that mandate counselors to report these incidents to authorities.
Elder Abuse
With the U.S. population living longer, I P V/D V will increasingly affect those within the aging population. Abuse of an older adult may occur within intimate relationships as well as extended families.
In addition to physical, sexual, and emotional abuse, types of older adult abuse include neglect (e.g., malnutrition, poor personal hygiene, unsafe or unclean living conditions), abandonment (e.g., desertion of an elder at a facility or public location), financial or material exploitation (e.g., sudden changes in bank account practices, unpaid bills despite availability of resources, forgery), and self-neglect.
Vulnerability and powerlessness can be common risk factors for elder abuse and individuals with disabilities.
Similar to mandated reporting laws for vulnerable persons, all states have mandated reporting laws for elder abuse.
Dating Violence among Adolescents and Young Adults
I P V/D V begins early for many—sometimes with their first dating experiences.
Dating violence includes physical, emotional, and sexual abuse and stalking.
Adolescents who do not have clear ideas about appropriate boundaries and healthy relationships may characterize abusive behaviors as acceptable or even positive characteristics of a relationship.
The C D C linked adverse childhood experiences (A C E s) to higher rates of teen dating violence.
Dating violence on college and university campuses is of particular concern.
The Counselor’s Response to Intimate Partner Violence Clients in Crisis
Screening for Intimate Partner Violence
Screening for Intimate Partner Violence
There are two absolutes that crisis counselors should follow with victims of I P V/D V.
Counselors should never ask about abuse in the presence of the client’s partner; doing so may greatly increase the risk for harm to the client. 
Counselors should be careful not to recommend ending the relationship as the only way to establish safety. 
Question Tree Screening Approach
I P V/D V is one of the areas that a Q-tree can assist, as many questions must be asked during the assessments to develop a plan.
As with all Q-trees, how a person replies to a surface question will determine needed follow-up questions.
The Q-tree approach allows counselors to determine whether a safety plan or harm reduction plan should be developed. It also allows counselors to determine whether a mandated report of abuse must be made.
Response to Intimate Partner Violence Disclosure
Immediately following an I P V/D V disclosure, it is critical for the crisis counselor to validate the victim’s experience and communicate that the victim is not to blame for the abuse.
Documentation that the client is experiencing I P V/D V is also important. It is helpful for crisis counselors or health care workers to make notes about the disclosure of I P V/D V and to take pictures of physical injuries, which could later be used in court if the victim chooses to take legal action.
Providing the victim with resources and protection is another essential component of responding to an I P V/D V crisis. Counselors should have an extensive list of I P V/D V resources available, including options for emergency shelter, transportation, food, child care, medical needs, mental health care, and legal aid.
  .
Response to Intimate Partner Violence Disclosure
The National Domestic Violence Hotline can be accessed 24 hours a day, 365 days per year at 1-800-799-S A F E or at www.thehotline.org
  .
Safety Planning and Harm Reduction Planning (1 of 2)
Safety Planning
If a client decided to leave the abuser, the counselor should develop a safety plan with the main goal of keeping the client physically safe, as well as any other vulnerable person living in the home.
The safety plan is tailored to the unique situation of each I P V/D V victim; the counselor and client should determine appropriate supports for the client, locations that provide physical safety (e.g., shelters), and whether law enforcement should be contacted.
It is important to emphasize that a safety plan in no way guarantees the safety of the I P V/D V victim.
Safety Planning and Harm Reduction Planning (2 of 2)
Harm Reduction Planning
These plans are developed for clients who do not intend to leave the abusive relationship.
The goal of the plan is to reduce the amount of harm a victim may encounter by an abuser.
Some harm reduction plans may also integrate strategies to assist in alleviating pain from abuse.
Intimate Partner Violence Shelters: Opportunities and Challenges
I P V/D V shelters provide a safe haven for many abused women and their children. Shelters not only protect victims from additional physical harm but also provide a safe place where emotional healing can begin.
While I P V/D V shelters are invaluable resources for many victims, life in a shelter is certainly not stress-free and it requires adherence to programmatic rules and regulations that may infringe on a victim’s sense of autonomy.
In addition, victims may not feel ready to address the emotional impact of their abuse yet may be required to do so as part of the shelter program.
Addressing the Emotional Impact of Intimate Partner Violence
The emotional consequences of I P V/D V can be as devastating as the physical consequences.
High rates of depression and low self-esteem are common among victims, and I P V/D V is a major predictor of drug and alcohol use in women.
Many I P V/D V victims experience symptoms of P T S D. In addition to the symptoms traditionally associated with P T S D, I P V/D V victims often experience a cluster of symptoms referred to as battered woman syndrome, which includes disrupted interpersonal relationships, difficulties with body image/somatic concerns, and sexual or intimacy problems.
When working with I P V/D V victims, it is essential to consider their trauma history in constructing a treatment plan or making a diagnosis.
COVID-19 Pandemic Impacts on I P V/D V
The increased isolation due to COVID-19 has impacted individuals in I P V/D V situations worldwide to the extent that the W H O issued statements regarding the need for a global awareness of social isolation on I P V/D V victims.
The decreased ability to seek leave from environments of abuse has been assumed to increase situations of D V.
Pre-pandemic supports such as visiting with family and friends and workplace and educational environments that often provided some form of distancing from an abuser were cut off during most of the pandemic, meaning diminished reprieves from the abuser.
Add in the stressors from the pandemic, such as economic changes, and abuse is believed to have increased.
The services that were available to individuals in I P V/D V relationships were impacted by the pandemic. Services may have been scaled back, personnel cut or limited, and shelters may have closed or limited access for safety.
Batterer Intervention (1 of 2)
I P V/D V response has two aspects: victim intervention and batterer intervention.
One model of attending to the needs of both I P V/D V victims and batterers is a coordinated community response (C C R), which involves the collaboration of local councils of service providers in developing a more comprehensive response to I P V/D V incidents.
C C R helps victims identify and access multiple available services, of which victims are often not aware. These services focus on advocacy, criminal justice, child services, health care, education, and vocation.
Batterer intervention programs have three primary goals:
Ensuring the safety of I P V/D V victims
Stopping future acts of partner violence
Increasing offenders’ accountability for their behavior.
Batterer Intervention (2 of 2)
Safety
Steps to ensuring safety include assessment of the resources that are available to I P V/D V victims, assessment of the severity and repetition of violent incidents, and assessment of the effects on children exposed to I P V/D V.
Cessation of Violence
One of the first steps toward stopping all forms of violence, including verbal, emotional, physical, and sexual abuses, is acknowledgement of one’s violent behavior.
Such an acknowledgment means discarding denial, minimizing, and blaming.
Accountability
Developing alternatives to battering is an essential component of increasing accountability.
A second task in becoming accountable is to learn to recognize triggers or cues leading to violent behavior.
Challenges to Referrals to Batterer Intervention Programs
One of the biggest challenges regarding batterer intervention is that most program participants are court mandated.
For a judge or probation officer to refer an I P V/D V offender to treatment, someone must first report an I P V/D V incident. Thus, if I P V/D V incidents go unreported, batterer intervention is nearly impossible.
The financial aspect of batterer intervention is also a challenge. Most federal and state monies for I P V/D V go to battered women’s programs.
Treatment refusal, overt or passive resistance, and premature termination (i.e., dropout) are common issues confronted by counselors in the typical batterer intervention program.
Assessing Perpetrators of Intimate Partner Violence
Assessment of batterers is important when considering the holistic impact upon victims, victim safety, and changing abusive behaviors.
Most assessment tools ask questions of victims rather than perpetrators, although both parties can be asked the same set of questions.
The more comprehensive assessment tools ask questions that seek to know more about the abusive relationship and invite discussion of how victimization patterns and relationships changed over time.
A significant but often overlooked factor that should be considered when assessing the severity of I P V/D V is stalking. Stalking is a course of conduct directed at a specific person that would cause a reasonable person to feel fear.
Screening batterers for alcohol and other drug (A O D) problems and substance use is highly recommended. Dual screening serves multiple purposes, including being more vigilant about victim safety and focusing on anger and trauma issues when working with A O D batterers.
Personal Safety
The presumption is that batterers will exert violence only toward their intimate partner. However, counselors should be aware of how they interact with perpetrators and be cognizant of personal safety.
Accusations and criticism should be kept at a minimum while balancing skills of challenging and confrontation to assist in empathy building, awareness of violence, and behavioral change.
Perpetrators should be held accountable, but they should also be allowed to work in an environment where they can safely explore their behaviors and not feel continuously criticized.
Counselors should also be able to identify when clients’ emotions become heightened to assist in calming the situation or, if that is not possible, to seek additional means of intervention.
Summary
Understanding the cycle of violence is a critical first step for crisis counselors working with I P V/D V victims.
Common crisis issues for I P V/D V victims include attending to physical injury, establishing immediate safety, and deciding whether to report the I P V/D V to the police.
Screening for I P V/D V includes gathering concrete and specific details about the client’s I P V/D V history and conducting a lethality assessment to determine the degree of urgency required in responding to the crisis.
When I P V/D V is disclosed, the counselor should validate the victim’s experience, document that it is occurring, and provide resources and continued support to the victim.
A specific component of I P V/D V response is safety planning or harm reduction planning, which is based on the victim’s decision to stay in or leave the abusive relationship.

Class 3
Chapters 7-8

Process 7:30-8
8-8:30- Dinner
8:30-10- Midterm
If this PowerPoint presentation contains mathematical equations, you may need to check that your computer has the following installed:
1) MathType Plugin
2) Math Player (free versions available)
3) NVDA Reader (free versions available)
Slides in this presentation contain hyperlinks. JAWS users should be able to get a list of links by using INSERT+F7.
Substance Use and Society
Over 50% of Americans age 12 years and older (about 132 million people) are current consumers of alcohol.
Over 21% of Americans age 12 years and older (nearly 58 million people) are current users of tobacco products.
Over 20% of Americans age 12 years and older (nearly 57 million people) reported using an illicit drugs in 2019.
Of the 20.4 million who reported needing treatment for an illicit drug or alcohol problem, only 2.3 million (11%) received treatment.
Substance Use, Crisis, and Society
There is an increased use of substances in the 2 years following terrorist events in the United States, and the rates of use were likely higher than estimated (DiMaggio et al., 2009).
Individuals who use substances are nearly six times more likely to attempt suicide than those who report no substance use problems (Dragisic et al., 2015).
Commonly Used Assessment Instruments 
Four commonly used assessment instruments are the:
Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test (AUDIT; Babor et al., 2001)
Michigan Alcoholism Screening Test (MAST; Selzer, 1971)
Substance Abuse Subtle Screening Inventory–4 (SASSI-4;Lazowski et al., 2016)
CAGE (Ewing, 1984)
Assessing SUD
Assessment for SUD usually focuses on 
Intensity
Frequency
Duration
Substance Use Disorder Diagnosis (1 of 2)
According to D S M-5-T R, at least two of the following criteria must be met for an individual to be diagnosed with a S U D:
significant problems exist at home, school, or work due to use
physical hazards occur due to repeated episodes of impaired driving or operating machinery while under the influence
substance use results in recurring legal problems (e.g., public intoxication)
tolerance increases
withdrawal occurs
increasing amounts of the substance are taken over time
usage levels are not being reduced
Substance Use Disorder Diagnosis (2 of 2)
a lot of time is spent acquiring the substance
social or work activities are sacrificed due to use or recovery from use
use continues despite interpersonal and physical effects
there is craving, a strong urge, or desire for the substance
Severity is determined by the number of presenting criteria (i.e., mild: two to three criteria; moderate: four to five criteria; severe: six or more criteria).
Assessing a Client’s Readiness to Change
Norcross et al. identified the following five stages of change:
Motivational Interviewing
engaging—establishing a connection between counselor and client that helps to build the working alliance
focusing—involves the counselor developing and maintaining a focused direction in the discussion about change
evoking—assisting the client to take ownership for their motivation for change
planning—establishing a commitment to change with a specific course of action where talk shifts from whether and why it happens to when and how to change
Treatment Admission and Placement (1 of 2)
Relapse and Crisis
During treatment, counselors need to prepare for relapse by helping clients explore triggers and learn new cognitive-behavioral strategies. Even in short-term crisis centers, these triggers can be identified, explored, and documented when clients are referred for treatment.
Counselors need to help clients differentiate when use is viewed as a “lapse” or a “relapse.”
A lapse is a single episode of using a substance following a period of abstinence. The individual often takes responsibility for this lapse and examines the specific experience or trigger(s) that resulted to build on recovery.
A relapse is a return to sustained use of the same substance or another mood-altering substance following a defined period of abstinence.
Safety and Self-Care
Counselors need to be aware of the dangers of working with clients who pose a threat to public safety or who have a history of violence, especially those who may also be under the influence of a substance.
Counselors should gauge the risk in working with clients who pose safety concerns and can take steps to minimize the risk by screening high-risk clients, avoiding seeing clients when there is a lack of physical security available, recognizing warning signs of violence, avoiding working alone during in-home counseling, and establishing rules prohibiting weapons in session.
Working with clients who have S U D s and significant symptoms creates unique challenges for counselors. Counselors who constantly face client resistance, ambivalence, motivational concerns, and relapse issues may question their effectiveness in facilitating change.
Chapter 8
Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence
Overview of Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence
Intimate partner violence (I P V) involves physical, sexual, and emotional aggression, violence, or stalking by a current or former intimate partner.
I P V is an inclusive term that is used to describe violence among opposite- or same-sex couples currently or formerly in a relationship.
I P V is only one aspect of domestic violence (D V) and, used by itself, is not inclusive of other individuals who are in violent relationships.
I P V is not the legal term used in federal or state statutes or regulations that outline mandated reporting laws for health care providers or laws that govern law enforcement practices when intervening in D V situations.
Cycle of Violence Theory
Tension-building
The explosion or acute battering incident
The honeymoon phase of kindness and contrite, loving behavior
Common Crisis Issues 
Attending to physical injury
Establishing immediate safety
Reporting IPV to the police
Risk Factors
Women of Color (54% of black WomEn, 44% of Hispanic Women, and 35% of White Women)
SES
Immigrant Status
Religion and Spiritual Beliefs
LGBTQ+
Female to Male Violence
Disability Status
Elderly Populations
Young Adults
The Counselor’s Response to Intimate Partner Violence Clients in Crisis
Screening for Intimate Partner Violence
Screening for Intimate Partner Violence
There are two absolutes that crisis counselors should follow with victims of I P V/D V.
Counselors should never ask about abuse in the presence of the client’s partner; doing so may greatly increase the risk for harm to the client. 
Counselors should be careful not to recommend ending the relationship as the only way to establish safety. 
Response to Intimate Partner Violence Disclosure
Validate the victim’s experience and communicate that the victim is not to blame for the abuse.
Documentation- make notes about the disclosure of I P V/D V and to take pictures of physical injuries, which could later be used in court if the victim chooses to take legal action.
Providing the victim with resources and protection is another essential component of responding to an I P V/D V crisis. 
  .
Response to Intimate Partner Violence Disclosure
The National Domestic Violence Hotline can be accessed 24 hours a day, 365 days per year at 1-800-799-S A F E or at www.thehotline.org
  .
Safety Planning and Harm Reduction Planning (1 of 2)
Safety Planning
If a client decided to leave the abuser, the counselor should develop a safety plan with the main goal of keeping the client physically safe, as well as any other vulnerable person living in the home.
The safety plan is tailored to the unique situation of each I P V/D V victim; the counselor and client should determine appropriate supports for the client, locations that provide physical safety (e.g., shelters), and whether law enforcement should be contacted.
It is important to emphasize that a safety plan in no way guarantees the safety of the I P V/D V victim.
Safety Planning and Harm Reduction Planning (2 of 2)
Harm Reduction Planning
These plans are developed for clients who do not intend to leave the abusive relationship.
The goal of the plan is to reduce the amount of harm a victim may encounter by an abuser.
Some harm reduction plans may also integrate strategies to assist in alleviating pain from abuse.
Intimate Partner Violence Shelters: Opportunities and Challenges
I P V/D V shelters provide a safe haven for many abused women and their children. Shelters not only protect victims from additional physical harm but also provide a safe place where emotional healing can begin.
While I P V/D V shelters are invaluable resources for many victims, life in a shelter is certainly not stress-free and it requires adherence to programmatic rules and regulations that may infringe on a victim’s sense of autonomy.
In addition, victims may not feel ready to address the emotional impact of their abuse yet may be required to do so as part of the shelter program.
Addressing the Emotional Impact of Intimate Partner Violence
Physical consequences
High rates of depression and low self-esteem 
Many IPV/DV victims experience symptoms of PTSD. 
Batterer Intervention (1 of 2)
Batterer intervention programs have three primary goals:
Ensuring the safety of I P V/D V victims
Stopping future acts of partner violence
Increasing offenders’ accountability for their behavior.
Challenges: court-mandated, finances, treatment refusal

content:
Welcome to Crisis Counseling and Abuse
I go by Dr. E or Dr. McCartney
esther.mccartney@northwestu.edu
Dr. Esther McCartney
A Little Bit About Me 
Education
M.Ed. In Clinical Mental Health Counseling with an Addictions Specialty from the College of William & Mary (2015)
Ph.D. in Counselor Education from University of South Carolina (2021)
Experience
Child & Adolescent Counselor in schools and worked with clients with safety plans
Worked as an on-call crisis stabilization counselor and worked with clients ages 5-18 and their families
Disclaimer
The Lord is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit.
Psalm 34:18
Think on your feelings about starting the new semester
Class Schedule
