Crisis Assessment, Intervention, and Prevention
Chapter 3: Ethical and Legal Considerations in Crisis Counseling
Agenda
6-6:45: Chapter 3-Ethics
6:45-7:30- Chapter 4- Review Skills
7:30-8-Dinner
8-9: Chapter 6-Suicide Assessments
9-10: practice Suicide Assessments
The illustration shows three overlapping circles. The circle on the left represents H I P P A. The college or university clinic only treats nonstudents AND is a covered entity. The circle on the center represents BOTH. The college or university clinic treats both students and nonstudents AND is a covered entity OR treats only students AND is a covered entity. The circle on the right represents F E P R A. The college or university clinic only treats students AND is not a covered entity AND the students records are education records, not treatment records. A text inside the rectangle below the circle reads, Neither the college or university clinic treats only non students AND is not a covered entity. 
Considerations to Prevent Ethical and Legal Problems
Counselors can focus on five areas to foster core prevention:
A compromised condition may leave the counselor vulnerable to ethical or legal lapses, making attention to the well-being of the counselor vital as a preventive measure.
Wellness
A compromised condition may leave the counselor vulnerable to ethical or legal lapses, making attention to the well-being of the counselor vital as a preventive measure.
Pearlman and MacIan (1995) noted 10 helpful strategies for crisis counselors to use to promote and maintain wellness:
Continuing Education
Required by certifying and licensing bodies to help counselors’ practice remain both competent and current
Counselors practicing in isolation without peer contact are at higher risk for impairment, and in-person continuing education is an important component in counselor wellness
Peer Supervision and Consultation
Counselors should prepare for crisis situations by preestablishing sources of in-person and online supervision and peer consultation.
Remote supervision requires mindfulness of power differentials and technological communication nuances. Cameron et al. (2020) found that remote supervision via videoconferencing provided increased confidence, new learning strategies, and reduced feelings of isolation for clinicians in rural areas.
Ethical Decision-Making Models
There are a variety of ethical decision-making models a counselor can use.
 Regardless of the model chosen, counselors should apply the core principles of autonomy, nonmaleficence, beneficence, justice, fidelity, and veracity when making ethical decisions.
A Practitioner’s Guide to Ethical Decision Making (Forester-Miller & Davis, 2016)
The basis for the ethical decision-making standards of the A C A Code of Ethics (2014, section I.1.b).
The practitioner’s guide outlines a seven-step decision-making model:
Examples of Other Ethical Models
Relevant Laws
Hippa, Ferpa, Duty to Warn, etc.
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (H I P A A) of 1996 is federal legislation that was implemented to address numerous perceived shortcomings in the management of information in the health care industry.
The first federal legislation to protect automated client records, and it mandated that all electronic transactions include only H I P A A-compliant codes, provide security provisions, and require national provider identification numbers.
Four areas of legislation:
privacy requirements (the specific rules for which would be subsequently constructed)
electronic transactions
security requirements
national identifier requirements
Addendums to HIPAA
Privacy Rule	
Added in 2003
A few of the provisions include:
Patients must be able to access their records.
Patients have the right to request and/or make corrections to their records.
Patients must be informed of how their personal information will be used.
Patient information may not be used for marketing purposes without prior consent.
Health insurers or providers must designate a privacy officer and train employees (Pozgar, 2018).
Security Rule
Added in 2005
The Security Rule requires that covered entities maintain three safeguards—administrative, physical, and technical—so that electronic protected health information remains confidential, secure, and integral.
How H I P A A Affects Crisis Counseling
A crisis typically occurs quickly and often in unanticipated ways, so counselors must respond immediately to stabilize the situation and provide a way for people to move forward.
According to D H H S (2022b, 2022c), H I P A A is not intended to interfere with the provision of emergency medical care associated with declared emergencies such as hurricanes, and covered health care providers may exercise their professional judgment and act if such actions are in the best interests of the patient.
Family Education Rights and Privacy Act
In 1974, Congress passed the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) prompted by concerns about privacy violations and the inclusion of immaterial comments and personal opinions in educational records.
Confusion regarding HIPAA and FERPA continues to be of concern as institutions of higher education and governmental partners struggle to strike a balance between the two federal laws.
What Is F E R P A?
How F E R P A Affects Crisis Counseling
According to the U.S. Department of Education (2020a), an emergency situation creates an exception, and schools may release information from general, medical, or mental health records to protect the health and safety of others.
In situations concerning health and safety, higher education institutions may disclose educational records to a parent if the student is involved in a health or safety issue or is under 21 years of age and has violated the school’s drug and alcohol policies.
According to the U.S. Department of Education (2021), “a school official may generally share information with parents that is based on that official’s personal knowledge or observation of the student.” Therefore, in situations where a student’s behavior involves the health and safety of self and others, school officials can communicate with parents.
The Application of H I P A A and F E R P A to College and University Student Records
The interplay between H I P A A and F E R P A has yet to be fully understood, and higher education institutions currently interpret H I P A A and F E R P A in different ways.
The Tarasoff versus Regents Decision (1 of 2)
In 1969, Prosenjit Poddar was seen by a psychologist on a voluntary outpatient basis at the University of California, Berkeley. Poddar confided his intent to kill a woman he claimed to be his girlfriend, a woman the psychologist surmised to be Tatiana Tarasoff, when she returned from Brazil.
The psychologist contacted the campus police and then began proceedings to have Poddar committed for a psychiatric evaluation.
The police questioned him and found Poddar to be rational and promised not to have contact with Tarasoff, so the police released him.
The psychologist followed up on his concerns with a letter to the campus police chief in which he again expressed his concern; however, the supervisor of the psychologist requested that the letter be returned, ordered all case notes destroyed, and ordered that no further action be taken.
The Tarasoff versus Regents Decision (2 of 2)
Poddar did not return to see the psychologist, nor was he committed or evaluated by any additional mental health professionals.
No one contacted Tarasoff or her family to alert them that a threat had been made.
Two months later, Tarasoff was stabbed to death by Poddar; her parents sued the University of California Board of Regents.
Initially, a trial court ruled that the university was not liable because Tarasoff was not the patient in this case, and therefore it had no duty of care toward her.
Upon appeal, the California Supreme Court ruled there are some circumstances in which a therapist should break confidentiality, specifically when such a disclosure is necessary to avert danger to the client or others.
This act of breaking confidentiality when disclosure is necessary to avert danger is known as the duty to warn.
What Was the Effect of Tarasoff versus Regents?
The terms duty to warn and duty to protect are often used interchangeable, adding to the confusion in the field. Definitions of each term vary by state.
The Tarasoff ruling establishes a duty to protect and a duty to warn. The ruling and interpretation of the Tarasoff principle for counseling professionals involves the awareness of the protective measure as a legal obligation to warn an intended victim of potential harm.
It is highly recommended that counselors seek peer supervision and consultation on a regular basis, but particularly whenever the issue of breaking confidentiality becomes a factor in practice. This principle is known as the duty to consult principle and can assist crisis counselors in decreasing liability issues in similar cases.
Washington Law- Duty to Warn
RCW 71.05.120
Exemptions from liability.
(1) No officer of a public or private agency, nor the superintendent, professional person in charge, his or her professional designee, or attending staff of any such agency, nor any public official performing functions necessary to the administration of this chapter, nor peace officer responsible for detaining a person pursuant to this chapter, nor any designated crisis responder, nor the state, a unit of local government, an evaluation and treatment facility, a secure withdrawal management and stabilization facility, or an approved substance use disorder treatment program shall be civilly or criminally liable for performing duties pursuant to this chapter with regard to the decision of whether to admit, discharge, release, administer antipsychotic medications, or detain a person for evaluation and treatment: PROVIDED, That such duties were performed in good faith and without gross negligence.
(2) Peace officers and their employing agencies are not liable for the referral of a person, or the failure to refer a person, to a behavioral health agency pursuant to a policy adopted pursuant to RCW 71.05.457 if such action or inaction is taken in good faith and without gross negligence.
(3) This section does not relieve a person from giving the required notices under RCW 71.05.330(2) or 71.05.340(1)(b), or the duty to warn or to take reasonable precautions to provide protection from violent behavior where the patient has communicated an actual threat of physical violence against a reasonably identifiable victim or victims. The duty to warn or to take reasonable precautions to provide protection from violent behavior is discharged if reasonable efforts are made to communicate the threat to the victim or victims and to law enforcement personnel.
How Tarasoff versus Regent Affects Crisis Counseling?
Collins and Collins’s (2005) developmental-ecological model of crisis
Intervention identifies three goals for a crisis worker dealing with a potentially violent individual:
Ensure that the client remains safe, reduce lethality, and stabilize the environment.
Help the client to regain short-term control.
Connect the client with appropriate resources.
The course of accomplishing these three goals, however, is balanced against the counselor’s decision that a duty to warn exists and confidentiality must be broken in part to accomplish those very goals.
While the principles of duty to warn and duty to protect are useful, counselors should not assume that Tarasoff applies without explicitly researching their state law. Counselors should be aware of the changing nature of legislation and of the pertinent regulations for their own state.
Harm to Others
From Chapter 6
Homicide
Homicide can be defined as the willful killing of one person by another person.
The potential for violence and homicide exists in families, schools, colleges, the workplace, and across all communities. Thus, it is essential that counselors in all practice settings be equipped to assess the lethality of clients with homicidal ideation and be capable of making decisions regarding the referral and treatment of dangerous clients.
Counselors in all settings may be called on to provide services to survivors of loss due to homicide.
Assessment of Homicide: Risk Factors
While they may assist crisis counselors in assessment, the existence of risk factors does not mean the client will become violent, and, conversely, the absence of risk factors does not indicate a lack of homicidal intention.
Individual risk factors include a history of violence victimization, attention deficit or learning disorders, history of early aggressive behavior, use of drugs, alcohol, or tobacco, and low I Q.
Family risk factors include authoritarian parents, inconsistent disciplinary practices, low parental involvement, low emotional attachment to parents or caregivers, and low education and income of parents.
Peer and social risk factors include delinquent peers, involvement in gangs, social rejection by peers, and lack of involvement in acceptable activities.
Community risk factors include diminished economic opportunities, high level of transiency, high level of family disruption, and low levels of community participation.
School Homicide and Violence
There is not a simple or obvious profile of a student who might enact a school shooting.
School shootings are precipitated by a complex interplay of personal issues and environmental circumstances.
By using systematic threat assessment and being attuned to warning signs, educational settings can more clearly identify individuals with violent intent.
There is a need to educate students, school staff and faculty, and the public about leakage, as violence in school is usually planned, and school staff and faculty need to be particularly attentive to signs and symptoms of violence as well as to school environmental factors that give rise to bullying, rejection, and the promotion of violence.
School Homicide and Violence 
Meloy et al. developed a typology of eight warning behaviors that can indicate amplified risk of targeted violence and should be used when assessing a threat:
Pathway warning behavior—the individual plans an attack
Fixation warning behavior—the individual becomes obsessed with a person or cause
Identification warning behavior—the individual identifies with attackers or warriors
Novel aggression warning behavior—the individual tries out violent behavior as a test
Energy burst behavior—the individual increases activities related to the intended victim(s)
Leakage warning behavior—the individual tells another person about the plans
Directly communicated threat warning behavior—the individual makes an explicit threat to the target or police
Last resort warning behavior—the individual displays an increase in distress and comes to believe that there is no other way forward but violence toward the target
Assessing Harm to Others
The following six questions can be used to assess a person’s potential for harming others:
Is the subject actively or passively engaged in violent or dangerous behavior?
Does the subject state that he or she is going to carry out violent or dangerous behavior?
Does the subject have a plan to follow through with this behavior?
Does the subject have the means to follow through with this plan?
Does the subject have a background of violent or dangerous behavior?
Has the subject acted on plans for violence in the past?
School Homicide and Violence 
In general, college campuses are safe environments, and there is a lower rate of crime on college campuses compared to off-campus.
Horrific shootings on college campuses are more reflective of problems with mental health access and compliance than of problems with campus safety.
access to mental health care, foster social and emotional support in residential facilities, and improve morale across group.
Workplace Violence and Homicide
The number of workplace homicides has decreased overall since 1993. Still, homicide in the workplace is the fourth leading cause of fatality at work and remains a disturbing reality.
Most workplace homicides involve robberies, and individuals who work in retail and hospitality establishments are at greater risk, as are customers and bystanders in these settings.
Approximately 80% of workplace fatalities result from shootings.
Workplaces should have clearly stated policies regarding violence and threats, a way for employees to communicate concerns, a system to promptly investigate concerns, a discipline policy, training for all employees on the signs and symptoms of violent intent, and an Employee Assistance Program that can counsel employees in distress to help prevent acts of violence.
Threat Level Assessment 
Threat Level Assessment
Low Level of Threat: A threat that poses a minimal risk to victim and public safety.
Medium Level of Threat: A threat that could be carried out, although it may not appear to be entirely realistic.
High Level of Threat: A threat that appears to pose an imminent and serious danger to the safety of others.
When dealing with a client expressing homicidal intent, crisis counselors must always keep the ethical obligation of public safety and the duty to warn at the forefront of their minds, even with low-threat clients.
Referral
Because of the danger to others and the possible existence of mental disorders in homicidal clients, counselors usually refer clients to or work collaboratively with other mental health providers, particularly psychiatrists, when treating clients who pose a threat to others.
Violent clients often need the help of specialists in forensic mental health, and it is a good idea for crisis counselors to be familiar with forensic specialists in their local area.
In instances with out-of-control and impulsive clients, homicidal and suicidal ideation may coexist and, therefore, such a client poses a threat to both self and others.
Treatment Options 
A therapeutic alliance is particularly imperative with potential homicide offenders, since client investment in treatment compliance is necessary for public safety.
Once the immediate homicidal crisis has been dealt with, the safety of others has been assured, and appropriate medical treatment has begun, long-term treatment planning can proceed.
Ongoing assessment of the client is crucial to both treatment outcomes and public safety.
Needs of Survivors of Homicide Loss
Homicide survivors may:
 Experience symptoms consistent with complicated grief due.
Need to be able to openly express what has happened to them and to fight for change in a system where they have felt injustice. 
Want to find meaning in the death of their loved one by working to benefit others experiencing similar circumstances; this gives meaning to the loss as well as purpose to the survivor.
Crisis counselors should:
Be prepared for complex and varied reactions.
Be sensitive to cultural differences in grief expression.
Special Considerations Related to Crisis
Negligence and malpractice
Documentation and record keeping
Informed consent
Confidentiality
Termination in crisis intervention
Negligence and Malpractice
Negligence and Malpractice
A breach of duty owed to another is considered negligence; negligence that occurs in a professional setting is malpractice.
Professional negligence results when a counselor departs from the usual standard of practice that is expected of professionals in a similar situation.
Malpractice is established if the court finds that a counselor has breached a professional duty, failed to take appropriate action (omission), or did something that should not have been done (commission) relative to the standard of care.
To prove malpractice, four factors (the four Ds) must be present: “a dereliction of duty that directly results in damages”
In the event of a lawsuit, a counselor’s only defense is documentation in the case file that proves that the counselor conformed to the standard of care. A means of protection is to never practice without having professional liability insurance.
chrome-extension://efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/https://www.hpso.com/getmedia/a3cd0d3c-d774-4103-8c74-8804d719b4d0/CNA_CLS_COUN24_061824_CF_PROD_ASIZE_SEC.pdf
Documentation and Record Keeping
“If it’s not written down, it didn’t happen.”
Documentation is an essential part of the standard of care of the counseling relationship.
Good documentation is particularly crucial in crisis situations such as suicide, homicide, mandated reporting (e.g., children, older adults, vulnerable persons), and disaster relief where the risk of litigation increases.
Inappropriate information should not be included in client records, meaning anything that is not treatment related and that could be problematic if viewed by others.
Crisis conditions (e.g., a disaster site) are not always conducive to writing case notes, but counselors working with clients in crisis need to clearly document exact dates, pertinent details, assessments, and treatment decisions.
Informed Consent in Crisis Settings
Confidentiality
Confidentiality is “the professional, ethical, and legal obligation of counselors that they not disclose client information revealed during counseling without the client’s written consent” (Gladding, 2017).
In crisis and disaster circumstances, provisions for confidentiality are certainly more challenging; however, the ethical standards and requirements of the profession still apply.
Counselors working with children need to explain confidentiality in developmentally appropriate language. Parents and guardians are often hypervigilant and overprotective during crisis interventions, so counselors must be clear about boundaries and limitations while still establishing rapport, a therapeutic alliance, and intervening to benefit the child.
Termination in Crisis Intervention
Appropriate termination should not only bring closure for the crisis counseling relationship but also bolster the goals and plans laid out in the crisis counseling process.
The ACA Code of Ethics (2014) prohibits abandonment;. The counselor provides the client with referral information for other appropriate mental health providers.
A final termination summary should be added to the client file. Bond and Mitchels (2014) recommend that this summary include the reason for termination, a summary of progress, the final diagnostic impression, a follow-up plan, and other pertinent information.
Spiritual Considerations
Multicultural Considerations
Crisis Intervention in a Technological World
Crises are more likely to be reported immediately to authorities and help is often obtained quickly and in some instances in real time (e.g., via Skype or Zoom).
Counselors must be aware of accurate information in real time to help clients navigate anxieties caused by false information spread by social media.
Furber et al. (2014) found that both text messaging and phone-based psychotherapy were beneficial resources for clients in crisis.
Online crisis counseling services are a valuable resource for clients in crisis who are geographically isolated, self-sequestered, or have limited access to face-to-face counseling (e.g., transportation problems, work release time).
Resources
National Hotline 988
King County-  1-866-4-CRISIS or 206-461-3222
Text START to 741741

title: Slides: Crisis Counseling-Chapter 4 Summary
content:
If this PowerPoint presentation contains mathematical equations, you may need to check that your computer has the following installed:
1) MathType Plugin
2) Math Player (free versions available)
3) NVDA Reader (free versions available)
Crisis Assessment, Intervention, and Prevention
Chapter 4: Essential Crisis Intervention Skills
Essential Crisis Intervention Microskills (1 of 2)
The difference between basic counseling skills and crisis counseling skills is less about what the counselor does or the techniques and strategies employed and more about contextual application, or where and in what situation skills are applied.
Crisis counseling tends to be more directive and can involve information giving, probing questions, and even direct or indirect confrontations.
The focus is on the immediate issue that is causing the instability for the client so the counselor must differentiate immediate issues (risk) that are the priority from longer-term issues that may be documented for postcrisis therapy.
Essential Crisis Intervention Microskills (2 of 2)
It is important to continuously be aware of and apply effective skills, since doing so is the hallmark of effective crisis counseling.
Ivey et al.’s microskills hierarchy—a set of verbal and behavioral responses that facilitate the process of counseling and alliance formation, regardless of the crisis counselor’s theoretical orientation.
The skills are presented as a hierarchy that is organized in a systematic framework.
At the bottom of the hierarchy are the basic attending skills, such as patterns of eye contact, body position, vocal tone, and silence.
A bit farther up the skills hierarchy is the basic listening sequence, which includes asking open and closed questions, using reflecting skills, and summarizing.
Attending Skills and the Solar Framework
Most meaningful communication takes place nonverbally; nonverbal attending behaviors communicate a counselor’s interest, warmth, and understanding to the client. Basic attending skills include:
Eye Contact, Body Position, Vocal Tone, and Silence
How would you use these skills?
Eye Contact
Maintaining good eye contact is how a crisis counselor conveys interest, confidence, and involvement in the client’s story.
Through eye contact, clients know a counselor is focusing on them and fully committed to the helping process.
Eye contact varies among cultures and is influenced by the intensity of emotions involved in the crisis situation.
A good guideline to follow is to maintain a moderate amount of eye contact while monitoring your client’s level of comfort and to adjust your eye contact accordingly.
Body Position
Your body position should convey to the client your interest and involvement.
Face the client and adopt an open, relaxed, and attentive body posture.
The physical distance between counselor and client should also be taken into consideration; getting too close can be overwhelming and uncomfortable, whereas being too distant can make the counselor appear aloof and may be awkward for the client.
By considering body position, you can create a safe environment for both you and your client.
Use your body posture and physical space to comfort clients and de-escalate the anxiety, anger, or agitation a client in crisis might feel.
Vocal Tone
Emotions are frequently conveyed via tone of voice.
The pitch, pacing, and volume can all influence how a client responds emotionally to a crisis counselor.
Your control and calmness may be among the greatest benefits to your client in crisis.
If your client is overly agitated, it is often helpful to speak more slowly and in a soothing tone, as this will help your client to slow things down and begin to focus.
To convey a sense of empathic understanding, it can be helpful to emphasize specific words used by your client. This technique of giving increased vocal emphasis to certain words or short phrases is called verbal underlining.
Silence
Silence is important for both clients and professional counselors. It is essential for crisis counselors to develop a comfort with silent pauses.
Clients in crisis need space to think, sort out their thoughts, and process what is happening. The counselor needs that time as well.
Silence, if used appropriately and at the right time, can convey acceptance and empathic understanding.
SOLER- a framework to show presence
The Basic Listening Sequence
The basic listening sequence represents a set of interrelated skills used to achieve three overarching goals:
To obtain an overall summary and understanding of the client’s presenting issue
To identify the key facts of the client’s situation
To identify the core emotions and feelings the client is experiencing
Counseling Skills
Skills Review
What is this skill?
How would it apply to crisis counseling?
Questions
Asking Open and Closed Questions (1 of 3)
Questioning is a primary skill that allows crisis counselors to gather important and specific information about clients.
Crisis counselors do not want counseling sessions to sound like an interrogation.
Thus, they need to be aware of how to use questions appropriately and pay close attention to the types of questions used to gather information.
Open Questions
Usually elicit fuller and more meaningful responses by encouraging the client to talk at greater length.
They typically begin with what, how, could, would, or why and are useful to help begin an interview, elaborate the client’s story, and bring out specific details.
Be careful when using why questions and “leading” questions.
Asking Open and Closed Questions (2 of 3)
Closed Questions
They can be used when crisis counselors need to obtain very specific, concrete information and get all the facts straight.
They typically either elicit a yes/no response or provide specific factual information.
Crisis counseling often requires quick and focused responses. Thus, these questions are useful because the counselor must gather specific information to aid in the prompt assessment of the problem and development of a plan of action.
Crisis counselors should still guard against the overuse of closed questions, which can cause clients to shut down and simply wait for the next question to answer.
Counselors should also avoid negative interrogatives, which are closed questions that are used in a subtle way to coerce the client into agreeing with the counselor.
Asking Open and Closed Questions (3 of 3)
Open versus Closed Questions
In crisis intervention work, crisis counselors often need to use closed questions to quickly identify and bring out specific details to seek the resolution of the crisis state.
However, they can often obtain the same information by asking open questions, so counselors should try to refrain from moving too quickly into a closed questioning approach unless they are unable to obtain the information otherwise.
Reflections
Reflecting Skills (1 of 4)
Reflecting skills are a form of active listening that conveys to the client your interest in and understanding of that which the client may be struggling.
They stimulate a deeper exploration and understanding of the problem so that the client can examine the issues more objectively.
They include paraphrasing, restating, reflecting feelings, and summarizing.
Paraphrasing
By paraphrasing, a crisis counselor mirrors back to the client, in a nonjudgmental way, an accurate understanding of the client’s communication and the implied meaning of that communication.
Paraphrasing conveys empathic understanding and facilitates the exploration and clarification of the client’s problems.
It is important for counselors to be sure the paraphrased information is accurate by checking with the client.
In crisis counseling, adding, “Is that correct” or “Did I hear you right” allows for both accuracy and for clients to gain some control over situations where they may not have held control.
Reflecting Skills (2 of 4)
Restating
In crisis counseling, the skill of restatements is intended to focus or direct the priority of the crisis and include the client’s exact words.
The counselor should understand the priority is first on risk issues followed by the issues that led to the risk.
The underlying issues would be secondary for crisis work and the focus of long-term counseling goals once the primary risk is identified and addressed.
Reflecting Feelings
Naming and identifying a client’s feelings can serve several important functions: 
It can help the client become aware of the emotions experienced in relation to the issue at hand.
It can have a positive impact on the therapeutic relationship.
When clients do not state their feelings directly, counselors may still be able to infer these feelings either from the context of a client’s communication or from the client’s nonverbal behaviors (e.g., facial expression, posture).
Reflecting Skills (3 of 4)
Summarizing
A process through which a crisis counselor can begin to put together the key themes, feelings, and issues that the client has presented.
By distilling the key issues and themes and reflecting this back to the client, counselors can begin to help clients make sense of what may have originally seemed to be an overwhelming and confusing experience.
A summary not only is used to end a session or to begin a new session by recapping the previous session but also can be used periodically throughout the session, helping to keep a focus and put together the pertinent issues at hand for the client.
Three types of summaries relevant to crisis intervention counseling: focusing summaries, signal summaries, and planning summaries.
Reflecting Skills (4 of 4)
Summarizing
Focusing summaries are often used at the beginning of the session to pull together prior information the client has given and to provide a focus to the session.
Signal summaries are used to signal to the client that you have captured the essence of the topic and that the session can move on to the next area of concern.
Planning summaries help provide closure and are used to recap the progress, plans, and any recommendations or agreements made. They are good for ending the session on a positive note and for providing a sense of direction for the client.
De-Escalating Client Emotions
There are four primary objectives when counseling agitated clients: safety, client emotion management to regain control, avoidance of physical restraint, and avoidance of escalating coercive interventions.
Richmond et al. reviewed the literature on verbal de-escalation of agitated patients and suggested the following guidelines for environment, people, and preparedness:
Physical space should be designed for safety.
Staff should be appropriate for the job.
Staff must be adequately trained.
An adequate number of trained staff must be available.
Objective scales should be used to assess agitation.
4 Types of Aggression (1 of 2)
Richmond et al. summarized four different types of aggression and suggested that each type required a different style of intervention:
Instrumental aggression—used by people who have learned that they can get their way through threats or actual violence.
If a client makes an overt threat, unspecified counteroffers should be used.
Fear-driven aggression—the client is on the defensive and may behave aggressively to protect against being hurt.
The counselor should give the client plenty of personal space and match the client’s pace and rhythm until the client is fully focusing on the content of the discussion rather than the internal state of fear.
Types of Aggression (2 of 2)
Irritable aggression based on boundary violations—usually displayed by individuals who have experienced some humiliation or interpersonal violation and are trying to put their world back into some semblance of order.
The best approach is to agree with the client that anger is a justifiable emotion, in principle, but that the resolution to the problem cannot occur while enraged.
Irritable aggression based on chronic anger—displayed by clients who are angry at the world and seem to be looking for ready excuses to “lose it.”
When interacting with an escalated, chronically aggressive client, it is essential not to appear defensive or startled, but to respond in an emotionless manner, repetitively offering choices and options for resolving or de-escalating the situation.
Handling Aggression
Reasoning with angry, out-of-control people is not likely possible. Thus, de-escalation becomes the primary goal.
De-escalation requires the counselor to remain calm, even when one is frightened and angry.
Recommendations on a practitioner’s physical stance and verbal interventions when faced with an aggressive client include:
Physical stance:
Never turn your back
Always be at the same level
Allow extra physical space
Verbal de-escalation instances:
Calmly bring the level of arousal down
Empathize with feelings but not with the behavior
Suggest alternative behaviors where appropriate
Defusing Through Reflection
When reflecting feelings, a useful way to help defuse a client in an emotional crisis state is to also reflect the client’s stated, or unstated, need or goal that is implied within the context of their narrative.
Because of the interpretive aspects, checking for accuracy is also important with this skill.
The formula for this would be: “You feel                        because                                                                                              and you want (or need)                    .”
De-escalation is about transferring calmness to the client and showing genuine interest in what the client has to say.
Be respectful, set clear limits, and trust that you have supplied the conditions that allow the client to want to respond positively.

title: Slides: Crisis Counseling-Chapter 6 Summary
content:
If this PowerPoint presentation contains mathematical equations, you may need to check that your computer has the following installed:
1) MathType Plugin
2) Math Player (free versions available)
3) NVDA Reader (free versions available)
Crisis Assessment, Intervention, and Prevention
Chapter 6: Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide
Prevalence and Rates of Suicide, Homicide, and Nonsuicidal Self-Injury
Counselors should know what the terms prevalence and rate mean to interpret how members of certain at-risk groups might require more attentive care:
Prevalence—a nominal figure that states how many lives were lost to suicide (or any other type of malady)
Rate or crude rate—a value explaining data that has been interpreted by leveling the playing field, so to speak; if all members of a population (or an age group, gender, or ethnic group) were divided into groups of 100,000, the rate would indicate how many people died per 100,000 people
Prevalence and Rates of Suicide, Homicide, and Nonsuicidal Self-Injury 
Suicide is a public health problem that all crisis counselors, regardless of setting, must learn to assess and treat because of its prevalence in society.
In the United States in 2020, suicide claimed 45,979 lives, with about one person dying from suicide every 11.1 minutes.
Suicide happens in all age groups; it’s the second leading cause of death for youth age 10–14 and adults age 25–34 years.
Women attempt suicide more often than men, at a ratio of
Men die more often than women, mainly because of more lethal means
Nonsuicidal self-injury (N S S I) behaviors are less often reported in the literature and often not the focus of a risk assessment unless it is the presenting problem.
NSSI is reported to occur among 13–17% of American adolescents and young adults.
Suicide Terminology
The phrase “committed suicide” implies a negative perspective of the person.
Language such as “died by suicide,” “suicide death,” or “took their own life” offer a more humanistic approach.
Suicide Terminology
Risk Factors, Protective Factors, and Warning Signs 
Defining
Risk Factors
Risk factors increase the likelihood of a person experiencing suicidal thoughts or behaviors.
They are often seen as the absence of something (e.g., supports, medication compliance, medical wellness, loss of loved ones) but may include underlying meaning-related factors (e.g., feelings of guilt/shame, abandonment, being a burden) that often increase risk lethality.
Many special groups have a heightened risk for suicide. These include Native Americans; members of the military or veterans; first responders; older persons; persons with life-threatening depression and/or bipolar disorder; victims of bullying; and youth who are lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, questioning, and other (LGBTQ+).
External determinants elevate the risk that members of these groups may become self-violent.
Risk factors are placed into three main categories:
Protective Factors
Protective factors make a client less likely to engage in suicidal behavior and can also promote resilience and ensure connectedness with others during difficult times, thereby making suicidal behaviors less likely. Often, the factors listed as risk factors for some clients may also be protective factors.
Protective factors include, but are not limited to:
Hopefulness
Restricted access to highly lethal means of suicide
Strong connections to family, friends, social, and community supports
Skills in problem solving, conflict resolution, handling problems in a nonviolent way, and lack of impulsivity in past situations
Ability to see alternatives, limited cognitive distortions, and less impulsivity
Cultural or religious beliefs that discourage suicide and support self-preservation
Medication compliance
Warning Signs 
Warning Signs 
Common warning signs of suicide include, but are not limited to:
Talking about wanting to die
Researching methods of dying, preparations, or rehearsal of taking one’s own life
Talking about feeling hopeless or having no purpose; self-hate statements
Talking about feeling trapped or being in unbearable pain
Talking about being a burden to others; recent hospital discharge
Increasing use of alcohol or drugs
Acute versus Chronic Suicide Risk 
Acute suicidal risk is defined as an individual who reports or presents with a desire to die in a foreseeable time frame—usually 24 hours or less—with statements about a specific plan, intent, means, and method. Require immediate professional intervention.
Chronic suicidal risk is often connected with long-term patterns of relationship difficulties, interpersonal conflicts, poor decision making in various aspects of life, and repetitive destructive patterns causing life situation difficulties.
Assessing Suicide Risk
Level of Suicidal Risk 
The gathering of information regarding risk factors, protective factors, warning signs. Most counselors assess a client’s suicidal risk on a continuum of low, moderate, or high:
No Risk-There should also be a note if there is no (or none) suicidal risk detected.
Low suicidal risk includes the presence of suicidal ideation/behaviors or infrequent thoughts without a plan and means to carry out the plan.
Moderate suicidal risk includes serious thoughts and desire to die with ideas of a plan and means, often no access, some existence of social support, but also some risk factors.
High suicidal risk includes details of a plan and means to carry out the plan, presence of risk factors, and absence of protective factors including support.
Level of Suicidal Risk 
The National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (N S P L) recommends following a rubric of assessing suicide risk based on the individual’s desire, capability to attempt, and intent to die or attempt suicide.
Suicidal desire is indicated by:
Desire to harm self or others
Psychological pain such as hopelessness, helplessness, or feeling trapped
Perceiving oneself to be a burden on others
Suicidal capability is evidenced by:
History of suicide attempts
Exposure to someone else’s death by suicide
History of or even current violent behaviors toward oneself or others
Having the means to hurt oneself or others
Current intoxication or excessive substance use
Recent dramatic mood changes or loss of orientation to reality
Extreme agitation, rage, increased anxiety, decreased sleep, and recent acts or threats of aggression
Assessing Risk
Pg. 173 and Safe-T
Case Studies
Case Studies
Main concerns with this client?
Thoughts about the counselor's approach?
What level would you rate your client? (low, moderate, or high risk) Why?
How would you gather additional information to identify impairments in functioning?
What parts of the SAFE-T model could you see applied to this client?
Level of Suicidal Risk 
Suicidal intent is reflected by:
Attempt in progress
Plan with a known method to hurt oneself or others
Preparatory behaviors (e.g., giving away possessions, finalizing one’s business affairs, saying goodbye [perhaps cryptically], counting pills, reading up on how to use the method, loading and reloading the gun, rehearsing the method) and stated intent to die
Level of Suicidal Risk 
The field has produced a variety of acronyms to assist counselors in recalling risk and protective factors and warning signs during suicide assessments.
Examples include:
I S P A T H W A R M: Ideation, Substances, Purpose, Anxiety, Trapped, Hopelessness, Withdrawal, Anger, Reckless, and Mood
S I M P L E S T E P S: S–are you Suicidal?, I—Ideation, M—Means, P— Perturbation, L—Loss, E–Earlier attempts, S–Substance abuse, T—Troubleshooting the client’s problem-solving ability, E–Emotion, P–Parents or family history/exposure, S–Stress and life events
M A N T H I S I S N’T F A I R: M—Mental issues, A—Attempts at self-destruction, N— No positive support from family/peers, T–Triggering stressors, H—Hopelessness, I—Ideas and intent, S— Substance abuse, I—Illness/chronic pain, S—Suicide in family history, N’T—(suicide) Note, F—Final arrangements/saying goodbye/giving away possessions, A—Access to means, I— Isolation, R—Recent psychiatric hospitalization
Effective Questions and Assessment Strategies for Suicide Intervention
A suicide risk assessment should always begin at the first session in traditional counseling, and it should be the main assessment goal in any crisis.
An effective suicide risk assessment relies heavily on clinical judgment and interpretation of available facts, indicators, evidence of past behaviors, rating of current mood and symptoms, and consideration of all these data against the backdrop of the field’s knowledge of risk factors, protective factors, epidemiology, and causative factors that are known to increase the risk of suicidal death.
Practice
With a partner, ask “have you ever thought about suicide?
No response needed.
Find another partner, ask “Are you having thoughts of suicide?”
Question-Tree Approach for Suicide Assessment: Essential Questions 
Using a question-tree (Q-tree) approach allows the counselor to weigh the risk and protective factors and formulate the most appropriate plan for a client experiencing suicidal thoughts or behaviors.
Some indicators may be difficult to assess by a question.
Desire to die, for example, may not, by itself, indicate an individual’s risk level. Thus, the counselor must ask further questions to assess how capable the client is to follow through on the desire.
Question-Tree Approach for Suicide Assessment: Essential Questions 
Counselors should observe a client’s:
Affective factors (e.g., nonverbals such as being withdrawn, uncooperative, distant, disinterested in taking part in a plan)
Behavioral factors (e.g., how likely a client is to act on their thoughts or follow through on a plan to stay alive)
Cognitive factors (e.g., Does a client seem rigid in their thought process or open to exploring the situation and options for life? Are thoughts distorted?)
Developmental factors (e.g., the perception of death as related to a client’s understanding of death by age, cognitive level, or developmental abilities and limitations)
Environmental factors (e.g., various cultural values important to the individual)
Pre-Suicide Dimensions
Unbearable Psychological/Emotional Distress- can involve many factors, or center around one main trauma, loss, or another psychologically activating experience. · 
Problem-Solving Impairment- takes the form of seeing only two choices: either something painfully unsatisfactory or cessation. ·
Agitation or Arousal- seems to push patients toward acting on suicide as a solution to their distress. 
Thwarted Belongingness and Perceived Burdensomeness- Suicidal thoughts and impulses are likely to increase when patients are in situations that trigger thoughts of social disconnection. 
Hopelessness- Patients with depressive symptoms may make unequivocal statements representing hopelessness. 
Suicide Desensitization- Patients may acquire, through desensitization, a numbness that reduces natural fears of pain and suicide. 
Suicide Plan or Intent- The standard approach to evaluating patients’ suicide plans is to follow the acronym, S-L-A-P (specificity, lethality, availability, and proximity of social support). ·
Lethal Means- researchers have repeatedly found that access to firearms greatly magnifies suicide risk
Q-Tree
Let’s Develop a Q-Tree of questions for a first session.
Potential Questions to Ask
Have you intentionally hurt yourself in any way?
When you hurt yourself, were you trying to commit suicide?
How old were you when you first hurt yourself?
How often do you hurt yourself?
Have you had to seek medical treatment for hurting yourself?
How do you feel after you hurt yourself?
Do you ever have the urge to hurt yourself?
Do you want to stop hurting yourself?
Do you have friends or anyone you know who hurts themselves?
Have you ever been able to stop right before you hurt yourself?
Do you use drugs or alcohol before you hurt yourself?
Is there a certain time of day that makes you feel like hurting yourself?
Is there a certain situation or place that makes you feel like hurting yourself?
Is there a certain person or people who make you feel like hurting yourself?
Do you know of anyone in your family who has hurt themselves?
Assessment
Goal/Outcome- gather information about the crisis and activate supports for client
Process-on going throughout the crisis intervention using interview skills
Relationship to treatment- help client re-establish equilibrium 
Materials needed-depends
Time involved-minimum 5 minutes until crisis is resolved
Information gathered- varies, but needs to include safety, the crisis situation, support
Training- no further education, but training in assessment needed
What is assessed? Client's level of functioning
Crisis Intervention Assessments
Safety
Suicide Lethality Assessments
Suicide lethality assessments include:
Beck Scale for Suicide Ideation (B S I)
Chronological Assessment of Suicide Events (C A S E)
Collaborative Assessment for Managing Suicide (C A M S)
Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-S S R S)
Mini-Mental Status Examination (M M S E)
Positive and Negative Suicide Ideation Inventory (P A N S I)
Suicide Assessment Five-Step Evaluation and Triage (S A F E-T)
Task Model of Crisis Assessment and Intervention
Should never be the only measure of used in suicide risk assessments
SLAP
You won’t always have an assessment with you, so you need to know what to ask.
Specificity- Are there specific steps? Or general thoughts?
Lethality- would this plan result in a suicide?
Accessibility- Do they have access to the items that they would need?
Proximity- How close are the items to the client?
Treatment Planning and Care Management with a Suicidal Client 
After assessing a client’s acute or chronic risk and determining the level of risk, many mental health professionals might again ask, “Now what do I do?”
Assessment is only part of the intervention process.
The assessment process yields valuable information to assist with the design of appropriate treatment interventions, beginning perhaps with the most important intervention of all: the development of a customized safety plan.
What comes next?
Treatment Planning and Care Management with a Suicidal Client 
Developing a safety plan with the client is an industry standard and expectation.
Stanley and Brown, considered the experts on safety planning, recommend that clients ask the following questions:
When should I use the plan?
What can I do to calm/comfort myself if I am feeling suicidal? 
What are my reasons for living?
Who can I talk to?
Who can I talk to if I need professional assistance?
How can I make my environment safe?
What should I do if I am still not feeling safe?
Treatment Planning and Care Management with a Suicidal Client 
A comprehensive safety plan addresses specific behavioral actions, determined in concert with the client.
Counselors developing safety plans should include a focus on means removal. It is best to engage a safety partner to assist with means removal to guarantee the means restriction more fully, rather than counting on the client to do so.
Safety plans should always involve resources, actions, and coping mechanisms that the client is able and willing to put into place, that are specific to the situation, and that are written with clear behaviorally specific verbiage.
No-Suicide Contract
Not considered a best practice but sometimes still utilized. 
Connection with Training Videos
How did you notice Dr. Sommers-Flannagen respond to clients? 
What interventions did he use?
Therapeutic Interactions
Validation
Don’t give false hope or simple statements (it will all be okay.”
Tell clients that their feelings are normal and typical for someone experiencing this type of situation. 
“I know that right now you feel that everything is falling apart, but many people have gone through the same situation and have survived. You have every reason to believe you can survive, too.” 
Education
Providing factual information, whether developmental or situational, is vital in every crisis. 
Clients often suffer merely because they lack or have incorrect knowledge about the precipitating event and aspects associated with it. 
Picture a woman who has been completely isolated from others because she is in an ongoing battering relationship. She will most likely perceive herself as abnormal and bizarre. When she learns that about 30 percent of women live in such relationships, she may feel differently about herself and the abnormality of the situation. Without this issue to deal with, the counselor is now free to process other issues.
Empowerment
Clients who are in certain crisis situations in which they feel violated, victimized, or helpless respond well to empowering statements. 
Clients are presented with choices and are encouraged to take back personal power by making helpful choices. Battered women, rape survivors, and survivors of child abuse often suffer from learned helplessness stemming from the abuse. 
Example: A rape victim might be told, “You did not have a choice in being raped, but now you do have a choice of what to do. You can call the police, go to counseling, tell a friend, or not do any of these things. Let us talk about your feelings and thoughts on each of these choices.”
Reframe
Defining a situation differently from the way the client is defining it. It is a cognitive-restructuring tactic that aims at changing the crisis from danger to opportunity. 
American clichés such as “Every cloud has a silver lining” and “When life gives you lemons, make lemonade” convey this idea quite clearly.
Reframing may seem like rationalizing away a problem to some. However, it is probably one of the strongest healing skills available to the crisis worker and for people in general. 
It allows us to acknowledge that life is a struggle, that we are not perfect, and that dwelling on our failings is not necessary or helpful. 
Reframing is possible only if the counselor first understands fully the client’s current frame of reference. Otherwise, the counselor would not know what should be reframed. 
Case Study
A 30-year-old woman feels hopeless and despondent. She thinks she is a bad mother and wife and cannot manage her household. She has no appetite and cannot sleep. She has little involvement with her children. She spends her days crying while the children are left to look after themselves. She has some vague thoughts that maybe it would be better if she were not around.
Reframe: A bad mother would not care if her children were not being cared for. This mother is not bad; she is just depressed.
Support: Let her know that being a mother is hard. It is not necessarily easy to manage a home and children.
Education: From what she is describing, she sounds like she is suffering from depression. Many people experience these same symptoms and there are many medications and types of counseling that are very effective in alleviating depression.
Empowerment: She is in control of how she proceeds to manage her depression. Her first sign of being in control was coming to this appointment.
Outpatient and Inpatient Treatment Decisions and Management
When a client is in immediate risk to self, the decision for hospitalization may be required to keep the person safe.
States and counties within states differ as to the process for involuntary and voluntary hospitalizations, and it is essential that clinicians understand the laws and policies in the state and county where their clients live.
Voluntary hospitalization is preferred over involuntary hospitalization as it allows the client (and supports) to be part of the decision-making process. In many states, when a person voluntarily seeks hospitalization, they may have more control in their discharge.
When clients and families agree to voluntary hospitalization, the crisis counselor should obtain a release of information and gain as much information as to where the client will be seeking admission so they can follow up.
A well-being check can be requested if there is any thought the client (or guardians) did not follow through. Law enforcement conducts the well-being check.
Outpatient and Inpatient Treatment Decisions and Management 
Involuntary hospitalization differs from state to state.
Some states require courts to mandate the commitment of an individual; others allow licensed professionals to initiate an emergency petition for involuntary hospitalization; some states integrate mobile crisis teams or crisis intervention teams, while others rely on a community service board that would make the final assessment and determination based on the clinicians’ observations.
Once a client is involuntarily hospitalized, the client will likely not have further contact with the crisis counselor, and clients will be discharged to outpatient mental health counselors
Outpatient and Inpatient Treatment Decisions and Management 
Partial hospitalizations are often referred to as a step down from a hospitalization when a person may need additional or more intense support than outpatient mental health.
Medical stabilization and pharmacological intervention are the goals of hospital mental health administrators, and discharge planning begins at admission.
Discharge planning and collaboration is essential between hospital personnel and outpatient counselors to eradicate the high window of risk for suicide after discharge.
The follow-up with a client who is at risk for suicide is critical practice; follow-up is a form of active postvention that is postventive, preventive, and interventive.
Follow-ups may include regularly scheduled phone calls, letters, or notes, and even texting or e-mailing, or they may include memoranda of agreements with provider organizations (e.g., between agencies or practices and hospital E D s).
Outpatient and Inpatient Treatment Decisions and Management 
Documentation of lethality
It is important that the counselor notes what a client reports and how they present.
Not asking does not mean a client is not suicidal. It means the standard of care was not met sufficiently.
A common risk statement should show that a verbal assessment was made as well as an observation of nonverbal behaviors.
This statement implies that the counselor has asked the appropriate questions and the client has been evaluated based on their behavioral presentation.
This documentation should also be extended to N S S I and homicide risk assessments.
Postvention 
Postvention has grown to include the care practices that practitioners provide to persons who have survived a suicide attempt.
Postvention with attempt survivors can include ongoing stabilization, long-term therapy, and improvement of help-seeking skills and overall self-care, with a goal of long-term improvements in quality of life and functioning.
Postvention has also been the type of care provided to survivors of suicide loss.
Survivors of suicide or homicide loss can develop protracted symptoms of grief, depression, anxiety, PTSD, longer and more complicated grief and bereavement; more demands on medical and mental health services; and greater potential for lost time at work and at school.
Survivors of suicide loss also have an elevated risk for suicide attempts.
Postvention 
Whether working with an individual or with a group, there are common factors to take into consideration that may be unique to those grieving the loss due to suicide:
(1) prevention of further suicides, 
(2) reconstructing the “why,” 
(3) commemorating the positive, 
(4) saying goodbye, and 
(5) an assessment of guilt/shame and absolution of guilt.
NSSI
Nonsuicidal Self-Injury (N S S I)
NSSI is the intentional, self-inflicted, immediate destruction of body tissue, without suicidal intent, that is not viewed as culturally acceptable behavior.
The severity of N S S I can range from superficial wounds to long-term disfigurement.
The most common N S S I behavior continues to be reported as cutting; other common behaviors or methods of N S S I include, but are not limited to:
Burning
Skin picking
Hair pulling
Banging or hitting the head or other body parts
Rubbing objects onto the skin
Skin erasing or carving
Nonsuicidal Self-Injury (NSSI)
NSSI increases substantially in early adolescence and decreases during late adolescence and early adulthood with more incidents of NSSI behaviors by females.
The likelihood that counselors and medical professionals will encounter NSSI is quite high.
NSSI has been viewed as not linked with suicidal intent, but most clinicians and researchers suggest that NSSI is a strong predictor of future suicidal behavior.
Assessments and Q-Tree 
It is a standard of care to integrate an N S S I assessment as part of all suicide risk assessments.
It is essential to understand the intentions of those who participate in N S S I to properly assess intent, implement the most effective treatment strategy, or appropriately refer to additional care.
Kruzan and Whitlock adapted the transtheoretical model (T T M) of behavior change proposed by Prochaska and DiClemente to assess motivation for change.
T T M integrates the following while assessing motivation of change: (1) precontemplation (with no intent to stop N S S I); (2) contemplation (intent to stop N S S I within 6 months); (3) preparation (intention of taking steps to stop N S S I within next month); (4) action (currently taking steps to stop N S S I); (5) maintenance (steps have been taken for more than 6 months); and (6) termination (cessation of N S S I for over 3 years).
Assessments and Q-Tree (2 of 2)Crisis counselors can integrate a Q-tree into the T T M to assess and identify a timeline of N S S I, risk factors, and motivation for change (protective factors) to provide referral for treatment.According to Wester and Trepal, the N S S I assessment should gather: (1) basic information, (2) contextual information, and (3) functional/reasoning information.N S S I assessment often requires long-term care, which may exceed the time a true crisis counselor can offer. 
Interventions for NSSI
Ineffective Interventions for Self-Harm
Summary
Suicide, NSSI, and homicide are crises that counselors will encounter during their careers.
No single universally recognized rubric for predictability of suicide, NSSI, or homicidal behaviors exists; however, it is a standard of care to approach these risk assessments with models for assessment to provide effective intervention.
Three primary goals to help crisis counselors dealing with a person threatening suicide or homicide are 
(1) ensure client safety, 
(2) assist the client in achieving immediate short-term mastery of self and situation, and 
(3) connect the client with formal and informal supports.
Practice
Suicide Risk Assessments
Practice
Practice conducting a suicide assessment and intervention. The person role playing the client should act as one of the clients presented in the training videos.
Use the SAFE-T model to identify the precipitating event, cognitions, emotional distress, and impairments in functioning.
 The person who is role-playing the client should be creative and should embellish the situation based on knowledge of the emotional and cognitive elements that were presented in the training videos.
The practicing counselor should use the suggested therapeutic comments included at the end of the case description. (reframe, support, education, or empowerment)
Feel free to be creative and create scenarios that include low-, middle-, and high-risk clients. 
Once you decide at which risk level the client is, discuss appropriate interventions. Any of these cases could be made into low, middle or high risk depending on the responses the client gives to the counselor’s questions.
Case Studies

about Event

and/or provide case study examples

presentation.

title: Slides: SAFET-Suicide Assessment Flyer
content:
Substance Abuse and Mental Health
Services Administration
SUICIDE ASSESSMENT
SAFE-T
Five-StepEVALUATION AND TRIAGE
1 IDENTIFY RISK FACTORS
Note those that can be modified
to reduce risk
2 IDENTIFY PROTECTIVE
FACTORS
Note those that can be enhanced
3 CONDUCT SUICIDE INQUIRY
Suicidal thoughts, plans,
behavior, method, and intent
4 DETERMINE RISK LEVEL &
INTERVENTION
Determine risk. Choose
appropriate intervention
to address and reduce risk.
5 DOCUMENT
Assessment of risk, rationale,
intervention, and follow-up
Suicide assessments should be
conducted (1) at first contact; (2) with
any subsequent suicidal behavior,
increased ideation, or pertinent
clinical change; (3) prior to changes
in behavioral health treatment; and
(4) at inpatient discharge.
RISK FACTORS	1. 1
• 	Prior suicide attempt(s)
• 	Alcohol or substance use
• 	History of mental health concerns, particularly
depression and other mood disorders
• 	Access to lethal means, including firearms
• 	Knowing someone who died by suicide, particularly
a family member
• 	Social isolation
• 	Chronic disease and/or disability
• 	Lack of access to behavioral health care
• 	Prolonged feelings of hopelessness
POPULATIONS AT INCREASED RISK FOR SUICIDE
• 	American Indian,
Alaska Native, and
Tribal communities
• 	Black youth
• 	Rural communities
• 	LGBTQI + youth and
young adults
• 	Middle-aged men
• 	Older adults
2. PROTECTIVE FACTORS1
Note: Protective factors, even if present,
may not counteract significant acute risk
• 	Connectedness to people, family, community, and
social supports
• 	Effective behavioral health care
• 	Life skills (including problem-solving skills, coping
skills, emotional regulation, ability to adapt to change)
• 	Self-esteem and a sense of purpose or meaning in life
• 	Cultural, religious, or personal beliefs that discourage
suicide
• 	No access to lethal means
1https://sprc.org/risk-and-protective-factors

-- 1 of 2 --

3. SUICIDE INQUIRY
Specific questioning about thoughts, plans, behaviors, intent
• 	Ideation: Frequency, intensity, duration—in last 48 hours, past month, and worst ever
• 	Plan: Timing, location, lethality, availability, preparatory acts
• 	Behaviors: Past attempts, aborted attempts, rehearsals versus non-suicidal self-injurious actions
• 	Intent: Extent to which the patient (1) expects to carry out the suicide plan and (2) believes the plan/act
to be lethal. Explore ambivalence: reasons to die versus reasons to live
For Youth: Ask parent/guardian about history of suicidal thoughts, plans, or behaviors, and changes in mood,
behaviors, or disposition.
4. RISK LEVEL/INTERVENTION
• 	Assessment of risk level is based on clinical judgment, after completing steps 1–3
• 	Reassess as patient or environmental circumstances change
• 	Develop a safety plan for all individuals at low, moderate, and high risk levels
RISK LEVEL 	RISK/PROTECTIVE FACTOR 	SUICIDALITY 	POSSIBLE INTERVENTIONS
HIGH 	Individuals experiencing severe
behavioral health symptoms or acute
precipitating event; protective factors
not relevant
Suicidal ideation with plan
(when and where), method
(how), and intent to carry
out the suicide plan
Emergency psychiatric treatment in a
secure setting may be necessary unless
a significant change reduces risk
MODERATE 	Multiple risk factors, experiences
some elevated behavioral health
symptoms, and few protective
factors
Suicidal ideation with plan,
but no intent or behavior
Admission may be necessary
depending on risk factors. Give
emergency/crisis numbers to
include the 988 Lifeline
LOW 	Manageable risk factors, strong
protective factors
Thoughts of death, no plan,
intent, or behavior
Outpatient referral with a warm handoff,
symptom reduction. Give emergency/
crisis numbers, 988 Lifeline
Note: This chart is intended to serve as an example of a range of risk levels and interventions, not actual determinations.
5. DOCUMENT
Document: Risk level and rationale; treatment plan to address/reduce current risk (e.g., safety plan,
medication, psychotherapy, contact with significant others, consultation, etc.); counseling on access to lethal
means; follow-up plan. Patients/clients should receive a copy of their safety plan. For youth, the treatment
plan should include roles for parent/guardian/supportive adult.
SUICIDE PREVENTION RESOURCES
988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, call or text 988 or chat at 988lifeline.org for 24/7 support
samhsa.gov/find-help/988
• 	Download this and additional
resources at store.samhsa.gov
• 	SAMHSA’s Suicide Prevention webpage:
samhsa.gov/mental-health/suicide
• 	The 2024 National Strategy for Suicide Prevention
and Federal Action Plan:
hhs.gov/programs/prevention-and-wellness/
mental-health-substance-abuse/national-strategy-
suicide-prevention/index.html
• 	Suicide Prevention Resource Center:
https://sprc.org
• 	National Action Alliance for Suicide Prevention:
https://theactionalliance.org
• 	American Foundation for Suicide Prevention:
https://afsp.org
• 	988 End Cards for Media: samhsa.gov/find-
help/988/partner-toolkit/end-cards-media

Screening for Mental Health, Inc., and the Suicide Prevention Resource Center. This material is based upon work supported by the
Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) under Grant No. 1U79SM57392. Any opinions, findings,
conclusions, and recommendations expressed in this material are those of the author and do not necessarily reflect the views of SAMHSA.
