-- 196 of 545 --

168 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
meaning-related factors (e.g., feelings of guilt/shame, abandonment, being a burden)
that often increase risk lethality.
Many special groups have a heightened risk for suicide. These include Native
Americans; members of the military or veterans; first responders; older persons; persons
with life-threatening depression and/or bipolar disorder; victims of bullying; and youth
who are lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, questioning, and other (LGBTQ+). For these
groups, suicide death rates are higher than average for the population. It is important to
note that a certain group does not have traits that make them more suicidal; rather, other
external determinants elevate the risk that members of some groups may become self-
violent. These factors may include marginalization, trauma events or complex trauma,
poverty, and exposure to self-violence or violence, among other factors.
Although agencies and clinicians may have different ways to inquire about risk
factors, for the following discussion, risk factors are placed into three main categories:
health factors, environmental factors, and historical factors. These categories may have
some factors in common.
HEALTH FACTORS 	Health factors include medical or mental health concerns. Often,
physical illnesses such as a terminal disease may also affect a person emotionally and
existen

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 53/154)
content:
ntially. Thus, such illnesses may become complex risk factors. For example, an
individual with cancer may not be able to work, may become dependent on others, and
this may affect their family (e.g., financially or via role changes). In addition, the
individual may perceive they have become a burden to family members for their care,
which may be a significant change from their previous role of taking care of others.
Therefore, it is important that counselors understand not only the surface risk factor
(medical diagnosis) but also other factors that may complicate the risk (e.g., financial,
job status, perception of being a burden on others, independence).
Some mental health concerns may also impact the risk for an individual. Often
when the client is not able to think through rational situations or is impulsive, a higher
risk of acting on suicidal thoughts exists. Mood disorders, schizophrenia, anxiety
disorders, substance use, and certain personality disorders may also present a higher
risk for suicidal behaviors.
ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS 	Environmental factors can include cultural, behavioral,
and personality issues. Cultural issues include, but are not limited to, those relating to
race/ethnicity, gender identity, socioeconomic status, political affiliation, religious/
spiritual affiliation, sexual orientation/identity, age, and sex. Other environmental
factors may include, but are not limited to:
• Hopelessness
• Impulsive or aggressive tendencies
• Family history of suicide
• Recent job or financial loss
• Recent loss of relationship
• Perceived rejection associated with personal meaning
• Access to lethal means
• Lack of social support and sense of isolation
• Stigma associated with asking for help
• Lack of health care, especially mental health and substance use disorder treatment

-- 197 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	169
• Cultural and religious beliefs, such as the belief that suicide is a noble resolution
of a personal dilemma
• Exposure to others who have died by suicide (in real life or via the media)
• Ability to be a part of one’s personal rescue plan from past suicide thoughts/
behaviors
HISTORICAL FACTORS 	Historical factors are unique to the client’s life and include, but
are not limited to, history of trauma or abuse, abandonment of social supports, family-
of-origin risk factors, previous suicide attempts, family history of suicide, or humiliating
past events that are still causing pain for the client.
Protective Factors
Protective factors make a client less likely to engage in suicidal behavior. Protective
factors can also promote resilience and ensure connectedness with others during diffi-
cult times, thereby making suicidal behaviors less likely. Often, the factors listed as risk
factors for some clients may also be protective factors. For example, a client’s religious
beliefs may be the reason the client is not following through on the plan to die by suicide.
Consider this statement made by a client, “I have not followed through because I do
not believe I would be joined with my other family members in heaven.” A counselor
would not say, “Yes, according to your beliefs, you may not go to heaven.” Rather, a
counselor may respond, “It sounds like being with your family after death is important
to you. Tell me more about your religious/spiritual beliefs and the importance of being
reunited with loved ones.”
Another protective factor may be a single mother who says, “I have
not acted on my suicidal thoughts because I would hate for the father of our
children, who has been abusive to me, to be given custody and abuse the
kids. I cannot do that to my children.” Her desire to make sure her children
are cared for is a protective factor. A counselor would not reply with, “Yes,
that would be quite selfish to have your children experience the abuse you
did because you decided to kill yourself.” Rather, a response to support
the protective factor may be, “It sounds like you do not want your children
to experience the same abuse from their father that you did, and you are
concerned that if you took your own life, they would not be cared for the
way you want to take care of them. Tell me more about your children and
what you want for them.”
Other protective factors include, but are not limited to:
• Hopefulness
• Restricted access to highly lethal means of suicide
• Strong connections to family, friends, social, and community supports
• Skills in problem solving, conflict resolution, handling problems in a nonviolent
way, and lack of impulsivity in past situations
• Ability to see alternatives, limited cognitive distortions, and less impulsivity
• Cultural or religious beliefs that discourage suicide and support self-preservation
• Medication compliance
• History of ability to solve problems and create solutions
• History of demonstrating skills for handling emotional crises

• Expression of concrete and detailed future plans
• Family commitments, such as raising children or caring for siblings
• Willingness to sign a no-harm contract
• Being resilient in past situations
• Having a sense of purpose or meaning
Warning Signs
Warning signs may be present prior to death by suicide but not recalled until after the
fact. Survivors may experience guilt in not intervening in the suicide, which may cause
more complicated grief issues. Other clients may not present with warning signs at all.
Often, individuals who do not present with warning signs are those who truly want to
die and do not want any intervention. In addition, some warning signs may be normal
behavior for the person who died and therefore may not be seen as a warning sign by
family, friends, and counselors.
Some warning signs may fall into risk factors. Aside from assessment of risk,
counselors should be aware of various warning signs that a client may present
and provide psychoeducation and information to family members and friends (as
appropriate), such as including possible things to look for when they know their loved
one has indicated they are experiencing thoughts of suicide. The following are common
warning signs of suicide that may lead to acute suicidal thoughts or behaviors requiring
immediate intervention (Rudd et al., 2006).
• Talking about wanting to die
• Researching methods of dying, preparations, or rehearsal of taking one’s own life
• Talking about feeling hopeless or having no purpose; self-hate statements
• Talking about feeling trapped or being in unbearable pain
• Talking about being a burden to others; recent hospital discharge
• Increasing use of alcohol or drugs
• Acting anxious, agitated, impulsive, or reckless, especially if this is not the norm
• Sleeping too little or too much
• Withdrawing or feeling isolated
• Showing rage or talking about seeking revenge
• Displaying extreme mood swings
• Cognitive, behavioral, or mood changes
ACUTE VERSUS CHRONIC SUICIDE RISK
Learning Objective 6.3 Distinguish between acute and chronic suicidal risk.
Acute suicidal risk is defined as an individual who reports or presents with a desire
to die in a foreseeable time frame—usually 24 hours or less—with statements about a
specific plan, intent, means, and method (Kleespies, 2000). Some counselors may allow
for a longer time period, but there is still a specific time frame. For example, a client
who has been experiencing chronic and complex grief may state, “I just cannot get over
my mother’s death. On Mother’s Day this year, I plan to end it all so I can be with my
mother.” It would still be appropriate to consider this as an acute risk situation and to
take appropriate measures to start the preventative suicidal assessment and intervention.

-- 199 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	171
Acute crisis refers to situations that require immediate professional intervention.
For example, Jobes (2016) concluded there is a strong relationship between acute risk
and acute crisis events, such as personal failures that may impact core meaning or
situations that are perceived as humiliating to the client. Any recent events need to be
assessed to determine whether the client perceives the events as humiliating. These
acute crises alone may increase the suicidal risk and lethality to a high level, resulting
in immediate and imminent risk. Examples include demotions at work, a relationship
breakup, legal situations, discovering a betrayal, foreclosure or encountering adverse
financial situations, educational failure, and medical issues that may be perceived to
cause a financial burden on family members. Often, a humiliating event can cause an
individual to not think clearly and, combined with impulsive personalities, can trigger
suicidal behaviors.
Chronic suicidal risk is often connected with long-term patterns of relationship
difficulties, interpersonal conflicts, poor decision making in various aspects of life,
and repetitive destructive patterns causing life situation difficulties. These chronic
risk situations may be associated with chronic suicidal thoughts and possibly chronic
behaviors, but individuals may have less specific plans and less lethal intent or means.
Thoughts of suicide may last over long periods of time and may not appear as imminent
or immediate. Chronic risk must be assessed for the possible emergence of active or
acute risk.
Individuals with chronic risk can be described as having “markers” of a potential
suicide risk that is not currently imminent. In other words, individuals may present with
some identifiers of persons who could become acutely suicidal but are not currently in
imminent danger. Just as with other medical diagnoses, the client in your office with a
family history of cancer (a risk factor) may not ever be stricken with cancer. A person
who is chronically in suicide risk may thus have markers or risk factors for potential
suicide, but there is a chance the person will never attempt suicide. Chronic risk should
be observed closely for the possible emergence of active or acute risk. A few of these
chronic markers might include the following:
• Being from a high-risk suicidal group
• Diagnoses including but not limited to mood disorders (depression or bipolar
disorder), anxiety, eating disorder, thought disorder, substance use disorder or
some personality disorders
• History of trauma, abuse, violence, self-injury, previous hospitalizations, low self-
esteem, high self-hate, tolerance and acceptance toward suicide, oppression related
to sexual orientation, perfectionism, divorced parents, family history of suicide
attempts or fatalities, and other factors
In summary, chronic risk suggests the presence of longer-term potential of
suicidality, but with less specificity of intent or plans (e.g., means or methods) to carry out
an imminent attempt. The counselor has more time to help the client mitigate, manage,
or resolve psychological pain (and the need to end the pain), can be less directive,
and may not resort to immediate protection such as hospitalization as a treatment
strategy. The counselor will instead reassess suicidality regularly, manage safety, and
attempt to stabilize long-term risk with effective counseling. Table 6.2 provides further
differentiation between the acute and chronic risk categories.

LEVEL OF SUICIDAL RISK
Learning Objective 6.4 Describe how counselors assess a client’s suicidal risk as no,
low, moderate, or high.
The gathering of information regarding risk factors,

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 54/154)
content:
protective factors, warning signs,
and acute versus chronic risk will likely render a decision connected with lethality. Most
counselors assess a client’s suicidal risk on a continuum of low, moderate, or high:
• Low suicidal risk includes the presence of suicidal ideation/behaviors or infrequent
thoughts without a plan and means to carry out the plan.
• Moderate suicidal risk includes serious thoughts and desire to die with ideas of a
plan and means, often no access, some existence of social support, but also some
risk factors.
• High suicidal risk includes details of a plan and means to carry out the plan,
presence of risk factors, and absence of protective factors including support.
Although many scales indicate low, moderate, and high, there should also be a note if
there is no (or none) suicidal risk detected. Indicating “no or none” is a best practice
and allows you and other referring counselors to have a better comparison of suicidal
thoughts and behaviors over time.

experiencing suicidal thoughts. Law enforcement “hostage negotiators” also use
variations of this guide for crisis response teams.
In addition, the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (NSPL; 2007) recommends
following a rubric of assessing suicide risk based on the individual’s desire, capability
to attempt, and intent to die or attempt suicide.
Suicidal desire is indicated by:
• Desire to harm self or others
• Psychological pain such as hopelessness, helplessness, or feeling trapped
• Perceiving oneself to be a burden on others
Suicidal capability is evidenced by:
• History of suicide attempts
• Exposure to someone else’s death by suicide
• History of or even current violent behaviors toward oneself or others
• Having the means to hurt oneself or others
• Current intoxication or excessive substance use
• Recent dramatic mood changes or loss of orientation to reality
• Extreme agitation, rage, increased anxiety, decreased sleep, and recent acts or
threats of aggression
Suicidal intent is reflected by
• Attempt in progress
• Plan with a known method to hurt oneself or others
• Preparatory behaviors (e.g., giving away possessions, finalizing one’s business
affairs, saying goodbye [perhaps cryptically], counting pills, reading up on how to
use the method, loading and reloading the gun, rehearsing the method) and stated
intent to die

suicidal cases.

The field has produced a variety of acronyms to assist counselors in recalling risk
and protective factors and warning signs during suicide assessments. These should not
serve to replace a formal model and Q-tree (which we discuss next) but may be helpful in
gathering information on risk and prote

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 55/154)
content:
ective factors. The following is not an exhaustive
list but provides examples:
• IS PATH WARM (Juhnke et al., 2003): Ideation, Substances, Purpose, Anxiety,
Trapped, Hopelessness, Withdrawal, Anger, Reckless, and Mood
• SIMPLE STEPS (McGlothlin, 2008): S—Are you Suicidal?, I—Ideation, M—
Means, P— Perturbation, L—Loss, E—Earlier attempts, S—Substance abuse,
T—Troubleshooting the client’s problem-solving ability, E—Emotion (hopeless,
hapless, worthless, loneliness, depression), P—Parents or family history/exposure,
S—Stress and life events
• MAN THIS ISN’T FAIR (Sankaranarayanan, 2007): M—Mental issues (depression,
anxiety, agitation, guilt, shame, delusions), A—Attempts at self-destruction, N—
No positive support from family/peers, T—Triggering stressors, H—Hopelessness,
I—Ideas and intent, S—Substance abuse, I—Illness/chronic pain, S—Suicide in
family history, N’T—(suicide) Note, F—Final arrangements/saying goodbye/
giving away possessions, A—Access to means, I— Isolation, R—Recent psychiatric
hospitalization
The above is only a guide to the factors that may affect risk and lethality.
There is no set number of indicators someone may have to determine lethality,
and a single indicator may not constitute higher risk. In other situations, an
individual may have many indicators but may not be high in lethality. In
still others, a few indicators together may be essential enough to qualify for
high risk. For example, if a client says they have a plan and means, have no
hope, and will follow through on their plan without seeking intervention,
then the individual may certainly have a high suicidal risk. There are many
assessments in the field that can be used to quantify the variables connected
with suicide.
EFFECTIVE QUESTIONS AND ASSESSMENT STRATEGIES
FOR SUICIDE INTERVENTION
Learning Objective 6.5 Describe effective questions and assessment strategies for
suicide intervention.
A suicide risk assessment should always begin at the first session in counseling, and it
should be the main assessment goal in any crisis. The counseling field has a plethora
of assessments and models for assessing suicide risk. A standard of care in our
profession is to not only provide a risk assessment of every person, regardless of the
client’s presenting problem, but also have a framework (model) upon which to base
the assessment. Although the profession does not dictate a specific suicide assessment
to use, it is a standard of care to use effective risk assessment to practice ethically and
reduce negligence.

178 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
An effective suicide risk assessment relies heavily on clinical judgment and
interpretation of available facts, indicators, evidence of past behaviors, rating of current
mood and symptoms, and consideration of all these data against the backdrop of the
field’s knowledge of risk factors, protective factors, epidemiology, and causative factors
that are known to increase the risk of suicidal death. Regardless of the model used, there
are a set of questions that should be integrated to weigh the risk and protective factors
and make a sound clinical impression of lethality to provide a treatment plan.
Question-Tree Approach for Suicide Assessment: Essential Questions
Regardless of the assessment used, certain questions should be part of the suicide
assessment. Depending on the answers, follow-up questions should be asked for a
comprehensive assessment. If a counselor only stays with a surface question and does not
follow up with additional questions, the assessment is not complete and the treatment
plan may be ineffective or inaccurate, leading the counselor to an incorrect lethality
level. Using a question-tree (Q-tree) approach allows the counselor to weigh the risk
and protective factors and formulate the most appropriate plan for a client experiencing
suicidal thoughts or behaviors (see Figure 6.1).
Notice that the risk and protective factors are included in the Q-tree. In addition,
some indicators may be difficult to assess by a question. Desire to die may not, by itself,
indicate an individual’s risk level. Rather, the counselor must assess how capable the
client is to follow through on the desire. Capability is defined by a sense of fearlessness
and impulsivity to follow through on a specific plan and the means available to follow
through on the plan to die or attempt suicide. Neither desire nor capability
predict active behaviors toward seeking death as strongly as intent (Kalafat
et al., 2005). Strong intent to die may lead to reduced motivation or reasons
for living and can result in a more lethal method selection.
The recommendations issued by the NSPL (2007) include exploring
buffers, which are like protective factors to lessen suicidal risk. These may
include (1) immediate supports, (2) social supports, (3) hopefulness and
future plans, (4) engaging in the therapeutic process, (5) desire to live, (6)
core values and beliefs that conflict with suicide, and (7) a sense of meaning/
purpose.
TRIAGE APPROACH 	While using any suicide assessment or Q-tree,
counselors must also be observant and use the TRIAGE approach.
Counselors should observe a client’s affective factors (e.g., nonverbals such as
being withdrawn, uncooperative, distant, disinterested in taking part in a
plan), behavioral factors (e.g., how likely a client is to act on their thoughts or
follow through on a plan to stay alive), and cognitive factors (e.g., Does a
client seem rigid in their thought process or open to exploring the situation
and options for life? Are thoughts distorted?). In addition, developmental
factors (e.g., the perception of death as related to a client’s understanding of
death by age, cognitive level, or developmental abilities and limitations) and
environmental factors (e.g., various cultural values important to the individual)
should be gathered and documented. See Chapter 1 for more details.

180 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
Suicide Lethality Assessments
Many assessments are available in the field and used as frameworks by crisis counselors
in lethality assessment. It is a standard of care to integrate at least one model into your
practice to provide documentation for each client. Regardless of the model selected, the
Q-tree discussed above should be integrated into any of these models. Some models will
indicate a section within the model for gathering risk and protective factors, although the
Q-tree or essential questions are not written into the model. The following assessments
provide a sampling from the field.
BECK SCALE FOR SUICIDE IDEATION 	The Beck Scale for Suicide Ideation (BSI) is a self-
report instrument requiring 10 minutes to administer that evaluates a client’s thoughts,
plans, and intentions to die by suicide. The 21 items are administered by the counselor
using a rating scale that includes items such as “I have made plans to die by suicide”
and “I have access to lethal means.” The instrument requires specific, detailed recall of
suicidal experiences, such as “I was XX% sure I would die by suicide” and “I have only
felt suicidal (number of) times.”
CHRONOLOGICAL ASSESSMENT OF SUICIDE EVENTS 	The Chronological Assessment of
Suicide Events (CASE) model was developed by a former emergency department (ED)
doctor who observed firsthand the high prevalence of suicide risk and suggested there
was a window of opportunity where professionals could intervene when a client was
discharged from psychiatric admission to the ED or psychiatric hospitalization. His
concern was with the high demands on ED medical professionals and being able to bet-
ter assess suicidal risk. The CASE method focused on the immediate factors of the cli-
ent’s suicidal risk—the conditions immediately prior to the psychiatric emergency (6–8
weeks buildup or a pileup of distress), the influences prior to 6–8 weeks of buildup
(e.g., long-ago exposure to suicide, family history of suicide, traumatic events)—and
concludes with questions about the “now” of suicidal feelings and pending days ahead.
The approach emphasizes five sets of question-asking:
1. Shame attenuation (i.e., normalizing feelings of psychological suffering and the
immediate issues the client has experienced)
2. Behavioral incident specificity (e.g., “Where is the weapon?”, “And then what did
you do?”, “And after that, what did you do?”, “Who knows about your plans?”)
3. Gentle assumption (e.g., “How have you thought of killing yourself?” rather than
“Have you ever thought about killing yourself?”)
4. Symptom amplification (e.g., “How much time do you spend thinking about
suicide in a 24-hour period?”)
5. Denial of the specific (e.g., asking clients if they have considered other methods—
this will not give clients the idea of having more options)
COLLABORATIVE ASSESSMENT FOR MANAGING SUICIDE 	Jobes (2016) developed an
assessment that combines the interview with the written instrument tool. Collaborative
Assessment for Managing Suicide (CAMS) is evidence and theory based and requires
close collaboration with the client to understand the details of the suicidal risk. CAMS
integrates safety and treatment planning and has been integrated into a 4-hour online
training. It can be found at http://www.empathosresources.com (Empathos, 2015).

-- 209 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	181
COLUMBIA-SUICIDE SEVERITY RATING SCALE (C-SSRS) 	The Columbia-Suicide Severity
Rating Scale (C-SSRS; Posner et al., 2011) includes detailed categories of suicide ideation,
intensity of ideation, and suicidal behavior to assess lethality of suicide. The scale has
been adapted and developed for various populations (e.g.,

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 56/154)
content:
general population, schools,
and first responders) and has suggested actions coded as red, yellow, and green based
on the responses of the individual.
MINI-MENTAL STATUS EXAMINATION (MMSE) 	The Mini-Mental Status Examination
(MMSE) is a brief (10- to 15-minute), quantitative measure to assess cognitive
impairment for those age 18–85 years, is available in over 70 languages, and is
recommended as a screening tool for depressed individuals (Folstein et al., 2016). The
MMSE demonstrated score validity and reliability in psychiatric, neurologic, geriatric,
and other medical populations. It is used to evaluate five areas of mental status:
orientation, registration, attention and calculation, recall, and language. The maximum
score is 30, and a score of 23 or lower demonstrates a level of cognitive impairment
indicating a referral for a more intensive cognitive evaluation. Orientation, place, and
recall are not impacted by level of education and are important to assess prior to
administering the examination. If only assessing for orientation, place, and recall, a
total maximum score is 17. The MMSE is attributed to the following descriptors that are
frequently referred to in many client charts:
• Oriented *3 (person, place, time)
• Oriented *4 (person, place, time, situation/event)
POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SUICIDE IDEATION INVENTORY 	Positive and Negative Suicide
Ideation Inventory (PANSI) is a 20-item self-report measure of positive and negative
thoughts related to suicide. It contains statements such as “I am in control of my life”
and “I think about killing myself,” to which clients respond on a 1- to 5-point Likert-
type response scale. This instrument is intended to be used as a screening tool for adults
and high school students.
SUICIDE ASSESSMENT FIVE-STEP EVALUATION AND TRIAGE 	The Suicide Assessment
Five-Step Evaluation and Triage (SAFE-T) can be used by mental health professionals
during the initial contact with a person experiencing thoughts of suicide. The five-
step assessment includes identification of risk and protective factors; conducting an
inquiry about suicidality (implementing a Q-tree); determining level of risk and
selecting an appropriate intervention; and documenting the process, including a
follow-up plan.
TASK MODEL OF CRISIS ASSESSMENT AND INTERVENTION 	The task model of crisis
assessment and intervention (see Table 6.4 and Chapter 1; see also Jackson-Cherry,
2018) can be integrated for a suicide assessment and focuses on four important areas of
crisis assessment leading to a more effective and appropriate four-task intervention plan
for individuals in virtually every type of crisis, regardless of setting. Assessment of
safety/stabilization; biopsychosocial/spiritual elements; clarification of the problem(s);
and coping skills, resources, and supports are essential first steps toward more accu-
rately defining the actual crisis situation.

-- 210 of 545 --

182 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
TREATMENT PLANNING AND CARE MANAGEMENT WITH A
SUICIDAL CLIENT
Learning Objective 6.6 Describe appropriate treatment planning and care
management with a suicidal client, including a safety plan, outpatient and inpatient
treatment, and postvention.
After assessing a client’s acute or chronic risk and determining the level of risk, many mental
health professionals might again ask, “Now what do I do?” Assessment is only part of the
intervention process. Rudd et al. (2004) and Jobes (2016), among others, provide qualified
step-by-step recommendations for appropriate treatment planning and responding. Just as
all treatment plans for clients seeking counseling for any condition are customized to meet
their needs, the same is true for clients at risk for suicide. The assessment process yields
valuable information to assist with the design of appropriate treatment interventions. This
section reviews several ideas for counselors to consider, beginning perhaps with the most
important intervention of all: the development of a customized safety plan.
Safety Plan
Developing a safety plan with the client is an industry standard and expectation, but
many mental health professionals were trained prior to the emergence of research
and data to write a “no-harm contract.” Based on research, best-practice thinking,
examination of the potential for false security regarding liability, and more evolved
legal, ethical, and clinical reasoning about how much a suicidal client might be able to
“agree” to such a “contract,” the no-harm contract has been replaced with professional
and empirical preference for the safety plan.
Even seasoned practitioners who are reluctant to do away with the no-harm
contract should be advised that not only is it ill-advised but it is also in contradiction
to many more modern insights about safety planning, and they could run the risk of
increasing liability if using no-harm contracts even in conjunction with safety plans.
Liability for knowing what to do in the case of a suicidal crisis is clearly the burden
of responsibility on the professional and not the client, who would be potentially

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	183
considered not “competent” to direct treatment decisions if incapacitated with life-
threatening depression or multiple symptoms of any diagnosis. Many acutely at-risk
suicidal clients are not in a psychiatrically oriented or lucid condition to shoulder the
burden of knowing enough about suicide or safety planning. Therefore, the attempt of
the clinician to contract with the temporarily disabled client increases the counselor’s
liability through the use of a no-harm contract.
While some authors have referred to safety plans by varying nomenclature (such
as crisis response plan and self-care plan), the NSPL’s Standards, Training, and Practices
Committee recommends the term safety plan.
We would always use the term “safety plan” . . . other terms [crisis response
plan or self-care plan] are too vague and do not explicitly reference the fact
that keeping the individual safe is the primary goal in developing a plan—
vagueness is not good. In addition, we would never reference a “safety plan”
without explicitly stating the avoidance of anything that resembles a “no
harm contract”—so would attempt to eliminate any confusion at the outset.
(G. Murphy, personal communication, September 22, 2010)
Stanley and Brown (2009) have written extensively about the components of a
safety plan and recommend that clients ask the following questions:
1. When should I use the plan?
2. What can I do to calm/comfort myself if I am feeling suicidal?
3. What are my reasons for living?
4. Who can I talk to?
5. Who can I talk to if I need professional assistance?
6. How can I make my environment safe?
7. What should I do if I am still not feeling safe?
Stanley and Brown addressed not only what should be included in safety planning but
also how to talk through its use with clients. They are widely considered the field’s
experts on safety planning, and practitioners should explore their resources and seek
training in their Safety Planning Intervention.
A comprehensive safety plan addresses specific behavioral actions, determined
in concert with the client, and includes several key focal points including means
restriction, self-soothing options, self-care options, family involvement options, resource
development, and crisis response options (M. Gould & D. Jobes, personal communication,
November 10, 2010), in addition to the recommendations that Stanley and Brown (2009)
suggested.
One essential component of safety planning is addressing means restriction
(Coombs et al., 2010; Mann et al., 2005). Since firearms are the most frequently used
method with which to attempt suicide in the United States—and the most lethal with
the least amount of time for active rescue or intervention—counselors developing safety
plans should include a focus on means removal. It is best to engage a safety partner to
assist with means removal to guarantee the means restriction more fully, rather than
counting on the client to do so.
An example of a safety plan is presented in Table 6.5, written with samples of
specific behavioral actions.

-- 212 of 545 --

184 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
A well-facilitated assessment will encourage a client to trust the counselor and
provide detailed information, which will allow the counselor to understand when,
how, and under what conditions the client becomes vulnerable to suicide. For example,
if a client reveals they want to end their life, particularly when experiencing conflict
with a partner, the treatment plan might recommend couples counseling and conflict-
resolution skills building. If an isolated 80-year-old widower reports being desperately
vulnerable between the hours of 5:00 and 8:00 p.m. each day when imagining all the
neighbors having dinner with their loved ones, a treatment plan should increase social
support during that period of vulnerability.
I will . . . (List specific behaviors such as the examples listed below). Customize the plan with
your client.
1. Remove gun and ammunition from house, car, truck, cabin, barn.
(Note to counselor: Means restriction)
2. Fill doctor’s prescriptions weekly (not a month at a time).
(Note to counselor: Means restriction)
3. Take my dog to play in the park.
(Note to counselor: Soothing feelings of distress)
4. Call a friend.
(Note to counselor: Soothing feelings of distress)
5. Eat three meals a day.
(Note to counselor: Self-care)
6. Drink nonalcoholic and noncaffeinated beverages.
(Note to counselor: Self-care)
7. Avoid lonely times by going to my sister’s.
(Note to counselor: Family support)
8. Spend the night at my cousin’s house.
(Note to counselor: Family support)
9. Go to a community support group for persons who have bipolar disorder.
(Note to counselor: Using resources)
10. Call my sponsor or go to a meeting.
(Note to counselor: Using resources)
11. Call 988 or contact https://988lifeline.org
(Note to counselor: Crisis or emergency support)
12. Go to my primary care physician, psychiatrist, or the ED.
(Note to counselor: Crisis or emergency support)

Safety plans should always involve resources, actions, and coping
mechanisms that the client is able and willing to put into place, that are
specific to the situation, and that are written with clear behaviorally specific

Outpatient and

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 57/154)
content:
d Inpatient Treatment Decisions
and Management
Changes in the health care insurance industry and managed care have
severely reduced the amount of hospitalized care that at-risk persons may
obtain, even when suicidal risk remains high. However, when a client is in
immediate risk to self, the decision for hospitalization may be required to keep the per-
son safe. States and counties within states differ as to the process for involuntary and
voluntary hospitalizations, and it is essential that clinicians understand the laws and
policies in the state and county where their clients live. It is better to know what to do
prior to a crisis for both the clinician and the client.
Voluntary hospitalization is preferred over involuntary hospitalization as it allows
the client (and supports) to be part of the decision-making process. In many states, when
a person voluntarily seeks hospitalization, they may have more control in their discharge.
Although this may be preferred, voluntary hospitalization may leave the crisis worker
with an uneasiness of not knowing whether the client followed through with this plan.
When clients and families agree to voluntary hospitalization, the crisis counselor should
obtain a release of information and gain as much information as possible as to where
the client will be seeking admission so they can follow up. A well-being check can be
requested if there is any thought the client (or guardians) did not follow through. The well-
being check is a request of law enforcement to check on the “well-being” of the individual.
Since well-being checks do not fall in the priority of most law enforcement priority calls,
these checks may be at the discretion of the law enforcement officer. An involuntary
hospitalization may follow a voluntary decision that is not followed through by the client.
Involuntary hospitalization differs from state to state, whereas some states require
courts to mandate the commitment of an individual; others allow licensed professionals
to initiate an emergency petition for involuntary hospitalization; some states integrate
mobile crisis teams or crisis intervention teams, while others rely on a community service
board that would make the final assessment and determination based on the clinicians’
observations. The crisis counselors should also establish professional relationships with
community service officers in their jurisdiction and become familiar with protocols. In
these situations, once a client is involuntarily hospitalized, the client will likely not have
further contact with the crisis counselor, and clients will be discharged to outpatient
mental health counselors.
Partial hospitalizations are often referred to as a step down from a hospitalization
when a person may need additional or more intense support than outpatient mental
health. For some this may be residential support in the evening when risk is higher. For
others, like children, partial hospitalization may be scheduled during the day where they
are able to continue their schoolwork and access mental health services and be discharged
to parents in the evening. Medical stabilization and pharmacological intervention are

186 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
the goals of hospital mental health administrators, and discharge planning begins at
admission. No matter how difficult, uncomfortable, or disruptive a hospitalization is
for clients, it does save lives. Discharge planning and collaboration is essential between
hospital personnel and outpatient counselors to eradicate the high window of risk for
suicide after discharge.
CRISIS FOLLOW-UP CONTACT 	The follow-up with a client who is at risk for suicide is
critical practice (Murphy, 2010). Follow-up as a clinical standard is a form of active
postvention that is postventive, preventive, and interventive. Follow-ups may include
regularly scheduled phone calls, letters, or notes, and even texting or e-mailing, or they
may include memoranda of agreements with provider organizations (e.g., between
agencies or practices and hospital EDs).
Data about follow-up assessments with crisis center callers is very encouraging
in that callers who sought out suicide intervention assistance from centers are very
positive and motivated to reduce ambivalence about wanting to die. Clinicians may
direct family care team members to obtain helpful booklets from agencies such as the
Feeling Blue Suicide Prevention Council (https://www.givepulse.com/group/2786-Feeling-
Blue-Suicide-Prevention-Council?) to assist family members following a suicide attempt.
The Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration also has several
“after an attempt” resource booklets for medical providers, attempters, and family
members of attempters that can be obtained at https://store.samhsa.gov/product/A-Guide-
for-Taking-Care-of-Your-Family-Member-After-Treatment-in-the-Emergency-Department/
sma18-4357eng.
DOCUMENTATION OF LETHALITY 	It is important that the counselor notes what a client
reports and how they present. Not asking does not mean a client is not suicidal. It
means the standard of care was not met sufficiently. How a client presents is also
important documentation. Is the client agreeable to a plan? Are they more distant today
than other sessions? Do statements made by the client and how the client is behaving
conflict? Do they appear disoriented? Does the client have distorted thoughts?
A common risk statement should show that a verbal assessment was made as well as an
observation of nonverbal behaviors: “The client does not report or present with current
suicidal or homicidal thoughts and behaviors. The client denies a history of suicidal
behaviors or hospitalizations for psychiatric concerns.” This statement implies that the
counselor has asked the appropriate questions and the client has been evaluated based
on their behavioral presentation. This documentation should also be extended to NSSI
and homicide risk assessments. Now read Voices from the Field 6.1.
Amanda Evans

Crisis Intervention with Clients Who Have Low Cognitive Functioning

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	187
Postvention
In recent years, postvention has grown to include the care practices that practitioners
provide to persons who have survived a suicide attempt. Postvention with attempt
survivors can include ongoing stabilization, long-term therapy, and improvement of
help-seeking skills and overall self-care, with a goal of long-term improvements in
quality of life and functioning.
Historically, postvention has been the type of care provided to survivors of suicide
loss. Survivors of suicide or homicide loss can develop protracted symptoms of grief,
depression, anxiety, PTSD, longer and more complicated grief and bereavement; more
demands on medical and mental health services; and greater potential for lost time
at work and at school. Survivors of suicide loss also have an elevated risk for suicide
attempts. The remainder of this section focuses on survivors of suicide loss.
BEREAVEMENT AND OTHER ISSUES 	Bereavement from both suicide and homicide can
remain complicated for many years. The DSM-5-TR (American Psychiatric Association,
2022) added a diagnosis of prolonged grief disorder; arguably, many survivors of suicide
or homicide loss might be at risk for this diagnosis due to the vexing circumstances
compared to other types of loss.
Given the prevailing prevalence of suicide in the United States, a conservative
estimate of persons who become newly bereaved due to the loss of a loved one by suicide
numbers over 275,000 per year (Suicide Awareness Voices of Education, 2022). The job of
bereavement counselors is an important one, due both to the large number of survivors
of suicide loss in the population and the misperceptions and myths about suicide both
before and after death. Survivors of suicide loss often report feeling misunderstood,
stigmatized, and marginalized. They are prone to bereavement and sorrow, complicated
grief, and posttraumatic symptoms (Harrington, 2011). Their bereavement may be
punctuated by the complexities of believing that their loved one “rationally chose”
death, as contrasted with viewing suicide as a multifactorial process of debilitation and
vulnerability (Survivors of Loved Ones’ Suicides, 2006). They may have lost a child, a
appears to be 50 years old may in fact function cognitively
as an adolescent or as a child. This can be challenging in
a hospital setting when clients in crisis require immediate,
effective, and appropriate medical attention.
Oftentimes, patients who were lower functioning
were expected to cope and manage in the same
way as their higher-functioning peers. This included
maintaining personal hygiene, medication compliance,
following the unit’s rules, and establishing appropriate
boundaries with their peers. In addition, I noted
counselors who provided treatment to these clients
struggled with gathering intake information and
establishing aftercare goals. This difficulty in serving
lower-functioning patients was most evident in the
staff’s attempt to deescalate crises. In my opinion,
clients who are lower functioning are commonly
misinterpreted by helping professionals, leading to
unnecessary rapid escalation in crisis situations, physical
restraints, and possibly medical restraints.
Based on my personal experiences, I want to
encourage counselors-in-training to consider alternative
methods to communicate with clients in crisis. When
patients who have low cognitive functioning are in
crisis, expecting mature, rational decision making may
be unrealistic; however, helping clients to express
themselves, calm down, or self-soothe is ideal. In
addition to talking through a crisis, working with
individuals using pictures, schedules, sign language,
silence, or techniques specific to the client’s needs
can be very helpful in deescalating a crisis. At times,
competent care requires stepping outside of the box
and using alternative interventions to best assist clients.

-- 216 of 545 --

188 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
sibling, or a partner within a cloud of unknown circumstances, such as losses or defeats
that resulted in acute suicide risk, such as a bad business deal, a negative performance
evaluation at work, or an act of infidelity that they could not work through, to name a few.
The circumstances surrounding the immediate scene of a suicide may include the
involvement of law enforcement, investigators treating the scene as a murder, potential
arrest warrants being issued, and limited or potentially gruff communication with
personnel such as coroners or first responders, possibly punctuated with yellow crime
scene tape. Of course, many survivors report exemplary conduct on the part of these
professionals, and so it is not a universal problem, but for some survivors of suicide loss,
the circumstances surrounding the immediate scene complicate the grieving process.
Adults and children alike a

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 58/154)
content:
re often witnesses to the actual suicide, they may
discover the body, or they might be involved in clean-up. Many family-related and
religious complications can ensue, with conflicts possible among stepfamily, family-of-
origin, or in-law family members who may disagree about stating the cause of death
openly to their communities. Sometimes the suicide may be like one domino in a row
of dominoes that continue to fall. For example, consider the scenario in which a middle-
aged person dies from suicide, leaving financial problems that result in foreclosure
or bankruptcy for the surviving family members, leaving college-age children unable
to continue their schooling, and a partner/parent who must reenter the workforce or
sell the house. The suicide can become one crisis of many in a sequence of events, not
unlike the ABC-X model of crises (Hill, 1949, 1958) or the Double ABC-X model of crises
(McCubbin & Patterson, 1982).
Counselors providing postvention grief services to survivors of loss should
become familiar with complicated bereavement and mourning, learn about counseling
techniques and countenance directly related to working with suicide loss, and especially
be aware of the feelings of helplessness, sense of responsibility, and internalized guilt
that the bereft feel after a suicide. Many family members have knowledge of their loved
one’s struggles and may have assisted in ways that in fact prolonged the life of their
loved one. Yet the traumatic circumstances, the stigma or belief that it was a rational
choice, or the complicated mourning leave survivors clinging to a sense of guilt-framed
attribution of responsibility.
FRAMEWORK TO INTERVENE WITH SURVIVORS 	Whether working with an individual or
with a group, there are common factors to take into consideration that may be unique to
those grieving the loss due to suicide: (1) prevention of further suicides, (2) reconstructing
the “why,” (3) commemorating the positive, (4) saying goodbye, and (5) an assessment
of guilt/shame and absolution of guilt. The main priority throughout the time with
survivors is to assess and prevent further additional suicidal behaviors. Often, suicide
survivors have no closure and may not know why their loved one decided to take their
own life. It is unlike the grief process during which a person is grieving the loss of a
loved one with a known cause (e.g., terminal illness, accident).
In suicide cases, survivors often are stuck in the “why.” Questions such as “Why
did he not come to me for help?” and “Why did she take her own life?” are things
that keep a survivor progressing through healthy grief work. Counselors may need
to normalize the repetition of these questions and reconstruct the why to understand
what the underlying meaning of these “why” questions is for the survivors. Meaning
may relate to the perception of being a “bad” parent, grieving the loss of a role of being

-- 217 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	189
a family member, or could uncover some hidden guilt in not being present when they
now believe they could have assisted in suicide prevention.
Connected with being stuck in the “why,” survivors are unable to have closure and
move on with their own lives through letting go or saying goodbye. Counselors, when
reconstructing the “why,” will often help clients work toward moving on in their own
life while still remembering the loved one lost. Similar to staying stuck in the cognitive
“why,” survivors may experience a range of emotions, including anger, sadness,
confusion, numbness, and guilt (to name a few) toward their loved one. Being stuck in
one emotion can also prevent survivors from moving on and healing in grief work and
may lead to negative feelings toward their loved one. Being able to commemorate a
positive trait or attribute can allow a survivor to start working through the loss.
Finally, in suicide cases, there may be a degree of guilt by the survivor, thinking
they could have done something to prevent the suicide. In many cases, survivors can
recall the warning signs of the loved one. However, these warning signs are often
only evident after the fact and could not have predicted a suicide. Therefore, it may be
important for survivors to know that whatever the situation or relationship leading up to
the suicide, it was ultimately the decision of the loved one to take their own life—offering
absolution of guilt. Having these aspects built into the grief process can help survivors
move into healthy grieving.
When working with family and friends, it is imperative to determine who may
have found the body. Often, a family member may not be forthcoming if they found the
body. The human senses (visual, hearing, touch, or smell) connected with the suicide
may trigger flashbacks for this individual; therefore, the individual who found the body
should be a priority in counseling.
Counselors should become familiar with how to find suicide bereavement
groups that exist in many localities around the country by visiting either the American
Foundation for Suicide Prevention or the American Association of Suicidology websites
for comprehensive listings. While survivors of suicide loss often report that they speak
the same language, a language that other bereft persons do not fully understand, not
all survivors will think that a group is the best option for them. Other suggestions
for therapeutic responses that counselors can recommend include bibliotherapy and
Internet resources, psychoeducation, family guidance, survivor outreach, cyber groups
or chatrooms, survivor conferences, and activism, with activism marking a full circle
from postvention to prevention.
Suicide bereavement counselors might provide psychoeducational insights to
survivors of suicide loss. For example, the counselor may provide insight that experts do
not believe that suicide is a “rational choice,” that intent looks very much like a “choice”
when clinically they are very different, that suicide was not done to punish the survivors
but rather to end the pain or to alleviate the burden that the decedent perceives they were
(often a mistaken perspective). Counselors should be prepared to support survivors of
loss when survivors encounter misinformation, myths, stigma, and judgments about the
type of death that occurred. Some people, even well-meaning friends, say hurtful and
untrue things, such as “I can’t believe he would do such a selfish and hostile thing to
you” or “How tragic that she committed this sin; she’ll surely go to hell.”
Finally, some counselors become crisis advisors to employers, agencies, schools,
faith-based groups, and communities. These professionals should be familiar with the
national resources available for responsible communication with the media following

resources available on their websites for distribution to decision makers, spokespersons,
and even media representatives to use the public news forum responsibly, prevent

NONSUICIDAL SELF-INJURY
Learning Objective 6.7 Describe assessments and treatment for NSSI, including the
use of a Q-tree.
The DSM-5-TR added suicidal behavior and nonsuicidal self-injury into the “other
conditions that may be a focus of clinical attention” section. The inclusion of NSSI
should improve documentation of these behaviors to determine risk factors for suicidal
ideation and actions and provide more effective treatment planning options. NSSI is
the intentional, self-inflicted, immediate destruction of body tissue, without suicidal
intent, that is not viewed as culturally acceptable behavior. Self-injurious behaviors can
be performed on any part of the body but often injuries are done to the hands, wrists,
stomach, and thighs. The severity of NSSI can range from superficial wounds to long-
term disfigurement. In one study, 33% of people who reported engaging in self-injurious
behaviors reported they should have sought medical attention; however, only 6.5% in the
same study reported receiving medical attention for their wounds. Although the most
common NSSI behavior continues to be reported as cutting, other common behaviors or
methods of NSSI include, but are not limited to:
• Burning
• Skin picking
• Hair pulling

-- 219 of 545 --

Chapter 6 • Risk Assessment and Intervention: Suicide, Nonsuicidal Self-Injury, and Homicide 	191
• Banging or hitting the head or other body parts
• Rubbing objects into the skin
• Skin erasing or carving
• Interfering with wound healing
• Bone breaking
Most of the literature suggests that NSSI increases substantially in early adolescence
and decreases during late adolescence and early adulthood (Plener et al., 2015) with
more incidents of NSSI behaviors by females (Fox, 2015). Considering the prevalence of
self-injury among adolescents, the likelihood that counselors and medical professionals
will encounter NSSI is quite high. Historically, NSSI has been viewed as not linked
with suicidal intent, but most clinicians and researchers suggest that NSSI is a strong
predictor of future suicidal behavior. Scott et al. (2015) estimated that suicide attempts
increased fourfold for those who previously engaged in NSSI and suggested that both
suicide ideation and NSSI are known risk factors for suicidal behavior with increased
predictive risk for suicide attempts.
Assessments and Q-Tree
The high correlation of NSSI and future suicide attempts prompted the DSM-5-TR
(American Psychiatric Association, 2022) to add suicidal behavior and NSSI into section
2, “other conditions that may be a focus of clinical attention.” Furthermore, it is a
standard of care to integrate an NSSI assessment as part of all suicide risk assessments.
It is essential to understand the intentions of those who participate in NSSI to properly
assess intent, implement the most effective treatment strategy, or appropriately refer to
additional care. Every i

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 59/154)
content:
ntake should have an NSSI assessment in addition to the suicide
and homicide risk assessment, even if NSSI is not a presenting issue. It would be better
to include a standard NSSI question than not ask and live with the consequences after
the fact. This could entail simply including the following:
• “Have you ever engaged in any nonsuicidal self-injurious behaviors?”
✓ If no, proceed to a suicidal risk assessment
✓ If yes, complete the NSSI assessment
Kruzan and Whitlock (2019) adapted the transtheoretical model (TTM) of behavior
change proposed by Prochaska and DiClemente to assess motivation for change. TTM
integrates the following while assessing motivation of change: (1) precontemplation
(with no intent to stop NSSI); (2) contemplation (intent to stop NSSI within 6 months);
(3) preparation (intention of taking steps to stop NSSI within next month); (4) action
(currently taking steps to stop NSSI); (5) maintenance (steps have been taken for more
than 6 months); and (6) termination (cessation of NSSI for over 3 years).
We have integrated a Q-tree into the TTM as an example for crisis counselors to
assess and identify a timeline of NSSI, risk factors, and motivation for change (protective
factors) to provide referral for treatment.
Have you self-injured in the last 6 months?
If YES (to the 6-month question), is self-injury something you would like to stop?
• If yes, have you taken steps to stop NSSI?
✓ If yes, explore ACTION STAGE and ACTION PLANNING

• If no, do you plan to take steps to stop NSSI?
✓ If no, client is in the PRECONTEMPLATION STAGE (consider psychoedu-
cation)
✓ If yes, client is in the PREPARATION STAGE (explore ideas and establish
plan)
If NO (to the 6-month question), have you self-injured in the past YEAR?
• If yes, is self-injury something you would like to stop?
• If no, have you self-injured in the past 3 YEARS?
✓ If yes, explore MAINTENANCE STAGE
✓ If no, no action is recommended
A more comprehensive assessment should follow this Q-tree screening approach.
The TTM approach assesses the timeline and motivations for change based on the
motivation for change theory. It is limited to these assessment categories unless you
implement additional elements needed for a more thorough NSSI assessment. We
suggest that more elements should be included for a comprehensive NSSI assessment
and have included the following adapted NSSI assessment, based on the research and
clinical work of Wester and Trepal (2017), who also found a high correlation between
NSSI and suicidal attempts. Thus, it is best practice to conduct independent suicidal risk
and NSSI assessments.
According to Wester and Trepal (2017), the NSSI assessment should gather:

and assessing NSSI risk, a crisis counselor can provide effective referral for a client.

Just as with suicidal risk assessment, the counselor should weigh the risk factors
and protective factors to gather all the information required for treatment planning and
referral. As a note, often NSSI behaviors can serve as protective factors so the counselor
is cautioned to not eliminate NSSI behaviors without evaluating the impact of stopping
the NSSI behavior, or exploring the elimination, as this could increase suicidal thoughts
or behaviors. This model is more comprehensive in gathering and reviewing several key
pieces of information that facilitate referral for continued care.
NSSI assessment, like suicide and homicide risk assessment, often requires long-
term care, which may exceed the time a true crisis counselor can offer, unless the NSSI
treatment takes place in long-term counseling. Often, crisis counselors will gather the
information and then refer the person to appropriate long-term counseling. Using
the template above allows a counselor to gather the basic, contextual, and functional
information needed to provide a solid referral for treatment.
Treatment Approaches
Understanding the most effective treatment approaches for NSSI is also essential. The most
common therapeutic approaches for NSSI include dialectical behavior therapy, cognitive-
behavioral therapy, problem-solving therapy (rational problem-solving therapy),
and treatment for self-injurious behaviors (T-SIB). T-SIB was developed as a brief
nine-session intervention to treat NSSI behaviors in young adults without borderline
personality disorder (see Table 6.7). T-SIB can be used as a standalone treatment or an
adjunctive intervention and includes the components in Table 6.7 with psychoeducation

and therapeutic interventions. Counselors know how stressful this work can be and are
