COMMON CRISIS ISSUES
Learning Objective 8.4 Understand the common crisis issues of individuals in violent
relationships.
The intense emotional and physical stress caused by IPV/DV can often exceed the
perceived coping resources of the abused partner. As a counselor responding to an IPV/
DV crisis, the overarching goal is the safety of the victims, regardless of whether they
decide to stay in or leave the violent relationships. Simultaneously, and because in most
instances the decision to stay in or leave a violent relationship is in the hands of the victim,
counselors should also work to empower clients to solve problems effectively based on
the client’s unique situation and available resources. Counselors should conduct a triage
assessment to determine which aspects of the situation require immediate intervention.
In addition, crisis counselors should work with IPV/DV clients in crisis to increase
perceived options, mobilize resources, and identify sources of continued support after
the crisis is stabilized (Greenstone & Leviton, 2011). Common crisis issues that emerge
when working with IPV/DV clients include attending to physical injury, establishing
immediate safety, and deciding whether to report IPV/DV to the police.
Attending to Physical Injury
Some IPV/DV victims receive treatment in emergency departments and doctors’ offices
for injuries inflicted by an intimate partner; yet health care providers often fail to assess
the cause of the injuries they treat (Ghandour et al., 2015). Just as it is imperative for
health care providers to screen for IPV/DV, it is also essential that counselors ask about
physical injuries and facilitate medical care when necessary. Physical injury resulting
from IPV/DV can range from scrapes and bruises to permanent disfigurement, disability,
and death. Emergency medical treatment may be required before any further counseling
intervention can occur; therefore, counselors should be prepared to refer clients and
arrange transportation to appropriate medical facilities. They should also be aware of
free and low-cost medical resources since victims may not have health insurance. In
addition, since IPV/DV can take the form of sexual assault and rape, counselors should
be aware of medical facilities that specialize in rape. Advising the client of what to expect
during use of a rape kit can help reduce anxiety and allow the client to make an informed
decision about consenting to the procedure.

258 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
When a referral to a medical facility is necessary, crisis counselors should ensure
continuity of care by communicating that they are not abandoning the client, checking
to make sure that the client arrives at the medical facility safely, and advising the client
that counseling is available for continued support once medical needs are addressed.
Establishing Immediate Safety
During an IPV/DV crisis, assessing and planning for physical safety or preventing
further harm to the client is paramount. Time is of the essence, and crisis counselors must
gain thorough and accurate information quickly by conducting a detailed assessment of
the situation, including violence severity, available resources, and barriers to accessing
resources and treatment (Ghandour et al., 2015). The counselor must also directly assess
the client for suicide and homicide risk and be prepared to take protective measures,
if needed (e.g., to act on the duty to warn, to arrange for involuntary psychiatric
hospitalization of the victim or perpetrator in severe cases).
In some situations, the client may not be able to return home safely, so the counselor
must arrange for emergency shelter. In the event a client decides to return home, a harm
reduction plan should be developed. In both scenarios determining others who may be
affected (e.g., children, other household members, supporters, and pets) is important in
crisis work. A detailed description of how to construct a safety plan or harm reduction
plan is provided later in the chapter.
Reporting Intimate Partner Violence to the Police
The process for reporting an IPV/DV incident to the police is complex and differs
from state to state. States outline who is a mandated reporter, what injuries require
reporting, and what exceptions are outlined in the law. A counselor should be aware
of the mandated state reporting law for DV. Some states have mandatory reporting
laws but require specified injuries and wounds (e.g., gunshot, stabbing) to mandate
the reporting of DV (Futures without Violence, 2022). Other states require consent of the
victim to report if specified injuries are not present.
Mandated reporting laws for IPV/DV are different from the mandated reporting
laws for suspected child, elder, or other vulnerable persons abuse. However, if a victim of
DV discloses there is also abuse to a child, elder, or vulnerable adult, then the mandated
reporting under these separate suspected abuse situations must be made in compliance
with state mandates—no exceptions. When a counselor must act as a mandated reporter
of child, elder, or vulnerable person, the IPV/DV victim may perceive this reporting
as more victimization. To minimize the victim’s loss of power, counselors should pay
special attention to the informed-consent process regarding limits of confidentiality and
encourage the IPV/DV client to report the abuse.
The majority of IPV/DV incidents are not reported to the police. A victim may
choose not to contact the police due to the belief that the abuse was not serious enough
to require police intervention, that the police will not be helpful, or that if the police
are called, the relationship with the abusive partner will need to end. In addition, a
victim may fear the consequences of contacting the police, such as the loss of housing or
the involvement of child protective services. At the same time, many victims want the
violence to stop but do not want to leave the relationship in the hopes that the situation
will get better.

Chapter 8 • Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence 	259
For members of racial-ethnic or other marginalized groups (e.g., LGBTQ+,
immigrants), the decision to call the police may be further complicated by other factors
discussed in more detail later in this chapter. Individuals in DV relationships may fear
that calling the police will increase the risk for further harm from their abusers or may
impact immigration status. Unless suicidality, homicidality, specific injuries sustained
(see previous discussion), or suspicion of child, elder, or vulnerable persons abuse is
disclosed, counselors are not required to report IPV/DV incidents and should support
the choice the client makes regarding police notification (Augustyn & Willyard, 2022;
Becker & Bachman, 2020). The counselor should discuss the pros and cons of police
intervention with the client and then respect the client’s decision. Counselors must
understand the laws regarding DV to help clients determine what is realistic based on
the laws that govern law enforcement officers’ actions. State laws differ regarding police
intervention, arrest policies, and charges. Complete Activity 8.2 to assess your beliefs
about IPV/DV. Then complete Think About It 8.1.

260 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
INTIMATE PARTNER VIOLENCE IN SPECIAL POPULATIONS
Learning Objective 8.5 Consider and explore special populations in IPV/DV
relationships and their needs.
Culture is important to consider when understanding and treating clients experiencing
IPV/DV. Culture guides how clients define, view, experience, and respond to IPV/DV.
Counselors responding to IPV/DV should attend to cultural dimensions such as sex,
race/ethnicity, socioeconomic status (SES), level of acculturation, language, religiosity,
sexual orientation, ability status, and age. With the increased acknowledgment that
IPV/DV is a global problem, the social and cultural contexts of IPV/DV are gaining
much-needed attention. Unfortunately, the impact of culture on the IPV/DV experience,
including the reporting of IPV/DV and help-seeking behaviors, has been largely ignored
in the counseling literature. The sections that follow highlight some of the social and
cultural considerations relevant to the conceptualization, prevalence, and presentation
of IPV/DV, including racial-ethnic and marginalized concerns and the intersection of
sex, social class, immigration status, and religiosity.
Race and Ethnicity
Race and ethnicity are important considerations in understanding and intervening
in IPV/DV. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC; 2021c) reported
that 54% of Black women and 44% of multiracial, non-Hispanic women experienced
sexual assault, IPV, or stalking in their lifetime, which is a higher proportion than
White, non-Hispanic women (35%). Asian or Pacific Islander women were less likely to
experience IPV/DV over their lifetime compared to White (non-Hispanic) women. This
report also estimates Alaskan Native men and Black, non-Hispanic men experienced
rape, physical violence, or stalking by an intimate partner more frequently than White,
non-Hispanic men.
Some studies show lifetime rates for physical and/or sexual abuse as high as
41–60%, depending on the amount of time subgroups have resided in the United States
(Yoshihama et al., 2015). Obviously, the prevalence of IPV/DV can differ depending on
the study; hence, reported data may not accurately indicate actual prevalence. Differences
may occur for several reasons:
1. Some rates may be underreported due to cultural values or social factors.
2. There are flaws in reporting as individuals get “lumped” into larger racial-ethnic
groups, ignoring great variation within each racial-ethnic group.
3. Information is self-report data obtained from samples of convenience, which vary
significantly depending on the setting in which they are obtained.
4. Many studies gather IPV/DV data from health departments and clinics that serve
lower-SES victims of IPV/DV; whereas higher-SES groups may be financially
able to obtain counseling services through private practices and therefore may be
underreported in many studies.
IPV/DV reporting trends may be associated with community services sought by
different racial-ethnic groups. For example, Black and Hispanic female IPV/DV victims
were two to three times more likely than White female victims to report IPV/DV to the

-- 289 of 545 --

Chapter 8 • Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence 	261
police in one study (Lipsky et al., 2009). Unfortunately, IPV/DV-related resources may
be unavailable in certain lower-SES communities of color, affecting reporting and help-
seeking trends (Hien & Ruglass, 2009).
Various forms of oppression such as racism, heterosexism, classism, and ableism
may intersect to further prohibit a sense of safety to report. Alternatively, the degree of
cultural solidarity may perpetuate IPV/DV. Strong cultural ties, particularly in smaller
communities, may isolate women from outside resources, promote greater acceptance
of gender inequities, and result in a stronger tradition of family secrecy.
Regardless of the differences in IPV/DV prevalence rates by race and ethnicity,
there seem to be similar reasons for remaining in violent relationships. However, there
are cultural subtleties in how IPV/DV is recognized and addressed that affect reporting
trends across racial-ethnic groups. The interrelated factors include patriarchal family
structure, SES, immigrant status, and religious and spiritual beliefs.
Socioeconomic Status
The role of lower SES may be connected to higher rates of IPV/DV incidents (National
Center for Victims of Crime, 2017). Poverty is inextricably linked to limited resources,
substance use, social isolation, pregnancy, and unemployment. These variables
collectively create unhealthy environ

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 77/154)
content:
ments that both initiate and perpetuate IPV/
DV. Some argue that it may be income or social inequality rather than poverty that is
associated with IPV/DV. That is, the wider the gap between the “haves” and “have-nots,”
the greater the violence victimization of those with lower SES. Social disorganization
theorists argue that concentrated poverty weakens informal community resources
and leads to a lowered sense of self-efficacy in the community for responding to IPV/
DV. Others argue IPV/DV transcend SES and occur at similar rates across SES groups.
However, middle- or upper-SES groups often have the resources to seek more formal
counseling, services, or supports that are not often used by reporting agencies. Much of
the statistics of IPV/DV originate from police reports and health care clinics, which may
be used less often by middle- to upper-SES groups.
Immigrant Status
IPV/DV is more common among immigrants and refugees than longer-term U.S. citizens
overall, as migration from one country to another often creates isolation that facilitates
IPV/DV situations. On a global level, 38% of all murders against women are at the hands
of intimate partners and approximately 30% of the world’s women have been physically
assaulted by a partner (World Health Organization [WHO], 2021b). In the United States,
for immigrant women there is an overall decrease in the use of social and health care
services. Language barriers often prevent women in abusive relationships from seeking
assistance. Lack of knowledge of available resources, distrust of government agencies,
and lack of knowledge of the U.S. legal system may also be barriers to help-seeking
for immigrant women who experience IPV/DV. Undocumented immigrant victims of
IPV/DV may be more hesitant to report IPV/DV due to concerns with deportation.
With many immigrant and refugee women entering the United States each year, it is
imperative that counselors acknowledge the prevalence, and assess for the consequences,
of IPV/DV in this population.

-- 290 of 545 --

262 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
Religious and Spiritual Beliefs
Religiosity, spirituality, and partner violence are linked in both positive and negative
ways (Houston-Kolnik et al., 2019; Orpinas et al., 2022). Some individuals who perpetrate
partner violence may use (and misuse) religious texts and beliefs to justify or rationalize
IPV/DV. Some IPV/DV victims may stay in abusive relationships due to religious or
spiritual beliefs that condemn divorce and promote male domination, superior male
morality, the value of suffering, and submission and obedience to husbands. Some IPV/
DV victims may be hesitant to seek help outside of the religious or spiritual community
or may be reluctant to disclose IPV/DV to preserve the family image. Some religious
leaders may be unprepared to respond to issues of IPV/DV.
Religious or spiritual practices may also serve as protective factors against IPV/DV
and as a coping mechanism in recovering from its consequences. Faith in a higher power can
serve as an inner coping resource for religious IPV/DV victims. In the Black community,
prayer is sometimes used for coping with IPV/DV consequences, and religious leaders
who are well trained in responding to IPV/DV may help bridge the gap between religious
and community resources for victims (Houston-Kolnik et al., 2019; Orpinas et al., 2022).
Female-to-Male Violence
Most of the literature, as well as provision of shelters and other resources, have
historically focused on women as victims of DV. Although research does support the
higher prevalence of women in DV relationships, data also show that men are victims of
DV. The National Center for Victims of Crime (2017) approximates one in four men in
the United States (25.7% or 29 million) have reported being slapped or pushed/shoved
by an intimate partner in his lifetime and one in seven men in the United States (13.8%
or 15.6 million) experienced more severe physical abuse resulting in injury.
Why might female-to-male violence be underreported? Gender norms may prevent
men who suffer emotional, physical, or sexual abuse from reporting because disclosure
might “emasculate” them. Men may also fail to define their experiences as abusive or
may fear being laughed at or ridiculed if they reach out for help. Male victims of IPV/
DV may also be reluctant to report abuse from a female partner for fear that they will
also be accused of being abusive (Douglas et al., 2012).
Additionally, there are fewer IPV/DV resources available for men. Most shelters
are designated for women and serve only women and children. Funding and grants
are often awarded to clinics and centers that assist women who are victims of IPV/DV.
Prevailing myths, discriminative training and education, and biased clinical practices
based only on feminist theory or accepted myths that men cannot be the victims of IPV/
DV may prevent many in the helping field from intervening or even asking males at
intake about being victims of IPV/DV.
To effectively meet the needs of male victims, counselors should validate their
experiences, provide accurate educational information about IPV/DV, and provide
gender-appropriate IPV/DV resources.
LGBTQ+ Violence
The lifetime prevalence of IPV/DV for LGBTQ+ (lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender,
questioning, and others) individuals is greater than that in heterosexual couples. The

-- 291 of 545 --

Chapter 8 • Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence 	263
National Center for Victims of Crime (2017) reports 4 in 10 lesbian women, 6 in 10 bisex-
ual women, 1 in 4 gay men, and 1 in 3 bisexual men have experienced rape, physical
violence, or stalking by an intimate partner. Prevalence in the transgender community
is harder to estimate, yet the problem seems to be more pervasive. Approximately 35%
of all transgender individuals reported experiencing physical abuse from a partner
(James et al., 2016).
Estimating the prevalence of LGBTQ+ violence presents difficulties like those
encountered in other populations. Among the systems affecting LGBTQ+ disclosure of
IPV/DV are family and friends, mutual friends of the abused and abuser in the LGBTQ+
community, LGBTQ+-affirmative shelters and crisis counselors, the legal system (e.g.,
attorneys, jurors, judges, laws), public policy, and societal myths based on gender norms,
homophobia, transphobia, and heterosexism (Walker, 2015).
Is IPV/DV a gendered problem, as many feminists assert? Can men be victims
and women be batterers? The feminist notion that IPV/DV is caused by traditional
gender-role socialization in a patriarchal family structure often does not fit LGBTQ+
relationships. If IPV/DV is because of patriarchy, gay and bisexual men cannot be
abused, and lesbian and bisexual women cannot be abusive. Feminist theory also fails
to account for the experiences of transgender individuals. Thus, feminist theory may
leave counselors working with LGBTQ+ couples with little information about the causes
of IPV/DV (Walker, 2015).
Homophobia and transphobia, or prejudice and discrimination against LGBTQ+
individuals and their culture, contribute to the minimization of IPV/DV in the LGBTQ+
community and preclude many from seeking help from traditional IPV/DV resources
(e.g., shelters, community agencies, the legal system). These attitudes and behaviors are
often based on heterosexism, the idea that heterosexuality is normative and thus superior
to other forms of sexuality. Stereotypical gender-role misconceptions also negatively
impact the transgender community. LGBTQ+ couples may internalize heterosexist and
transphobic ideas and thus feel responsible for protecting one another due to societal
oppression and the resulting social isolation.
An LGBTQ+ partner who is being abused may find it difficult to report the abuse
for several reasons: (1) the abuser may threaten to “out” the abused if the IPV/DV is
disclosed to anyone; (2) the abused may be fearful of disclosing sexual orientation or
transgender identity in the process of disclosing the abuse; (3) the abusive partner may
be the only source of support; (4) the abusive partner may share the same friends as
the abused partner, and embarrassing the abuser may result in potentially
losing the community in which the abused partner’s sexual orientation and/
or gender identity is accepted; (5) the process of disclosure may reinforce or
strengthen internalized homophobia or transphobia in the abused partner;
and (6) the desire to maintain the LGBTQ+ community’s reputation for
offering safety and advocating for equal rights (e.g., marriage, adoption)
may outweigh the desire to assert that there is “something wrong” in the
community, such as IPV/DV (James et al., 2016; Walker, 2015).
Taken together, these factors have contributed to a lack of resources
for LGBTQ+ individuals who experience IPV/DV. Gay and bisexual
men may have fewer resources than do lesbian and bisexual women.
Few shelters accept gay and bisexual men due to the propensity toward
aggression and homophobia of other male batterers who may be living

in the shelter. Transgender IPV/DV victims are rarely welcomed at shelters or other
community agencies who define IPV/DV as a problem that affects women in heterosexual
relationships (Walker, 2015). Furthermore, IPV/DV victims seeking services may be
revictimized if their relationship is viewed as not being “real” or if they are subjected to
other forms of homophobia, transphobia, and heterosexism.
Those providing community resources are often placed in a difficult position. Since
LGBTQ+ violence goes against the typical view of the causes and course of IPV/DV,
they are left to decide “who to believe” and thus who should receive assistance and who
should be reprimanded. This creates many situations where victims are ignored, abusers
are unintentionally allowed into similar shelters as those they abuse, and abusers are
not prosecuted.
Disability Status
Another special population includes individuals with physical, cognitive, or emotional
disabilities. Controlling for race/ethnicity, age, and SES, women with disabilities are
physically or sexually assaulted at a rate double that of those without a disability
(Breiding & Armour, 2015). A victim or perpetrator with a disability is at risk for IPV/
DV, or the disability may be a result of IPV/DV toward the individual. Vulnerability and
powerlessness are prevalent in this population, as they often depend on their intimate
partners to assist them with activities of daily living, such as providing medication,
bathing, dressing, and running errands.
A vulnerable person is a person who is under the care of another person for some
or even all their daily living activities. Because of being “under the care of” another,
laws are in place that mandate counselors to report these incidents to authorities. More
on vulnerable person abuse is found in other chapters of this text. Crisis counselors are
strongly encouraged to ensure that the agencies in which they work are adequately
prepared for individuals with disabilities; this may include having physical items such as
wheelchairs readily available and making referrals for physical health needs. Counselors
should be aware of the laws for mandated reporters of abuse of vulnerable persons.
Elder Abuse
With the U.S. population living longer, IPV/DV will increasingly affect those within the
aging population. Abuse of an older adult may occur within intimate relationships as well
as extended families. Available data suggest that approximately 3.5 million Americans
age 65 years and older experience ab

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 78/154)
content:
use from someone they are dependent on for care and
protection (National Center on Elder Abuse, 2022). Older adult women are more likely than
older adult men to experience abuse, and as age increases, so does the likelihood of abuse.
In addition to physical, sexual, and emotional abuse, types of older adult abuse include
neglect (e.g., malnutrition, poor personal hygiene, unsafe or unclean living conditions),
abandonment (e.g., desertion of an elder at a facility or public location), financial or
material exploitation (e.g., sudden changes in bank account practices, unpaid bills despite
availability of resources, forgery), and self-neglect. Vulnerability and powerlessness can
be common risk factors for elder abuse and individuals with disabilities.
Signs of IVP (e.g., bruising, unexplained injuries, confusion) may be overlooked
or considered medical issues associated with aging. Mental health service providers
may hold ageist beliefs that could negatively impact older adults in IPV/DV situations.

Similar to mandated reporting laws for vulnerable persons, all states have mandated
reporting laws for elder abuse. More on elder person abuse is found within this text.
Counselors should be aware of the laws for mandated reporters of older adult abuse.
Culturally competent counselors recognize that IPV/DV is a real and salient issue in the
older population and respond with appropriate screening, interventions, and resources.
Counselors should be aware of the laws for mandated reporters of abuse of elder persons.
Dating Violence among Adolescents and Young Adults
IPV/DV begins early for many—sometimes with their first dating experiences. An
estimated 23% of females and 14% of males report some form of IPV/DV prior to the age
of 18 years; and 71% of females and 58% of males report some form of IPV/DV before age
25 years (National Center for Victims of Crime, 2017). Dating violence, just like other forms
of IPV/DV, includes physical, emotional, and sexual abuse and stalking. Adolescents
who do not have clear ideas about appropriate boundaries and healthy relationships
may characterize abusive behaviors as acceptable or even positive characteristics of a
relationship. For example, an adolescent may view possessive, stalking behaviors as a sign
that their dating partner cares about them. Many adolescents spend large amounts of time
using social media and other forms of technology. Social media can increase the prevalence
of abuse when used to control behavior or intimidate, humiliate, or coerce a partner.
The CDC (2021b) has linked adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) to higher rates
of teen dating violence. ACEs are potentially traumatic events that include experiences
of or witnessing violence, abuse, or neglect within the family; having a member of the
family attempt or die by suicide; substance use; mental health concerns or instability
to a parental separation or alienation; and other circumstances that can impact safety,
stability, and bonding. In their research, an estimated 61% of adults surveyed reported
they experienced at least one type of ACE; one in six reported they had experienced four
or more ACEs. Unfortunately, dating violence in adolescence significantly predicts IPV/
DV in young adulthood. Exposure to violence in childhood increases the potential for
perpetrating and experiencing IPV/DV.
Dating violence on college and university campuses is of particular concern.
College women who experience IPV/DV possess characteristics that distinguish their
IPV/DV experiences from the experiences of victims in other age groups. For example,
most college-age women do not live with their partners, do not have children, and are
not financially dependent on their partners (Edwards et al., 2012). Thus, when college-
age IPV/DV victims decide whether to remain in a relationship with an abusive partner,
they may consider these and other variables.
Due to the prevalence of and serious consequences associated with dating violence,
multiple prevention programs have been developed for adolescents and young adults.
The CDC (2021c) reports that experiences of IPV/DV in teen dating relationships can
have short- and long-term negative effects, including (1) symptoms of depression and
anxiety; (2) higher engagement in using tobacco, drug, and alcohol use; (3) presenting
with antisocial behaviors (e.g., lying, theft, bullying); and (4) thoughts of suicide. Due to
the high rate of IPV/DV among teens, the CDC developed two programs: (1) Preventing
Intimate Partner Violence Across the Lifespan and (2) Dating Matters ®: Strategies to Promote
Healthy Teen Relationships targeted for 11- to 14-year-olds. Complete Activity 8.3, which
focuses on raising awareness about adolescent dating violence.

266 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
THE COUNSELOR’S RESPONSE TO INTIMATE PARTNER VIOLENCE
CLIENTS IN CRISIS
Learning Objective 8.6 Describe how to intervene with IPV/DV victims.
Crisis counselors have numerous opportunities to make a difference in the lives of
victims and survivors of IPV. These opportunities include accurate screening, use of
Q-tree assessments, effective responses to IPV disclosure, implementing effective harm
reduction and safety planning, intentional referrals to IPV shelters, and helping clients
navigate the emotional ramifications of IPV.
Screening for Intimate Partner Violence
Due to the prevalence of IPV/DV, it is generally agreed that IPV/DV screening should
be universal. Just as crisis counselors assess for suicidal and homicidal ideation with
every client, they should ask all clients directly about IPV/DV. Most research on IPV/
DV screening focuses on assessment in doctors’ offices and emergency departments,
since many victims require medical treatment for physical injury inflicted by an intimate
partner but may not necessarily seek counseling to address the effects of IPV/DV.
Nevertheless, the research that follows provides important information for counselors.
While acknowledging the need for intervention with IPV/DV victims, most health
care providers, crisis counselors, health care professionals, and clinicians fail to routinely
screen for IPV/DV (Clark et al., 2020). A primary barrier to universal IPV/DV screening
is the lack of knowledge of and training on how to ask about IPV/DV, how to recognize
symptoms that may indicate an individual is experiencing abuse, and how to respond
if IPV/DV is disclosed.
Personal variables may also prevent health care professionals and crisis counselors
from asking about IPV/DV. For example, clinicians may have negative attitudes toward
IPV/DV victims stemming from personal experiences with IPV/DV or have prejudicial
attitudes including racism, classism, ageism, and homophobia. Health care workers may also
avoid screening for IPV/DV due to fear for their own safety, fear of offending their patients,
or perhaps because they do not view intervention in domestic affairs to be part of their health
care responsibilities (Alotaby et al., 2013; Qasem et al., 2013; Todahl & Walters, 2011).
Institutional and professional barriers to IPV/DV screening are the perception of
powerlessness to help victims due to insufficient community resources and the fear of
marginalization by colleagues (Alotaby et al., 2013; Qasem et al., 2013; Todahl & Walters,
2011). However, the many reasons for not asking about IPV/DV do not outweigh the
Unfortunately, dating violence is a problem that affects

Chapter 8 • Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence 	267
argument for its screening. Screening can help prevent injury and literally help save the
lives of individuals who experience partner abuse.
The first step in screening for IPV/DV requires a cultural shift in the regular intake
assessment. The prevalence of IPV/DV incidents should make it a standard of care to
assess for IPV/DV. Screening should occur during wellness visits and during visits
for chronic disease management and emergency encounters. Following a chronic care
management model, assessments should occur during follow-up visits to assess for
important changes in risk and safety.
Creating a safe environment is an imperative part of the screening process.
Disclosing IPV/DV can be a very difficult and painful process for victims due to shame,
embarrassment, and fear of being judged. The crisis counselor’s interpersonal style can
also help create a safe atmosphere. Creating an environment of unconditional positive
regard and acceptance of the client’s choices will decrease feelings of being judged.
All persons should receive comprehensive assessment and/or appropriate referral
(Dutton et al., 2015).
There are two absolutes that crisis counselors should follow with victims of IPV/
DV. First, counselors should never ask about abuse in the presence of the client’s partner;
doing so may greatly increase the risk for harm to the client. Second, counselors should
be careful not to recommend ending the relationship as the only way to establish safety.
In fact, the risk for harm to an IPV/DV victim may increase following separation from an
abusive partner. Post-separation violence involves the batterer’s attempts to regain control
over the abused partner and may include physical assault, rape, stalking, harassment, and
even homicide (Bates, 2019; Spearman et al., 2022). Research on nonlethal post-separation
violence among heterosexual married couples indicates that the prevalence of IPV/DV
is many times greater among women who are separated or divorced from their abusive
husbands than among women who are still living with their husbands.
Once a safe environment and rapport are established, counselors should ask directly
about IPV/DV, gathering as many concrete and specific details as possible (see Question
Tree below). Counselors must be comfortable assessing for not only physical abuse but
also sexual abuse. Vocal tone and phrasing are especially important during IPV/DV
assessment, since counselors do not want to imply judgment or raise the client’s level
of defensiveness. It may be helpful to begin with more general questions, such as asking
clients whether they feel safe in the current relationship or whether anyone is hurting them.
As the assessment progresses, more specific information must be gathered. For example, a
counselor may ask who is hurting the client, how arguments usually begin, details of the
most recent incident of violence, how long the most recent incident lasted, what specific
forms of injuries occurred, and what happened when the incident was over. The client’s
IPV/DV history is important as well, including childhood exposure to IPV/DV, violence in
previous relationships, the first and worst incidents of violence in the current relationship,
past attempts at intervention by others (e.g., family, friends, neighbors, police, the legal
system), and the role of mental health and substance use issues in the IPV/DV.
Counselors should consider conducting a variety of assessments for lethality:
suicidal, homicidal (see Chapter 6), and level of threat as perceived by the victim. Often
victims have thoughts of suicide or homicide to avoid further abuse. In addition, a threat
assessment based on the client’s perception of injury should be completed to determine the
appropriate crisis response, which may range from developing a safety or harm reduction
plan with the victim to seeking immediate police intervention and hospitalization.

A lethality assessment includes questions about the severity of violence, other
criminal behaviors of the abuser (e.g., assaults or harassment of others, previous
criminal charges), failed past interventions (e.g., multiple calls made to 911; abuser
ignoring court orders; family, friends, and neighbors have tried to intervene, yet violence
continues), obsessive or stalking behaviors, psychological risk factors (e.g., previous
homicidal or suicidal threats or attempts, substance use issues, external life stressors,
severe depression), perceived threats to the relationship (e.g., victim planning to leave,
separation or divorce, infidelity), access to weapons, and behaviors that prevent the
victim from accessing emergency resources.
Remember that victims may initially deny that they are experiencing abuse. If
this occurs, make sure to revisit questions about IPV/DV again in the session or in a
subsequent session. The following intake provides an example of how to conduct an
initial IPV/DV screening.
Counselor: 	Hayden, we have talked a lot about your relationship with
your partner. I know the two of you have been through a lot
and your relationship is really strained right now. I need to ask
you if your partner has ever hurt you in any way.
Hayden: 	(30 seconds of silence while looking down) Not really.
Counselor: 	I know it can be really hard to talk about. Take your time and
know that I am not judging you in any way.
Hayden: 	Are you going to call the cops if I talk about this?
Counselor: 	As we have discussed before, there are a few situations that
would require me to break confidentiality to protect you or
someone else. I would need to act if I believed you were going
to kill yourself or someone else or in cases where I suspected
child abuse. Otherwise, what we talk about is between us. It
would be your decision to report to the police.
Hayden: 	(30 seconds of silence, crying) I feel so embarrassed to say it out
loud. (Crying for 30 seconds) Last weekend, we both had too
much to drink. We started arguing about the same things we
always fight about—the bills, past lovers, chores around the
house. A beer bottle was thrown at me and it hit me on the
head. I fell over and started crying. Aster, my partner, started
yelling, “Get up, bitch!” then came over and kicked me in the
stomach a few times and spat on me. Then Aster stormed out
and didn’t come back home for two nights. When Aster came
back, it was all flowers and crying and telling me it would
never happen again. I still can’t believe it happened. I never
thought Aster would hurt me.
Counselor: 	Hayden, it took a lot of courage to share that experience with
me. I can see how painful it is for you to talk about that night.
I need to ask you a few more questions, but let’s take it at a
pace you feel comfortable with. How are you feeling now?

Question Tree Screening Approach
As with other topics in this book, the Question Tree (Q-tree) screening
approach has been developed to offer a framework of questions that should
be addressed while also prioritizing other individual questions unique to
the person’s situation or ecological determinants (see Chapter 1). IPV/DV
is one of the areas that a Q-tree can assist, as many questions must be asked
during the assessments to develop a plan. As with all Q-trees, how a person
replies to a surface question will determine needed follow-up questions.
The following are some questions connected with IPV/DV:
• What type of violence have you experienced (physical, sexual,
emotional, stalking, other aggression or violence)? If yes, the following
may be asked:
✓ What is the type of violence, its severity, its frequency, and the date
of the last incident? What was your response? Have you ever
attempted to leave? What was the response of the abuser? (This
allows counselors to include safety mechanisms into a safety plan
if that is the direction needed by the client.)

• Have family, friends, coworkers, or other supporters (e.g., faith based) been

• Has the abuser demonstrated any stalking behaviors or other behaviors that have

• Have you noticed recent abnormal behaviors by the abuser?
• Who else lives in the home? If any others:
✓ Has there been any abuse of others in the home? Are there children or others
who are in your care? (This assists counselors in any mandated reporting.) Are
there any pets?
This is only a sample Q-tree and is not limited to these questions. The Q-tree approach
allows counselors to determine whether a safety plan or harm reduction plan should be
developed. It also allows counselors to determine whether a mandated report of abuse
must be made.
Response to Intimate Partner Violence Disclosure
Because nearly all counselors will work with an IPV/DV victim at some
point, they must be prepared to respond when a client reports being abused
by a partner. Immediately following an IPV/DV disclosure, it is critical for
the crisis counselor to validate the victim’s experience and communicate
that the victim is not to blame for the abuse (Martín-Fernández et al., 2018).
Documentation that the client is experiencing IPV/DV is also
important. Victims indicate that it is helpful for crisis counselors or health
care workers to make notes about the disclosure of IPV/DV and to take
pictures of physical injuries, which could later be used in court if the victim
chooses to take legal action. If some injuries are not visible, using a diagram
of a person to indicate where you visually see physical injuries and where
the victim reports injuries can also be part of documentation.

Chapter 8 • Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence 	271
Providing the victim with resources and protection is another essential component
of responding to an IPV/DV crisis (Martín-Fernández et al., 2018). Counselors should
have an extensive list of IPV/DV resources available, including options for emergency
shelter, transportation, food, child care, medical needs, mental health care, and legal
aid. The National Domestic Violence Hotline can be accessed 24 hours a day, 365 days
per year at 1-800-799-SAFE or at www.thehotline.org. Crisis counselors may also consider
providing a list of IPV/DV resources to all clients. Some agencies place small cards
printed with IPV/DV crisis resources in the restroom so that victims may anonymously
take the information and hide it if necessary.
While crisis intervention is typically more directive than traditional counseling,
counselors should strive to empower IPV/DV victims by giving them as much control
over their situations as possible (Todahl & Walters, 2011). Finally, victims indicate that it
is helpful to be informed that even if they initially choose not to access IPV/DV resources,
they can return to the agency and receive assistance in the future.
Safety Planning and Harm Reduction Planning
It is crucial for a counselor to know whether a client is deciding to stay in the abusive
relationship (i.e., will return home) or leave the abusive relationship prior to the client
leaving the crisis center or counselor. The counselor will want to implement a plan based
on the client’s decision, taking into consideration the unique issues of and resources
available to the client.
SAFETY PLANNING 	The National Center for Victims of Crime (2017) reports that 9% of
homicides are completed by intimate partners. Many relate to the decision to leave an
abusive relationship. Therefore, a comprehensive assessment and safety plan that meets
the needs of the individual client is imperative. If a client decided to leave the abuser, the
counselor should develop a safety plan with the main goal of keeping the client physically
safe, as well as any other vulnerable person living in the home (e.g., children, older adults).
The safety plan is tailored to the unique situation of each IPV/DV victim; therefore,
gathering concrete and specific information about the victim’s experiences is essential. The
counselor and client should determine appropriate supports for the client, locations that
provide physical safety (e.g., shelters), and whether law enforcement should be contacted.
It is also important to emphasize that a safety plan i

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 80/154)
content:
in no way guarantees the safety
of the IPV/DV victim. For example, civil protective orders are designed to prevent an
abusive partner from having future contact with an IPV/DV victim and can be tailored to
meet the victim’s specific needs. A protective order may contribute to an increased sense
of safety for many IPV/DV victims. However, approximately half of protective orders
are violated by IPV/DV perpetrators, particularly if there is a history of severe violence
or stalking in the relationship (Bates, 2019; Spearman et al., 2022). Additionally, some
IPV/DV victims may choose not to seek a protective order due to fear of retaliation, nega-
tive feelings toward the legal system, or limited financial resources (Lynch et al., 2022).
HARM REDUCTION PLANNING 	Harm reduction plans are developed for clients who do
not intend to leave the abusive relationship. The goal of the plan is to reduce the amount
of harm a victim may encounter by an abuser. Counselors should assess where incidents
of violence mainly occur to attempt to avoid those rooms, how to read signs of

-- 300 of 545 --

272 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
heightened anger, using code words for children to protect them from danger, using
code words to alert neighbors or family members of potential abuse, and arranging an
escape bag with necessary items (medications, keys, legal documents) in case the victim
must leave immediately. Since storing a bag in the home could increase violence,
collaborating with supporters or local religious centers can help keep the items safe
without the abuser’s knowledge. All of these things are accounted for in the harm
reduction plan. It is also important for clients to not demonstrate too many behavioral
changes lest an abuser become suspicious of their leaving.
Some harm reduction plans may also integrate strategies to assist in alleviating pain
from abuse. Meditation, mindfulness, praying, refocusing, and other self-care techniques
may need to be explored with the client as part of the harm reduction plan. Developing
a safety plan or a harm reduction plan may allow a client to gain a little control in
uncontrollable situations. Voices from the Field 8.1 provides some real-life insights into
harm reduction planning.
Victoria E. Kress
“My boyfriend hits me. . . . I’ve lost part of my
hearing. . . . I have two missing teeth, and I’ve had two
concussions. . . . I know I need to leave, but I’m not
sure I am ready. . . . He says he’ll take the baby from
me if I leave. . . . He’s threatened to kill me if I leave.”
When I was in graduate school, I never learned what
to say or do when counseling a person making these
remarks. The first time I worked with a woman in a
violent relationship, my first reaction was to tell her
to leave, to run to the nearest shelter and to be safe.
My client’s safety was a priority, of course, but I felt
a personal need to protect her as well. As counselors
we do have an obligation to encourage client safety,
but we also must balance this with clients’ right to
autonomy; their right to make their own decisions on
their own terms and schedule. So how do we respect a
client’s autonomy while also facilitating their welfare?
While it may be difficult, I recommend that
counselors working with clients in violent relationships
should not suggest that clients leave the relationship
but should instead encourage their clients to be as
safe as possible. People in abusive relationships have
typically been told by multiple people to leave, and
they themselves have often thought about leaving.
Counselors suggesting that clients leave a relationship
may indicate an inability to understand the complexity
of each client’s situation. Maybe more importantly,
suggesting that someone leave a relationship indicates
that the counselor does not believe that the client
can make autonomous choices. Finally, at the time a
woman leaves a violent relationship, her risk of being
murdered escalates dramatically. So, telling a woman
to leave a relationship before she is prepared to do so
can be dangerous.
Instead of focusing on the client leaving the
relationship, I suggest counselors develop a thorough
harm reduction plan as a first step. When I develop
harm reduction plans, I encourage my clients to do
specific things:
• Identify at least two places where you can go
the next time you need to leave the house or go
somewhere safe.
• Tell friends or neighbors about the violence and
request that they call the police if they get
suspicious.
• Identify the safest rooms in your house where
you can go if there is fear that an argument will
occur.
• Develop and store somewhere safe and
accessible an escape kit that includes a copy of a
protection order, extra keys, money, checks,
important phone numbers, medications, Social
Security card, bank documents, birth certificates,
change of clothes, etc.
• Process the safety plan with children when
appropriate.
• Practice escape routes and identify rooms that
are safe and not close to weapons.
VOICES FROM THE FIELD 8.1
Safety and Harm Reduction Planning with Intimate Partner Violence Victims

Chapter 8 • Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence 	273
Intimate Partner Violence Shelters: Opportunities and Challenges
IPV/DV shelters provide a safe haven for many abused women and their children. IPV/
DV shelters not only protect victims from additional physical harm but also provide a
safe place where emotional healing can begin. Shelter residents often deeply connect with
one another around their shared experiences. Shelter staff may provide much-needed
support and encouragement to victims. Many IPV/DV shelters also offer counseling
services and provide victims with comprehensive resources, including legal aid and
assistance finding long-term housing and employment.
While IPV/DV shelters are invaluable resources for many victims, life in a shelter is
certainly not stress-free and it requires adherence to programmatic rules and regulations
that may infringe on a victim’s sense of autonomy. In addition, victims may not feel
ready to address the emotional impact of their abuse yet may be required to do so as
part of the shelter program. Adjusting to a shared living environment may also present
difficulties, particularly for victims from cultural minority groups (Walker, 2015).
Finally, because of their traumatic experiences, some IPV/DV victims struggle with self-
regulation and engage in behaviors such as verbal or physical altercations with shelter
staff and residents or with substance use.
Working as a counselor in an IPV/DV shelter can also be stressful. For example,
counselors may experience secondary traumatization from exposure to victims’
experiences. Counselors must be sure to practice self-care to avoid burning out or taking a
blaming attitude toward victims. Counselors may also experience the stress of dual roles.
For example, a counselor may deeply empathize with a victim during an individual or
group session and then later be required to enforce shelter rules (e.g., imposing a penalty
for not completing a required chore, dismissing the victim from the shelter for substance
use). Solidify your knowledge of IPV/DV by completing Activity 8.4.
Compile a list of IPV/DV resources available in your
community. Consider the multitude of needs of
someone leaving an abusive partner (e.g., shelter,
food, transportation, clothing, child care, employment
assistance, legal aid, medical care) and barriers to
accessing resources (e.g., having no Internet access,
having to sneak out of the house to go to counseling,
having limited financial resources). Gather specific
information about how to access these community
resources (e.g., hours of operation, cost of services,
criteria for assistance). If possible, obtain written
materials or brochures from community organizations
that could be provided to IPV/DV victims. What was
your experience like when trying to gather information?
Were resources hard to identify? Were the agencies that
you contacted helpful? How might your experiences
identifying resources be like or different from those of
an IPV/DV victim in crisis?
ACTIVITY 8.4
Addressing the Emotional Impact of Intimate Partner Violence
The emotional consequences of IPV/DV can be as devastating as the physical
consequences. High rates of depression and low self-esteem are common among victims,
and IPV/DV is a major predictor of drug and alcohol use in women (Langdon et al.,
2014). Many IPV/DV victims experience symptoms of PTSD, including a reexperiencing
of the trauma (e.g., intrusive recollections, nightmares, flashbacks), avoidance/numbing
(e.g., restricted range of affect, anhedonia, social withdrawal, inability to recall aspects

-- 302 of 545 --

274 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
of the trauma), and increased arousal (e.g., hypervigilance, exaggerated startle response,
difficulty falling or staying asleep). In addition to the symptoms traditionally associated
with PTSD, Walker (2015) argues that IPV/DV victims often experience a cluster of
symptoms referred to as battered woman syndrome, which includes disrupted interpersonal
relationships, difficulties with body image/somatic concerns, and sexual or intimacy
problems.
When working with IPV/DV victims, it is essential to consider their trauma history
in constructing a treatment plan or making a diagnosis. Unfortunately, many victims are
misdiagnosed and prescribed inappropriate medications by mental health care providers
who fail to account for the effects of IPV/DV. Medicating a client inappropriately does
not address the issue of IPV/DV and can only create another potential problem for the
client. Victims may present with depression and anxiety without knowledge of the root
of the symptoms. Most victims will not disclose information without prompting, which is
why counselors should assess for IPV/DV with every client, regardless of the presenting
issue. Victims may be empowered by learning that the symptoms they are experiencing
are a normal response to a traumatic event (McLeod et al., 2010). Case Study 8.2 describes
Melinda’s experiences as an IPV/DV victim. Then complete Activity 8.5 to address
counselor self-care issues in work with IPV/DV.
CASE STUDY 8.2
Counseling a Victim of Intimate Partner Violence: Melinda’s Journey
Melinda, a 25-year-old, middle-class, Black female, first came to counseling for depression.
During the initial intake interview, she reported having trouble sleeping, frequent crying
spells, low self-esteem, and feelings of hopelessness. Melinda’s counselor, Candace,
screened for suicidal ideation, homicidal ideation, and IPV/DV as a routine part of the
initial interview. Melinda denied suicidal and homicidal ideation and said that she had
never been abused by her current boyfriend. At the end of the initial session, Candace
provided Melinda with a packet of materials that she distributes to all new clients, which
included a pamphlet on IPV/DV.
Over the next two sessions, Melinda and Candace developed a strong rapport.
During her third session, Melinda tearfully disclosed that her live-in boyfriend sometimes
pushes or hits her when he has been drinking. Candace listened empathically to Melinda
and let her know that the abuse was not her fault and that she believed her. After
Melinda’s disclosure, Candace asked for more information about the abuse, including
how arguments usually begin, details of the most recent incident of violence, and her
history of experience with IPV/DV. Melinda reported that the last time her boyfriend hit
her was a week ago, when he came home drunk from a party. She sustained a split lip
but did not require stitches.
Candace also completed a lethality assessment and determined that Melinda’s
boyfriend had never threatened to kill her, did not have a criminal record, and did not
have a gun in the house. Melinda stated that she had never feared for her life when
her boyfriend became abusive. She indicated that she did not want to break up with
her boyfriend and that she did not think the abuse was serious enough to report to
the police.

Candace respected Melinda’s autonomy and provided her with additional edu-
cational materials about IPV/DV and information on 24-hour IPV/DV crisis resources
that she could use in case of an emergency. Since Melinda reported that her boyfriend
typically drank every weekend, Candace scheduled another appointment with her
before the upcoming weekend to develop a safety plan. Melinda and Candace col-
laboratively developed the following safety plan to minimize the risk and impact of
IPV/DV:
1. Plan to be away from the house when Melinda suspects her boyfriend may come
home drunk.
2. If her boyfriend comes home drunk unexpectedly, stay away from the bathroom
and kitchen.
3. Pack and hide an emergency bag with clothes, cash, and an extra set of keys in case
of the need to flee the house quickly.
4. Program IPV/DV crisis hotline number into cell phone.
Melinda and Candace also discussed ideas for building coping resources, includ-
ing engaging in self-care activities like reading empowerment books and joining
a women’s support group at church. Over the next several months, Candace and
Melinda met once a week for counseling. They continued to discuss and evaluate her
safety. Despite her efforts to avoid physical violence from her boyfriend, the incidents
of abuse became more frequent over time. Melinda had now sustained several inju-
ries, including a broken arm and a broken rib. A turning point occurred when she
learned that she was 8 weeks pregnant with her first child. Melinda decided that she
did not want to raise a child in an abusive home. She was ready to leave. Candace and
Melinda discussed the risks associated with leaving an abusive partner and tips for
ensuring a safe escape. Together, they decided that an IPV/DV shelter was the best
option, since Melinda had no trusted family or friends in the area. Candace arranged
for transportation to the shelter straight from her office and waited with Melinda until
help arrived.
Discussion Questions

2. What are some short-term and long-term therapeutic considerations?
3. What are some case management issues that must be addressed?
4. What referrals need to be made for Melinda?
Working with victims of IPV/DV can vicariously
traumatize counselors; therefore, self-care is essential.
Self-care is a preventive process of proactively
enhancing health by building resilience against illness
and disease. It involves identifying and practicing
ways to promote personal well-being. Start practicing
self-care now. Make a list of activities that you find
personally renewing. Develop a plan to incorporate at
least one activity into each day of the upcoming week.
Keep a brief journal about your self-care experiences.

COVID-19 PANDEMIC IMPACTS ON IPV/DV
Learning Objective 8.7 Describe how COVID-19 has impacted persons in IPV/DV
relationships.
The increased isolation due to COVID-19 has impacted individuals in IPV/DV situations
worldwide to the extent that the WHO (2021a) issued statements regarding the need for
a global awareness of social isolation on IPV/DV victims. Even though their focus is on
women and children, it is imperative to extend this awareness to all who are isolated
without supports in abusive environments. The decreased ability to seek leave from envi-
ronments of abuse has been assumed to increase situations of DV. Pre-pandemic supports
such as visiting with family and friends and workplace and educational environments
that often provided some form of distancing from an abuser were cut off during most
of the pandemic, meaning diminished reprieves from the abuser. Add in the stressors
from the pandemic, such as economic changes, and abuse is believed to have increased.
Those seeking supportive services struggled to maintain them during the pan-
demic. Even using telehealth for counseling services may have decreased safety when
abusers learned of the counseling. Code words and scans of the room may have allowed
for assurance of privacy, but the conversation may still have been overheard within
the household. At the same time, those persons involved in IPV but not living with the
abuser may have seen a decrease in violent relationships due to social isolation.
The services that were available to individuals in IPV/DV relationships were
impacted by the pandemic. Services may have been scaled back, personnel cut or limited,
and shelters may have closed or limited access for safety. In other situations, lack of
economic means may have limited or not allowed individuals access to the Internet to
participate in telehealth.
The WHO (2021a) distributed psychoeducation about the pandemic and increased
IPV/DV incidents, suggesting:
• Reducing exposure to news outlets to 1–2 hours per day
• Staying in contact with family and friends by e-mail and text
• Maintaining daily routines including physical activities and sleep
• Integrating meditation and grounding exercises to relieve stressful thoughts
• Engaging in activities that help reduce stress
• Developing a safety plan with family in case violence increases
• Keeping important documents, money, and personal items accessible in case you
need to leave immediately and planning ahead on how you might leave
• Having numbers on hand for hotlines, shelters, social workers, child protective
services, and the nearest police station
• Being discreet and not keeping any of this information where the abuser may find it

BATTERER INTERVENTION
Learning Objective 8.8 Explain effective IPV/DV perpetrator identification and
treatment.
IPV/DV response has two aspects: victim intervention and batterer intervention. Both
are valid attempts to end IPV/DV. One model of attending to the needs of both IPV/DV
victims and batterers is a coordinated community response (CCR), which involves the
collaboration of local councils of service providers in developing a more comprehensive
response to IPV/DV incidents. CCR helps victims identify and access multiple available
services, of which victims are often not aware. These services focus on advocacy, criminal
justice, child services, health care, education, and vocation.
Without a network of support, IPV/DV victims face the frustration and fatigue
of searching for and navigating through multiple systems and services, thus reducing
the likelihood that victims will seek help at all (Shorey et al., 2014). In addition to
the efforts of community organizations, over half of the states have laws that make
specific reference to batterer intervention programs, and many designate specific
entities to certify, license, or establish standards for batterer intervention programs
(CDC, 2021c).
Batterer intervention programs have three primary goals: (1) ensuring the safety
of IPV/DV victims, (2) stopping future acts of partner violence, and (3) increasing
offenders’ accountability for their behavior. Batterer intervention programs teach
abusive partners to develop empathy for their victims, identify and self-monitor negative
thoughts, and identify and interrupt the negative self-talk that often precedes incidents
of violence to prevent further abuse. Additionally, batterer intervention programs teach
gender equality in relationships and typically teach participants to identify various
forms of abuse, recognize and detail the type of abuse they have engaged in, take
responsibility for the abuse, and find alternatives to violence, often helping participants
learn to develop a nonabusive response to behavior that they might ordinarily respond
to in an abusive manner. Although most batterer intervention programs are designed
for long-term intervention, counselors should understand the components of these
programs and know whether they are available in their community. Understanding the
minimizing behaviors perpetrators use can also be a helpful psychoeducation activity
for victims.
Safety
The primary goal of batterer intervention programs is to ensure the safety of the abused
partner and any children who may be involved. Steps to ensuring safety include
assessment of the resources that are available to IPV/DV victims, assessment of the
severity and repetition of violent incidents, and assessment of the effects on children
exposed to IPV/DV. Depending on the aforementioned factors, offenders may need to
reside separately from the victims. If law enforcement is at the scene and an arrest occurs,
incarceration of the offender may provide temporary safety.
Cessation of Violence
The second goal of batterer intervention programs is stopping all forms of violence,
including verbal, emotional, physical, and sexual abuses. One of the first steps toward

-- 306 of 545 --

278 	Part II • Special Issues in Crisis Intervention
this goal is acknowledgment of one’s violent behavior. Such an acknowledgment means
discarding denial (e.g., “I didn’t do it”), minimizing (e.g., “I only hit her”), and blaming
(e.g., “She should have been home on time”). It also means accepting that threats,
coercion, and emotional abuses—the types of violence where there is no physical
contact—qualify as IPV/DV.
Accountability
The third goal of batterer intervention is increasing individual accountability for acts
of IPV/DV in the form of accepting responsibility for one’s behavior. Simply saying
“I’m sorry,” especially if those words have been said on multiple occasions, is not
enough. Developing alternatives to battering is an essential component of increasing
accountability. One alternative strategy involves understanding and subsequently
changing negative self-talk to positive self-talk. Negative self-talk involves destructive
comments made when challenged with an uncomfortable or threatening situation. For
example, a batterer might say, “I’m so mad that my partner doesn’t have dinner ready.”
The idea is to change this statement to something more constructive, such as “Since
dinner isn’t ready yet, maybe I can help.”
A second task in becoming accountable is to learn to recognize triggers or cues
leading to violent behavior. Triggers can be words, phrases, or statements that either
the individual or partner uses (e.g., profanity, degrading comments); situations that
typically create arguments (e.g., payday, drinking/drug use); and physiological changes
(e.g., muscle tension, headaches, increased blood pressure/pulse). The identification of
triggers enables IPV/DV offenders to be responsible for initiating behaviors that will
decrease their level of anxiety, frustration, and anger and thereby prevent any further
acts of IPV/DV.
Counselors who work with IPV/DV perpetrators encounter numerous challenges,
including the underreporting of IPV/DV incidents, the need to distinguish batterer
intervention programs from anger management programs, limited financial resources,
and low completion rates. Other issues include IPV/DV and substance use, the duty to
protect and the duty to warn, personal safety, and the counselor’s comfort working with
batterers. Working with victims of IPV/DV is only part of decreasing it. The counseling
profession needs counselors who are willing to work with batterers to assist in ending
generations of abuse.
Challenges to Referrals to Batterer Intervention Programs
One of the biggest challenge

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 82/154)
content:
s regarding batterer intervention is that most program
participants are court mandated. For a judge or probation officer to refer an IPV/
DV offender to treatment, someone must first report an IPV/DV incident. The
reasons so many cases go unreported are complex and varied: fear of the unknown
and of the batterer, existence of children, promises of reform, guilt, lack of self-
esteem, love, gender-role beliefs, economic dependence, religious beliefs, stigma of
a broken home, or denial of a problem. Although many of these reasons are valid, if
IPV/DV incidents go unreported, batterer intervention is nearly impossible. Read
Voices from the Field 8.2 to hear from a former director of a batterer intervention
program.

-- 307 of 545 --

Chapter 8 • Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence 	279
Financial Resources
The financial aspect of batterer intervention is also a challenge. A significant number
of batterer programs charge fees for services to sustain the program’s existence. Most
federal and state monies for IPV/DV go to battered women’s programs—and rightly so.
However, if we do not intervene with batterers and re-educate them, IPV/DV will likely
never end. Therefore, batterer program staff members must solicit referrals from judges
and probation officers. Unfortunately, potential referral sources often refuse to make
appropriate referrals to batterer intervention programs due to their cost.
Completion Rates
Treatment refusal, overt or passive resistance, and premature termination (i.e., dropout)
are common issues confronted by counselors in the typical batterer intervention
program. Numerous authors have reported treatment noncompliance and client
attrition rates ranging between 40 and 70% (Ferrer-Perez & Bosch-Fiol, 2018; Hamel
et al., 2015). Recidivism rates for batterers were substantially lower for those who
completed intervention programs when compared with those who stopped attendance
prematurely. In addition, re-arrest rates were substantially lower for batterers who
completed treatment than are generally found in the literature on batterer recidivism.
These findings offer optimism for counselors willing to address the batterer’s resistance
as a major component of treatment planning.
Assessing Perpetrators of Intimate Partner Violence
Assessment of batterers is important when considering the holistic impact upon victims,
victim safety, and changing abusive behaviors. A plethora of surveys exist to assess the
severity of batterer behavior. Most assessment tools ask questions of victims rather than
perpetrators, although both parties can be asked the same set of questions. The more
comprehensive assessment tools ask questions that seek to know more about the abusive
John Muldoon
I was the director of a batterer intervention program
for almost 7 years. While my body sometimes cringed
at statements men would make, I also saw the trauma
that some male offenders experienced because of
growing up with inappropriate or absent male role
models and the interconnection of substance use and
IPV/DV. My experiences raised my awareness about the
lack of equality inherent in batterer intervention. Many
of the batterers who chose to attend our program to
avoid incarceration were either unemployed or working
in blue-collar jobs. IPV/DV offenders with higher income
levels were often able to hire an attorney to dispute
the criminal charges at the time of the incident. I also
saw the lack of consistency from one jurisdiction to
another and even from one judge or probation officer
to another in terms of willingness to understand the
general mission of batterer programs and to collaborate
with our program. To this end, I know there are pockets
of places that are committed to take action to end IPV/
DV. Overall, there is work that still needs to happen
to educate people about IPV/DV. It continues to be a
social crisis that is greatly unknown and misunderstood,
except to those who experience it.
VOICES FROM THE FIELD 8.2

relationship and invite discussion of how victimization patterns and relationships
changed over time.
Typically, an assessment of IPV/DV severity includes a review of the following risk
factors: (1) access to/ownership of guns, (2) use of weapon in prior abusive incidents,
(3) threats with weapons, (4) serious injury in prior abusive incidents, (5) threats of
suicide, (6) drug or alcohol abuse, (7) forced sex of female partner, (8) obsessiveness/
extreme jealousy/extreme dominance, and (9) previous victimization or attempts of any
violence (National Resource Center on Domestic Violence, 2011).
Based on a review of data on domestic homicide, researchers found that the
following factors existed before the killing of an intimate partner: (1) a prior history of DV
on the part of the perpetrator; (2) an estrangement, separation, or an attempt at separation
by the victim; (3) prior involvement with police by the perpetrator, accompanied by
a failure to be deterred by police intervention or other criminal justice initiatives;
(4) threats by the perpetrator to kill the victim, with details communicated to the victim,
family members, friends, colleagues at work, or others; and (5) a restraining order or an
order of protection by the victim against the perpetrator (National Resource Center on
Domestic Violence, 2011).
Another significant but often overlooked factor that should be considered when
assessing the severity of IPV/DV is stalking. Morgan and Truman (2022) define stalking
as a course of conduct directed at a specific person that would cause a reasonable person
to feel fear. They reported that 3.4 million people in the United States (1.3% of all people
16 years and older) were victims of stalking in 2019, 67% of stalking victims knew the
stalker, 16% of stalking victims sought victim services, and 74% of those received services.
When considering co-occurring mental health and substance abuse issues, Thomas
et al. (2012) found that batterers who abused substances were more violent and were
more likely to perpetrate violence that is severe in nature, reported higher levels of
anger and trauma, and had a greater proclivity toward a borderline personality disorder.
Screening batterers for alcohol and other drug (AOD) problems and substance use is
highly recommended. Dual screening serves multiple purposes, including being more
vigilant about victim safety and focusing on anger and trauma issues when working
with AOD batterers.
Personal Safety
The presumption is that batterers will exert violence only toward their intimate partner.
However, counselors should be aware of how they interact with perpetrators and be
cognizant of personal safety. Accusations and criticism should be kept at a minimum
while balancing skills of challenging and confrontation to assist in empathy building,
awareness of violence, and behavioral change. Perpetrators should be held accountable,
but they should also be allowed to work in an environment where they can safely explore
their behaviors and not feel continuously criticized.
Counselors should also be able to identify when clients’ emotions become heightened
to assist in calming the situation or, if that is not possible, to seek additional means of
intervention. Assess the best way to maintain personal safety, get out of the violent path,
and then follow up for help, which may involve contacting law enforcement. Chapter 2
presents an expanded discussion of safety precautions and Chapter 14 presents an
expanded discussion of counselor self-care strategies during a crisis. Now complete Think
About It 8.2 and read Case Study 8.3. For additional resources on IPV/DV, see Table 8.1.

SUMMARY
IPV/DV is an all-too-common crisis. As a result,
counselors must be prepared to respond to the
needs of its victims. Understanding the cycle of
violence is a critical first step for crisis counselors.
The cycle of violence consists of the tension-
building phase, the acute battering incident, and
the honeymoon phase, which is characterized
by contrite, loving behav

title: textbooks CRISIS~1 (Part 83/154)
content:
ior. It is also important
for counselors to examine their beliefs about
why IPV/DV victims stay in relationships
with abusive partners. In this chapter, various
explanations for staying with an abusive partner
were offered, including learned helplessness
theory and the ecological theory of IPV/DV.
Common crisis issues for IPV/DV victims
include attending to physical injury, establishing
immediate safety, and deciding whether to report
the IPV/DV to the police. When addressing IPV/
DV crises, counselors should strive to empower
victims to problem-solve effectively and respect
victims’ decisions to stay in or leave an abusive
relationship. When addressing IPV/DV in racial-
ethnic minority and LGBTQ+ communities,
counselors must be aware of the compounded
effects of IPV/DV on these populations due
to oppression stemming from factors such as
racism, classism, transphobia, and homophobia.
In addition, issues specific to female-to-male
violence, dating violence, and violence against a
partner with a disability must also be considered.
This chapter discussed barriers to IPV/DV
screening and endorsed universal screening of
IPV/DV in every crisis, regardless of whether the
presenting issue is IPV/DV. Screening includes
gathering concrete and specific details about
the client’s IPV/DV history and conducting a
lethality assessment to determine the degree
of urgency required in responding to the crisis.

-- 311 of 545 --

Chapter 8 • Intimate Partner Violence and Domestic Violence 	283
When IPV/DV is disclosed, the counselor should
validate the victim’s experience, document
that it is occurring, and provide resources and
continued support to the victim.
A specific component of IPV/DV response
is safety planning or harm reduction planning,
which is based on the victim’s decision to stay
in or leave the abusive relationship. When
responding to IPV/DV, the counselor should
consider the emotional consequences of abuse
and be careful not to pathologize a normal
response to the trauma of IPV/DV. Counselors
who work with IPV/DV victims must be mindful
of self-care to avoid vicarious traumatization
and burnout.
The chapter concluded with a discussion
of the various aspects of batterer intervention.
The goals of batterer intervention programs
include ensuring the safety of IPV/DV victims,
stopping future violence, and increasing the
batterers’ accountability for their behavior.
Batterer intervention programs are based on
power and control theory, moral development
theory, attachment theory, and feminist-
informed 	cognitive-behavioral 	theories.
Common challenges encountered in batterer
intervention include the underreporting of
IPV/DV, confusion surrounding the differences
in anger management problems and battering
behavior, and limited financial resources.
